topics.title: AI Techniques for Security: Anomaly Detection Techniques and Algorithms, Statistical Methods for Anomaly Detection, Machine Learning Models (Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, etc.)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT633 - AI FOR CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AI Techniques for Security: Anomaly Detection Techniques and Algorithms, Statistical Methods for Anomaly Detection, Machine Learning Models (Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Anomaly Detection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anomaly Detection (Outlier Detection)** in cyber security is the identification of rare items, events, or observations which raise suspicions by differing significantly from the majority of the normal operational data. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus (PECAT633), anomaly detection is a **supervised, semi-supervised, or unsupervised learning paradigm** that models the distribution of legitimate behaviour and flags deviations as potential **intrusions, fraud, malware, or policy violations**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, anomaly detection sits at the intersection of **statistical inference** and **machine learning**, forming the algorithmic backbone of modern **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)**, **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** platforms, and **User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA)**.

### 1.2 Taxonomy of Anomalies in Security

| Anomaly Type | Definition | Security Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Anomaly** | A single data instance far from the rest | A single 50 GB data upload by a regular employee |
| **Contextual Anomaly** | Anomaly w.r.t. a specific context (time, location) | Login at 3 AM from a foreign IP for a 9-to-5 user |
| **Collective Anomaly** | A collection of related data points is anomalous | Sequential failed SSH logins forming a brute-force pattern |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Security Guard at a Malls Parking Lot" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a security guard at a residential apartment's parking lot who has memorised the **average behaviour of regular residents** — they arrive between 6 PM and 10 PM in a small car, park in their assigned slot, and leave by 8 AM. One night, a black van with tinted windows reverses into a basement slot at 2 AM. The guard does not need to *know* it is a thief; he only needs to recognise that **the pattern of behaviour is statistically improbable** compared to the history of normal activity. This is exactly what an anomaly detection algorithm does — it builds a **profile of normalcy** from historical data and raises an alarm when a new observation falls **outside the learned envelope of normality**.

The guard never saw a "thief" before (no labelled attack data required) — this is the **unsupervised** power of anomaly detection, making it invaluable in cyber security where novel (zero-day) attacks constantly emerge.

---

## 1.4 Why Anomaly Detection is Indispensable in Cyber Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO2):** Anomaly detection addresses the **"unknown unknowns"** problem in cyber defence. Signature-based systems (anti-virus, Snort rules) can only catch **known** threats. Anomaly detection can theoretically flag **never-before-seen** attack vectors such as zero-day exploits, advanced persistent threats (APTs), and insider threats.

**Key Security Domains where Anomaly Detection is Deployed:**

1. **Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS)** — detecting port scans, DDoS traffic, C2 beaconing.
2. **Host-based IDS (HIDS)** — flagging unusual system calls, process executions, registry modifications.
3. **Fraud Detection** — anomalous credit card transactions, insurance claims.
4. **Insider Threat Detection** — employees accessing data outside their role-perimeter.
5. **Malware Detection** — unusual API call sequences, network traffic patterns.
6. **Authentication Anomalies** — impossible travel, credential stuffing.

---

## 1.5 Core Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Definitions)

The following metrics are **bolded** as they are the universally accepted evaluation standard in KTU 2024 Scheme question papers:

- **True Positive (TP):** Anomalous instance correctly flagged as anomaly.
- **True Negative (TN):** Normal instance correctly classified as normal.
- **False Positive (FP):** Normal instance falsely flagged as anomaly (Type I Error — *the most expensive mistake in security operations*).
- **False Negative (FN):** Anomalous instance missed (Type II Error — *the most dangerous mistake*).

**Derived Formulas (used throughout the KTU 2024 question bank):**

$$\text{Detection Rate (Recall)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$\text{False Alarm Rate (FPR)} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

$$F1\text{-Score} = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of an Anomaly Detection System

The operational pipeline of any AI-driven anomaly detector follows a **four-stage architecture**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — NetFlow logs, Sysmon events, authentication logs, packet captures (PCAP).
2. **Feature Engineering Layer** — conversion of raw events into numerical vectors (packet size, inter-arrival time, entropy of destination IPs).
3. **Modelling Layer** — application of statistical or ML algorithms to learn the distribution of normality.
4. **Decision Layer** — thresholding the anomaly score and triggering alerts/SOAR playbooks.

> [!NOTE]
> The **quality of features** dominates the **sophistication of the algorithm** in anomaly detection. A simple Z-score on well-engineered features often beats a deep neural network on raw, noisy logs.

---

## 2.2 Statistical Methods for Anomaly Detection — Deep Dive

Statistical methods form the **first-generation AI techniques** for security and remain in production use today due to their **interpretability and low computational cost**.

### 2.2.1 Parametric Methods (Assume a Distribution)

**A. Z-Score (Standard Score) Method**

The Z-score measures how many standard deviations an observation lies from the mean. It assumes the underlying feature follows (or approximates) a **Gaussian (Normal) distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

$$Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$

- If $\vert Z_i \vert > 3$ (the **3-sigma rule**), the point is flagged as an anomaly. This corresponds to a probability of $0.27\%$ under the Gaussian assumption.
- **Cyber Use Case:** Detecting anomalous packet sizes, login frequencies, or CPU utilisation spikes.

**B. Grubbs' Test (Extreme Studentized Deviate — ESD)**

Used when the anomaly is suspected to be a **single outlier in a univariate dataset**. The test statistic is:

$$G = \frac{\max_i \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{s}$$

where $\bar{x}$ is the sample mean and $s$ is the sample standard deviation. The critical value $G_{crit}$ is:

$$G_{crit} = \frac{(n-1)}{\sqrt{n}} \sqrt{\frac{t_{\alpha/(2n), \, n-2}^2}{n - 2 + t_{\alpha/(2n), \, n-2}^2}}$$

If $G > G_{crit}$, the most extreme point is an outlier. $t_{\alpha/(2n), \, n-2}$ is the t-distribution critical value.

**C. Mahalanobis Distance (Multivariate Generalisation of Z-Score)**

When features are **correlated**, Euclidean distance fails. The Mahalanobis distance accounts for the **covariance structure** of the data:

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \, \Sigma^{-1} \, (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$$

where $\Sigma$ is the covariance matrix of the feature space. Points with $D_M^2 > \chi^2_{d, \, 0.975}$ (where $d$ is the number of features) are flagged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Mahalanobis distance is the **gold standard for multivariate statistical anomaly detection** in K-Network datasets like NSL-KDD and UNSW-NB15.

### 2.2.2 Non-Parametric Methods (No Distribution Assumption)

**A. Histogram-Based Method**

A histogram of normal traffic is built; bins with **frequency below a threshold** are flagged as anomalous regions. Simple but suffers from the **curse of dimensionality**.

**B. Kernel Density Estimation (KDE)**

Estimates the underlying probability density function (PDF) without assuming its shape:

$$\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} K\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$

where $K(\cdot)$ is a kernel function (commonly Gaussian), $h$ is the **bandwidth** (smoothing parameter), and $d$ is the dimensionality. Anomalies are points with $\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) < \tau$.

---

## 2.3 Machine Learning Models for Anomaly Detection

### 2.3.1 Isolation Forest (iForest)

**Core Philosophy (Liu, Ting & Zhou, 2008):** *Anomalies are few and different.* Therefore, they are **easier to isolate** than normal points. iForest builds an ensemble of **Isolation Trees (iTrees)** by randomly selecting features and split values.

- **Path Length** $h(\mathbf{x})$: number of edges traversed from root to terminal node when isolating $\mathbf{x}$.
- **Anomaly Score:**

$$s(\mathbf{x}, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(\mathbf{x}))}{c(n)}}$$

where $E(h(\mathbf{x}))$ is the average path length over all iTrees, and $c(n)$ is the average path length of an unsuccessful search in a Binary Search Tree (BST):

$$c(n) = 2H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$$

with $H(i) \approx \ln(i) + \gamma$ (Euler-Mascheroni constant $\gamma \approx 0.5772$).

**Score Interpretation:**
- $s \approx 1 \Rightarrow$ anomaly
- $s < 0.5 \Rightarrow$ normal
- $s \approx 0.5 \Rightarrow$ no clear distinction

### 2.3.2 One-Class Support Vector Machine (OC-SVM)

**Core Philosophy (Schölkopf et al., 2001):** Learn a **hyperplane that maximally separates the origin from the data** in a high-dimensional feature space induced by a kernel.

**Primal optimisation problem:**

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, \rho, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + \frac{1}{\nu n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i - \rho$$

subject to:

$$(\mathbf{w} \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i)) \geq \rho - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

**Decision function:**

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sgn}\!\left(\mathbf{w} \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}) - \rho\right)$$

$\nu \in (0, 1)$ is the **upper bound on the fraction of training errors** and a lower bound on the fraction of support vectors. $\Phi(\mathbf{x})$ maps $\mathbf{x}$ to a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) — typically a Gaussian/RBF kernel.

**Cyber Use Case:** Detecting novel network attacks where only "normal" traffic is available for training (the typical scenario in zero-day defence).

### 2.3.3 Other Prominent ML Models (Exam-Relevant Summary)

| Model | Type | Key Idea | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local Outlier Factor (LOF)** | Density | Compares local density of a point to its $k$ neighbours | Clustered anomalies, contextual outliers |
| **DBSCAN** | Clustering | Points in low-density regions are noise (outliers) | Spatial anomalies in network logs |
| **Autoencoders** | Neural Net | Reconstruction error is the anomaly score | High-dimensional cyber telemetry |
| **LSTM-AE** | Deep Learning | Sequential reconstruction error | Time-series anomaly detection |
| **GAN-based** | Deep Learning | Discriminator score as anomaly measure | Sophisticated, adversarial-aware IDS |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the definitive "cheat sheet" for KTU 2024 ESE preparation on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Z-Score** | $Z_i = (x_i - \mu)/\sigma$ | $\vert Z_i \vert > 3$ → anomaly |
| 2 | **Mahalanobis Distance** | $D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | $D_M^2 > \chi^2_{d, 0.975}$ |
| 3 | **Grubbs' Test Statistic** | $G = \max_i \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert / s$ | $G > G_{crit}$ |
| 4 | **KDE Estimator** | $\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n h^d} \sum K((\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i)/h)$ | $\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) < \tau$ |
| 5 | **Isolation Forest Score** | $s = 2^{-E(h(\mathbf{x}))/c(n)}$ | $s \to 1$ anomaly, $s < 0.5$ normal |
| 6 | **BST Avg. Path Length** | $c(n) = 2H(n-1) - 2(n-1)/n$ | Used in iForest normalisation |
| 7 | **OC-SVM Objective** | $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + \frac{1}{\nu n} \sum \xi_i - \rho$ | Subject to separation constraints |
| 8 | **OC-SVM Decision** | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sgn}(\mathbf{w} \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}) - \rho)$ | Output $+1$ normal, $-1$ anomaly |
| 9 | **LOF Score** | $\text{LOF}_k(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{o \in N_k(\mathbf{x})} \frac{\text{lrd}(o)}{\text{lrd}(\mathbf{x})}$ | $\text{LOF} > 1.5$ → outlier |
| 10 | **Reconstruction Error (AE)** | $\mathcal{L} = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2$ | High loss → anomaly |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC Operations:** Splunk, Elastic, and Microsoft Sentinel use **statistical thresholding + ML ensembles** for real-time threat detection.
- **Cloud Security:** AWS GuardDuty uses **anomaly detection on VPC flow logs** to identify crypto-mining and credential compromise.
- **Fraud:** PayPal deploys **iForest + LSTM ensembles** for transaction fraud scoring.
- **Endpoint Security:** CrowdStrike Falcon uses **behavioural ML models** trained on millions of endpoints for zero-day malware detection.
- **IoT/OT Security:** Isolation Forest is the **de-facto standard** for resource-constrained IoT intrusion detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Z-Score Threshold Justification under Gaussian Assumption

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Prove mathematically why $\vert Z \vert > 3$ corresponds to an anomaly under a Normal distribution.

**Step 1 — Start with the standard normal PDF:**

$$f(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2 / 2}$$

**Step 2 — State the probability that $\vert Z \vert > 3$:**

$$P(\vert Z \vert > 3) = 2 \int_{3}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2 / 2} \, dz$$

**Step 3 — Use the complementary error function (erfc) form:**

$$P(\vert Z \vert > 3) = \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx 0.0027$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\vert Z \vert > 3) &\approx 0.27\% \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Only 27 out of 10,000 observations exceed this threshold under normality.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** A point exceeding $\pm 3\sigma$ is statistically rare, justifying its flagging as an anomaly. This **68-95-99.7 rule** (Empirical Rule) is foundational to the KTU 2024 statistical syllabus.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Mahalanobis Distance as a Whitened Euclidean Norm

**Step 1 — Begin with the multivariate Gaussian PDF:**

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^d \vert \Sigma \vert}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the quadratic form (the "exponent"):**

$$Q = (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

**Step 3 — Eigendecompose the covariance matrix** $\Sigma = U \Lambda U^T$, where $U$ is orthogonal and $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_d)$:

$$Q = (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T U \Lambda^{-1} U^T (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \frac{(u_i^T (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}))^2}{\lambda_i}$$

**Step 4 — Define the whitened variable** $\mathbf{y} = \Lambda^{-1/2} U^T (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$. Then $Q = \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} = \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert^2$.

**Step 5 — The Mahalanobis distance is therefore:**

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{Q} = \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$$

**Step 6 — Under the Gaussian assumption, $D_M^2 \sim \chi^2_d$ (chi-squared with $d$ degrees of freedom).** Hence the decision rule becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_M^2(\mathbf{x}) &> \chi^2_{d, \, 1-\alpha} \\
\Rightarrow (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) &> \chi^2_{d, \, 1-\alpha}
\end{aligned}$$

For $d = 2$ and $\alpha = 0.025$, $\chi^2_{2, 0.975} \approx 7.378$. For $d = 10$, it is approximately **20.48**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in cyber security:** The Mahalanobis distance naturally handles **correlated features** (e.g., packet size and inter-arrival time are strongly correlated in HTTP traffic). A plain Euclidean distance would mis-flag normal correlated points as anomalies.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Isolation Forest Anomaly Score

**Step 1 — Define a single Isolation Tree (iTree) construction:**

An iTree is built by recursively:
1. Selecting a feature $q$ at random.
2. Selecting a split value $p$ uniformly between $\min(q)$ and $\max(q)$.
3. Splitting the data into left ($x_q < p$) and right ($x_q \geq p$) partitions.
4. Recursing until every point is isolated or tree height reaches $\log_2 n$.

**Step 2 — Path length for a point** $h(\mathbf{x})$ is the number of edges from root to the leaf containing $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 3 — Average path length of an unsuccessful BST search** (used to normalise the score):

The expected depth of a node in a BST with $n$ nodes when search fails is:

$$c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n} = 2 \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \frac{1}{k} - \frac{2(n-1)}{n} \cdot 1$$

**Step 4 — Harmonic number approximation:**

$$H(n-1) \approx \ln(n-1) + \gamma \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma \approx 0.5772$$

**Step 5 — Final anomaly score derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s(\mathbf{x}, n) &= 2^{-\frac{E(h(\mathbf{x}))}{c(n)}} \\
\text{When } E(h(\mathbf{x})) \to c(n) &\Rightarrow s \to 0.5 \text{ (normal)} \\
\text{When } E(h(\mathbf{x})) \to 0 &\Rightarrow s \to 1 \text{ (anomaly)} \\
\text{When } E(h(\mathbf{x})) \to n-1 &\Rightarrow s \to 0 \text{ (deeply normal)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 ESE style):**

> *Suppose in an iForest of 100 trees, the average path length of a candidate network flow across all iTrees is $E(h(\mathbf{x})) = 3.2$, and $n = 256$. Compute the anomaly score.*

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c(256) &= 2 H(255) - \frac{2 \cdot 255}{256} \\
&= 2 (\ln 255 + 0.5772) - 1.9922 \\
&= 2 (5.5413 + 0.5772) - 1.9922 \\
&= 2 (6.1185) - 1.9922 \\
&= 12.2370 - 1.9922 \\
&= 10.2448
\end{aligned}$$

$$s(\mathbf{x}, 256) = 2^{-3.2 / 10.2448} = 2^{-0.3123} = e^{-0.3123 \cdot \ln 2} = e^{-0.2165} \approx 0.805$$

Since $s \approx 0.805 > 0.5$ and approaching 1, the flow is flagged as **anomalous**. (The threshold is typically 0.5 or 0.6 depending on contamination rate.)

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — One-Class SVM Dual Problem

**Step 1 — Start with the primal form** (Schölkopf et al.):

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, \rho, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + \frac{1}{\nu n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i - \rho$$

subject to $\mathbf{w} \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \geq \rho - \xi_i$, $\xi_i \geq 0$.

**Step 2 — Construct the Lagrangian** with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\beta_i \geq 0$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + \frac{1}{\nu n} \sum \xi_i - \rho - \sum \alpha_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i) - \rho + \xi_i) - \sum \beta_i \xi_i$$

**Step 3 — Take derivatives and set to zero** (KKT conditions):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} &= \mathbf{w} - \sum_i \alpha_i \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \rho} &= -\sum_i \alpha_i + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \sum_i \alpha_i = 1 \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} &= \frac{1}{\nu n} - \alpha_i - \beta_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq \frac{1}{\nu n}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the dual problem:**

$$\min_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

subject to $\sum_i \alpha_i = 1$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq 1/(\nu n)$.

**Step 5 — Kernel substitution:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$. For RBF: $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$.

**Step 6 — Final decision function:**

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sgn}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) - \rho\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU 2024:** $\rho$ is computed from any support vector with $0 < \alpha_i < 1/(\nu n)$ as $\rho = \sum_j \alpha_j K(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i)$. This is why **only a small subset of training points (the support vectors) define the decision boundary**, making OC-SVM memory-efficient.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important comparison table for KTU 2024 14-mark questions.**

| Dimension | Z-Score / Grubbs | Mahalanobis | KDE | Isolation Forest | OC-SVM | Autoencoder |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Assumption** | Gaussian | Multivariate Gaussian | None (kernel) | None | None | None |
| **Dimensionality** | Low (1D) | Medium (≤ 20) | Low–Medium | High | High | Very High |
| **Training Speed** | Instant | Fast (covariance) | Slow | Fast | Slow | Slow |
| **Inference Speed** | Instant | Fast | Slow | Fast | Fast | Fast |
| **Interpretability** | Very High | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| **Handles Correlation** | No | **Yes** | No | Yes (implicit) | Yes (kernel) | Yes (latent) |
| **Robust to Noise** | Low | Low | Medium | **High** | Medium | Medium |
| **Cyber Dataset Suitability** | Simple NetFlow | NSL-KDD | Rare | **All IDS datasets** | Zero-day IDS | APT detection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Anomaly Detection Pipeline — End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[S1: Raw Cyber Telemetry] --> B[S2: Log Parser and Normaliser]
    B --> C[S3: Feature Engineering Module]
    C --> D[S4: Feature Vector X]
    D --> E[S5: Algorithm Selection Layer]
    E --> F[S6A: Statistical Method]
    E --> G[S6B: Isolation Forest]
    E --> H[S6C: One Class SVM]
    E --> I[S6D: Deep Autoencoder]
    F --> J[S7: Anomaly Score]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{S8: Threshold Check}
    K -->|Score Above Threshold| L[S9: Alert Generation]
    K -->|Score Below Threshold| M[S10: Normal Logged]
    L --> N[S11: SOC Analyst Review]
    N --> O[S12: SOAR Response Action]
    M --> P[S13: Model Retraining Loop]
    L --> P
```

---

## 4.2 Isolation Forest — Tree Isolation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Root: Full Dataset of 256 Flows] -->|Split on Packet Size 320| T1a[Left: Small Packets]
    T0 -->|Split on Packet Size 320| T1b[Right: Large Packets]
    T1a -->|Split on Duration 0.5s| T2a[Small and Short Normal]
    T1a -->|Split on Duration 0.5s| T2b[Small and Long Suspicious]
    T1b -->|Split on Protocol Flag| T3a[TCP Normal]
    T1b -->|Split on Protocol Flag| T3b[UDP Anomaly Flagged]
    T2a --> T4a[Path Length 4 Normal]
    T2b --> T4b[Path Length 3 Suspicious]
    T3a --> T4c[Path Length 4 Normal]
    T3b --> T4d[Path Length 2 Anomaly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that the anomalous flow (T4d) is isolated at a much shorter path length (2) compared to normal flows (4). This is the core intuition behind iForest — **anomalies get isolated early**, and short path lengths translate to a high anomaly score.

---

## 4.3 One-Class SVM — Kernel Mapping and Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Original Feature Space X] -->|Phi Mapping| A2[RKHS High Dimensional Space]
    A2 -->|Maximise Margin from Origin| A3[Separating Hyperplane]
    A3 -->|Project Back| B1[Non Linear Boundary in X]
    A3 -->|Support Vectors Define Boundary| B2[SV1 and SV2 Hold the Plane]
    B1 --> C[Test Point x Classification]
    C -->|Above Boundary| D1[Normal Class]
    C -->|Below Boundary| D2[Anomaly Class]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** OC-SVM is mathematically equivalent to a **Support Vector Data Description (SVDD)** when using an RBF kernel. The hyperplane separates data points from the origin; points projected *towards* the origin are outliers.

---

## 4.4 Statistical vs. Machine-Learning Methods — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Selecting an Anomaly Detection Method] --> Q1{Is data labelled?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Supervised| Q2[Use Classifier RF or XGBoost]
    Q1 -->|No Unsupervised| Q3{Is dimensionality low and 10 or less?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Data approximately Gaussian?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5[Use Mahalanobis Distance]
    Q4 -->|No| Q6[Use KDE or LOF]
    Q3 -->|No High Dimensional| Q7{Need interpretability?}
    Q7 -->|Yes| Q8[Use Isolation Forest]
    Q7 -->|No| Q9{Have abundant compute?}
    Q9 -->|Yes| Q10[Use Autoencoder or LSTM AE]
    Q9 -->|No| Q11[Use One Class SVM with RBF Kernel]
```

---

## 4.5 Operational Deployment Architecture in a SOC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[SIEM Aggregator] --> srcB[Log Shipper Logstash]
    srcC[EDR Telemetry] --> srcB
    srcD[NetFlow Collector] --> srcB
    srcB --> fe[Feature Engineering Pipeline]
    fe --> store[(Feature Store on Kafka)]
    store --> model1[Isolation Forest Model]
    store --> model2[OC SVM Model]
    store --> model3[Autoencoder Model]
    model1 --> score[Ensemble Scoring Engine]
    model2 --> score
    model3 --> score
    score --> alert[Alert Manager]
    alert --> soc[SOC Dashboard]
    alert --> soar[SOAR Playbook Executor]
    soar --> action[Containment and Forensic Capture]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Q1. Define anomaly detection in the context of cyber security and list the three types of anomalies. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Anomaly detection is the process of identifying patterns in data that deviate significantly from established normal behaviour, signalling potential security incidents such as intrusions, fraud, or malware. The three types are: (1) **Point anomalies** — a single data instance deviating from the rest (e.g., one unusually large data transfer), (2) **Contextual anomalies** — an instance anomalous in a specific context like time or location (e.g., a login at 3 AM), and (3) **Collective anomalies** — a group of related instances that together form an abnormal pattern (e.g., a sequence of failed logins indicating brute force).

---

### Q2. State the Isolation Forest anomaly score formula and explain the significance of the term $c(n)$. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The Isolation Forest anomaly score is given by $s(\mathbf{x}, n) = 2^{-E(h(\mathbf{x}))/c(n)}$, where $E(h(\mathbf{x}))$ is the average path length of point $\mathbf{x}$ over all isolation trees, and $c(n)$ is the average path length of an unsuccessful search in a Binary Search Tree of $n$ nodes. The term $c(n)$ serves as a **normalising constant** that bounds $E(h(\mathbf{x}))$ between 0 and $c(n)$, ensuring the score lies in $[0, 1]$. A score approaching 1 indicates an anomaly, while a score below 0.5 indicates a normal point.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Statistical & Multivariate Methods

**Q.A (a)** Explain the Z-score method and Grubbs' test for anomaly detection. Derive the decision rule for Grubbs' test and discuss its limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.A (b)** With a neat mathematical derivation, show that the Mahalanobis distance reduces to the squared Euclidean distance when the covariance matrix is the identity matrix. Hence explain its advantage over Euclidean distance in multivariate cyber data. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Step 1:** The Z-score is defined as $Z_i = (x_i - \mu)/\sigma$, measuring deviation in standard-deviation units. A threshold of $\vert Z_i \vert > 3$ (the 3-sigma rule) flags an anomaly under a Gaussian assumption.

**Step 2:** Grubbs' test statistic: $G = \max_i \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert / s$, where $s$ is the sample standard deviation.

**Step 3:** Critical value derivation — the test is based on the t-distribution:

$$G_{crit} = \frac{(n-1)}{\sqrt{n}} \sqrt{\frac{t_{\alpha/(2n), n-2}^2}{n - 2 + t_{\alpha/(2n), n-2}^2}}$$

**Step 4:** Decision rule: If $G > G_{crit}$, reject the null hypothesis (data has no outliers) and flag the most extreme point as an outlier.

**Step 5:** **Limitations** — assumes normality, sensitive to masking effect (one outlier can hide another), univariate only, and requires manual sample size consideration.

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step 1:** Mahalanobis distance: $D_M(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute $\Sigma = I$ (identity matrix):

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T I^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})} = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})} = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu} \Vert_2$$

This is the **Euclidean distance** between $\mathbf{x}$ and the mean.

**Step 3:** **Advantage over Euclidean distance:** When features are correlated (e.g., packet size and inter-arrival time in HTTP traffic), the covariance matrix $\Sigma \neq I$, and the Mahalanobis distance accounts for the **shape and orientation of the data cloud**. Euclidean distance treats all features as independent and identically scaled, which produces **excessive false positives** in high-dimensional correlated security telemetry.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Machine Learning Models

**Q.B (a)** Explain the Isolation Forest algorithm with its anomaly score derivation. Discuss how the algorithm leverages the "anomalies are easier to isolate" hypothesis. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B (b)** With the necessary mathematical formulation, explain the One-Class SVM algorithm. Discuss the role of the $\nu$ parameter and the RBF kernel in cyber security applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Step 1:** Isolation Forest (Liu, Ting, Zhou — 2008) builds an ensemble of Isolation Trees (iTrees). Each iTree is constructed by recursively selecting a random feature and a random split value until each data point is isolated.

**Step 2:** **Hypothesis:** Anomalies are "few and different," hence they require **fewer splits** (shorter path length) to be isolated.

**Step 3:** Anomaly score: $s(\mathbf{x}, n) = 2^{-E(h(\mathbf{x}))/c(n)}$, where $E(h(\mathbf{x}))$ is the average path length over $t$ trees and $c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - 2(n-1)/n$ is the BST normalisation constant.

**Step 4:** Score interpretation:
- $s \approx 1$: highly anomalous (short path, isolated quickly)
- $s < 0.5$: normal point
- $s \approx 0.5$: ambiguous

**Step 5:** **Cyber application:** iForest is the de-facto choice for **unsupervised intrusion detection** in datasets like NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, and CICIDS-2017 because it requires no labelled attack data, handles high dimensionality, and has $O(n \log n)$ training complexity.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Step 1:** One-Class SVM (Schölkopf et al., 2001) learns a hyperplane that maximally separates the data from the origin in the Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS).

**Step 2:** Primal optimisation problem:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, \rho, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + \frac{1}{\nu n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i - \rho$$

subject to $\mathbf{w} \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \geq \rho - \xi_i$, $\xi_i \geq 0$.

**Step 3:** **Role of $\nu$:** The $\nu \in (0, 1)$ parameter is an **upper bound on the fraction of training errors** and a **lower bound on the fraction of support vectors**. In a cyber-security context, $\nu$ can be set to the expected anomaly contamination rate (e.g., $\nu = 0.01$ for 1% expected malicious traffic in a corporate network).

**Step 4:** **Role of the RBF kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$. The RBF kernel maps cyber features into an infinite-dimensional space, enabling a **non-linear decision boundary** in the original space. This is critical because attack patterns form irregular, non-linear clusters in feature space.

**Step 5:** **Decision function:** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sgn}(\sum_i \alpha_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) - \rho)$. Output $+1$ = normal, $-1$ = anomaly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 Valuation on this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "$\nu$ parameter" explanation** in OC-SVM answers — examiners explicitly allocate 2 marks for $\nu$ interpretation. Writing only the objective function without discussing $\nu$ will cost you those marks.
> 2. **Confusing Mahalanobis with Manhattan distance** — the squared term $(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$ is *the* examiner's favourite trap.
> 3. **Skipping the normalisation constant $c(n)$** in iForest answers — a full-marks answer must derive the BST path-length constant.
> 4. **Not distinguishing parametric vs. non-parametric** statistical methods — this is a guaranteed sub-question in 14-mark papers.
> 5. **Writing generic AI answers instead of cyber-security-specific examples** — KTU 2024 Scheme rewards "applied" answers. Always tie algorithms back to **NSL-KDD, network packets, or authentication logs**.
> 6. **Skipping the confusion matrix and F1-score formulas** in Section 1 metrics — at least 2 marks are reserved for this in any complete answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this section before the exam!)**

- ✅ **Anomaly Detection** = identifying rare observations that deviate from learned normal behaviour. Three types: **point, contextual, collective**.
- ✅ **Z-Score** under Gaussian assumption: $\vert Z \vert > 3 \Rightarrow$ anomaly. **3-sigma rule** covers 99.7% of normal data.
- ✅ **Grubbs' Test** for single outlier in univariate data — uses $t$-distribution critical value.
- ✅ **Mahalanobis Distance** is the multivariate, correlation-aware generalisation. Reduces to Euclidean when $\Sigma = I$. Decision rule: $D_M^2 > \chi^2_{d, 0.975}$.
- ✅ **KDE** estimates PDF without distributional assumption. Bandwidth $h$ is critical — too small → overfit, too large → over-smoothed.
- ✅ **Isolation Forest** = ensemble of random iTrees. Anomalies have **short path lengths**. Score: $s = 2^{-E(h)/c(n)}$. No labels required.
- ✅ **OC-SVM** = learn a hyperplane separating data from the origin in RKHS. Uses RBF kernel for non-linear boundaries. $\nu$ controls the upper bound on training errors.
- ✅ **LOF** = local density comparison. $\text{LOF} > 1$ indicates an outlier. Good for clustered anomalies.
- ✅ **Autoencoders** = reconstruction error is the anomaly score. Works on high-dimensional telemetry.
- ✅ **Key Security Datasets:** NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, CICIDS-2017, Kyoto 2006+. Standard evaluation: Accuracy, Detection Rate, FPR, F1-Score.
- ✅ **Cyber-Specific Tip:** Always normalise features (StandardScaler / MinMax) before feeding into distance-based or kernel methods.
- ✅ **Algorithm Selection Cheat Code:** *Low-dim + Gaussian → Mahalanobis. High-dim + unlabelled → iForest. Non-linear boundary → OC-SVM. Time-series → LSTM-AE. Resource-constrained IoT → iForest.*
- ✅ **Production Reality:** Real SOCs use **ensemble scoring** (iForest + OC-SVM + AE) to reduce false positives, not a single algorithm.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO2 — Apply AI/ML techniques for cyber threat detection and mitigation.**

---

**End of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 Notes — Topic: AI Techniques for Security (Anomaly Detection)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT633 - AI FOR CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Threat Intelligence and Prediction: Data Collection and Processing - Gathering and Preprocessing Threat Data, Feature Extraction and Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Threat Intelligence — Formal Definition

**Threat Intelligence (TI)** is the collection, processing, and analysis of evidence-based knowledge about existing or emerging threats to assets, derived from diverse internal and external sources, that can be used to inform defensive decisions and proactive countermeasures. In the context of AI-driven cyber security, TI forms the foundational data layer upon which machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) models are trained to predict, classify, and respond to malicious activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Threat Intelligence = **Context + Indicators + Actionable Insight**. Raw indicators (IPs, hashes, domains) alone are *not* intelligence — they become intelligence only when processed, correlated, and contextualized.

## 1.2 Intuition: The "Hospital Triage" Analogy

Imagine a large hospital emergency room. Doctors cannot treat patients without first performing **triage**:
- **Step 1 — Gathering Data:** Ambulances bring in patients (logs, alerts, packet captures arrive from network sensors, SIEMs, honeypots).
- **Step 2 — Preprocessing:** Nurses record vitals, clean wounds, and standardize records (parsing, normalization, deduplication).
- **Step 3 — Feature Extraction:** Doctors identify key indicators — heart rate, blood pressure, symptoms (entropy, payload length, protocol type).
- **Step 4 — Diagnosis (Prediction):** Based on extracted features, the doctor diagnoses the illness and prescribes treatment (ML classifier predicts: benign vs. malicious).

**Threat Intelligence and Data Processing is the cyber-security equivalent of medical triage.** Without clean, well-engineered features, even the most powerful AI model will produce garbage predictions — a phenomenon often called **"Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO)"**.

## 1.3 Core Sub-Topics Defined

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of this Module (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Gathering Threat Data** — Sources, collection strategies, and data taxonomy.
> 2. **Preprocessing Threat Data** — Cleaning, normalization, transformation, balancing.
> 3. **Feature Extraction and Engineering** — Crafting discriminative numerical/vector representations of threat data for ML pipelines.

## 1.4 Key Standardized Metrics

The following quantitative metrics are used universally in the threat data processing literature:
- **Precision, Recall, F1-Score** — model evaluation.
- **Information Gain (IG)** — feature importance.
- **Entropy (H)** — uncertainty measure.
- **TF-IDF Weight** — term importance in textual threat reports.
- **Packet Capture (PCAP) sample size** — measured in **bytes, kilobytes, or flows**.
- **IOC (Indicator of Compromise)** — atomic, computed, or behavioral.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase 1 — Gathering Threat Data (The "Collection" Pipeline)

Threat data is gathered from heterogeneous, voluminous, and high-velocity sources. A standard taxonomy used in KTU-aligned AI/cyber curricula is:

### 2.1.1 Internal Sources (First-Party Telemetry)
- **Network Logs:** NetFlow, sFlow, IPFIX records from routers/switches.
- **Host Logs:** Windows Event Logs, Sysmon, Linux `auth.log`, `/var/log/secure`.
- **Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) telemetry:** Process trees, file modifications, registry changes.
- **SIEM Aggregates:** Pre-correlated alerts from Splunk, QRadar, Elastic Stack.

### 2.1.2 External Sources (Open-Source and Commercial)
- **Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT):** AlienVault OTX, AbuseIPDB, MISP, VirusTotal, Shodan.
- **Commercial Feeds:** Mandiant, Recorded Future, CrowdStrike Falcon Intel.
- **Dark Web / Deep Web Monitors:** Tor hidden-service scrapers, marketplace monitors.
- **Government & ISACs:** CERT-In advisories, US-CERT, ENISA bulletins.

### 2.1.3 Honeypot / Honeynet Data
Decoy systems instrumented to capture attacker behavior. High-fidelity datasets like **CICIDS2017**, **NSL-KDD**, **UNSW-NB15**, and **CTU-13** are derived from such controlled environments.

## 2.2 Phase 2 — Preprocessing Threat Data

Raw threat data is notoriously noisy. The preprocessing pipeline typically contains these stages:

1. **Data Ingestion & Parsing** — Convert PCAP, JSON, XML, CSV, EVT formats into structured tables.
2. **Deduplication** — Remove redundant records (e.g., the same malicious hash reported 1,000 times).
3. **Handling Missing Values** — Imputation (mean/median/mode) or removal.
4. **Outlier Detection** — Z-score, IQR-based filtering, Isolation Forest.
5. **Normalization / Scaling** — Min-Max scaling or Z-score standardization.
6. **Encoding Categorical Variables** — One-hot, label, target encoding.
7. **Class Imbalance Handling** — SMOTE, ADASYN, undersampling, class weights.
8. **Time-Series Alignment** — Sliding window segmentation for sequential data.

## 2.3 Phase 3 — Feature Extraction and Feature Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feature Engineering is the single most impactful step in an AI-for-cyber pipeline.** Empirically, domain-driven feature engineering often improves model accuracy more than swapping algorithms.

### 2.3.1 Categories of Features
- **Network Flow Features:** Duration, protocol, src/dst bytes, packet count, inter-arrival time.
- **Statistical Features:** Mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis of packet sizes.
- **Temporal Features:** Hour-of-day, day-of-week, burst frequency.
- **Behavioral Features:** System call sequences, API call n-grams.
- **Textual Features (Threat Reports):** TF-IDF vectors, embeddings.
- **Graph Features:** Node degree, betweenness centrality on communication graphs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Range / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Min-Max Scaling | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | Normalize feature to [0, 1] | $\vert x' \vert \le 1$ |
| Z-Score Standardization | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardize to zero mean, unit variance | $\vert z \vert \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Uncertainty in feature distribution | $0 \le H \le \log_2 n$ bits |
| Information Gain | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in A} \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Reduction in entropy by splitting on feature A | $IG \ge 0$ |
| TF (Term Frequency) | $tf(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Frequency of term *t* in document *d* | $0 \le tf \le 1$ |
| IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) | $idf(t,D) = \log \dfrac{\vert D \vert}{\vert \{ d \in D : t \in d \} \vert}$ | Rarity weight of term across corpus | $idf \ge 0$ |
| TF-IDF | $tfidf(t,d,D) = tf(t,d) \times idf(t,D)$ | Term importance weight | $tfidf \ge 0$ |
| Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert\vert A \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert B \vert\vert}$ | Similarity between threat vectors | $-1 \le \cos \theta \le 1$ |
| Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = \sum_{x,y} p(x,y) \log \dfrac{p(x,y)}{p(x)p(y)}$ | Shared info between features and labels | $I \ge 0$ bits |
| SMOTE Synthetic Sample | $x_{new} = x_i + \lambda \cdot (x_{nn} - x_i), \lambda \sim U(0,1)$ | Oversample minority class | synthetic instance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Notation:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ denotes either "such that" in set-builder notation, **cardinality** $\vert S \vert$ (number of elements in set *S*), or absolute value. KTU examiners specifically test whether students distinguish $\vert S \vert$ (cardinality) from $S$ (set itself).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These techniques are deployed in commercial systems like:
- **Splunk User Behavior Analytics (UBA)** — uses entropy and statistical features.
- **Darktrace** — employs unsupervised feature engineering on network flows.
- **Microsoft Defender ATP** — uses behavioral feature sequences and graph features.
- **VirusTotal** — applies TF-IDF and embeddings on malware binary strings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shannon Entropy of a Threat Feature

**Goal:** Derive the Shannon entropy expression for a discrete random variable representing the distribution of a categorical threat feature (e.g., protocol type in a network flow).

**Step 1 — Define the probability distribution.**
Let a categorical feature $X$ take values $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ with probabilities $\{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$ such that $p_i \ge 0$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = 1$.

**Step 2 — Define self-information.**
The self-information (surprisal) of outcome $x_i$ is defined as the negative log of its probability:

$$
I(x_i) = -\log_2(p_i)
$$

**Logic:** Rare events carry more information than common events — a single malicious payload among millions of benign flows is "surprising."

**Step 3 — Take the expected value over all outcomes.**
The entropy is the expected self-information:

$$
H(X) = \mathbb{E}[I(X)] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot I(x_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the surprisal definition.**

$$
H(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot (-\log_2 p_i) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i}
$$

**Boundary Check:** If $p_i = 1$ for exactly one $i$ (deterministic), $H(X) = 0$. If uniform $p_i = 1/n$, then $H(X) = \log_2 n$ (maximum entropy).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Information Gain for Feature Selection

**Goal:** Show how Information Gain (IG) is derived from entropy, justifying its use in selecting the *best* feature to split a threat dataset.

**Step 1 — Compute the entropy of the parent dataset $S$.**

$$
H(S) = -\sum_{c \in C} p(c) \log_2 p(c)
$$

where $C$ is the set of class labels (e.g., {Benign, Malicious}).

**Step 2 — Partition $S$ by feature $A$.**
For a feature $A$ with values $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_v\}$, partition $S$ into subsets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_v$ where $S_j = \{ s \in S : A(s) = a_j \}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted entropy after the split.**

$$
H(S \vert A) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \dfrac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, H(S_j)
$$

**Logic:** Each subset $S_j$ contributes its entropy, weighted by its fraction of the total samples $\vert S_j \vert / \vert S \vert$.

**Step 4 — Information Gain is the reduction in entropy.**

$$
\boxed{IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A) = H(S) - \sum_{j=1}^{v} \dfrac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_j)}
$$

**Interpretation:** A feature $A^*$ with the highest $IG(S, A^*)$ is the most discriminative and is selected as the root in a Decision Tree (ID3/C4.5 algorithms used in IDS).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: TF-IDF Weight (Critical for Threat Report NLP)

**Goal:** Derive the TF-IDF weighting for a term $t$ appearing in document $d$ within a corpus of $N$ documents.

**Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF).**
Normalize the raw count $f_{t,d}$ by the total terms in the document:

$$
tf(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}
$$

**Step 2 — Document Frequency (DF).**
The number of documents in the corpus $D$ that contain the term $t$:

$$
df(t, D) = \vert \{ d \in D : t \in d \} \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF).**
Apply a logarithm to dampen the effect of very common terms:

$$
idf(t, D) = \log \dfrac{\vert D \vert}{df(t, D)}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine TF and IDF multiplicatively.**

$$
\boxed{tfidf(t, d, D) = tf(t,d) \times \log \dfrac{\vert D \vert}{df(t, D)}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Terms like "the", "attack" appear in nearly every threat report (low IDF), so they get suppressed. Rare yet meaningful terms like "Zeus", "CVE-2024-XXXX" get amplified.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Preprocessing Techniques

| **Technique** | **Input** | **Output** | **Use-Case in Threat Data** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Min-Max Scaling | Numeric column | Bounded [0, 1] | Neural network inputs (e.g., packet length) |
| Z-Score Normalization | Numeric column | Mean 0, Var 1 | SVM, Logistic Regression on flow features |
| One-Hot Encoding | Categorical column | Binary vector | Protocol, Flag, Service type |
| SMOTE | Imbalanced binary set | Synthetic minority samples | DDoS vs. Benign imbalance |
| TF-IDF | Threat report corpus | Sparse vector matrix | NLP-based IOC extraction |
| Wavelet Transform | Time-series traffic | Frequency-time features | Burst anomaly detection |
| Graph Embedding (Node2Vec) | Communication graph | Dense node vectors | Lateral movement detection |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Information Gain on a Toy IDS Dataset

Consider 10 network flows: 4 malicious, 6 benign. We test the feature *"Packet Size > 500 bytes"*.

| **Split** | **Malicious** | **Benign** | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Size > 500 | 3 | 1 | 4 |
| Size ≤ 500 | 1 | 5 | 6 |

**Step A — Parent entropy:**

$$
H(S) = -\left(\tfrac{4}{10}\log_2 \tfrac{4}{10} + \tfrac{6}{10}\log_2 \tfrac{6}{10}\right) \approx 0.971 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step B — Subset entropies:**

$$
H(S_{>500}) = -\left(\tfrac{3}{4}\log_2 \tfrac{3}{4} + \tfrac{1}{4}\log_2 \tfrac{1}{4}\right) \approx 0.811 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
H(S_{\le 500}) = -\left(\tfrac{1}{6}\log_2 \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{5}{6}\log_2 \tfrac{5}{6}\right) \approx 0.650 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step C — Weighted post-split entropy:**

$$
H(S \vert A) = \tfrac{4}{10}(0.811) + \tfrac{6}{10}(0.650) \approx 0.715 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step D — Information Gain:**

$$
IG(S, A) = 0.971 - 0.715 = 0.256 \text{ bits}
$$

**Conclusion:** The feature "Packet Size > 500 bytes" provides 0.256 bits of discriminatory power — a positive value confirms it is a useful feature for the IDS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Threat Data Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threat Data Sources] --> B[Data Ingestion Layer]
    B --> C[Preprocessing Engine]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering Module]
    D --> E[ML/DL Model Training]
    E --> F[Threat Prediction Output]

    A1[Network Logs PCAP] --> A
    A2[Host Telemetry EDR] --> A
    A3[OSINT Feeds MISP VirusTotal] --> A
    A4[Honeypot Captures] --> A

    C1[Parsing and Deduplication] --> C
    C2[Missing Value Imputation] --> C
    C3[Outlier Removal Z-Score IQR] --> C
    C4[Class Balancing SMOTE] --> C
    C5[Normalization MinMax Z-Score] --> C

    D1[Statistical Features] --> D
    D2[Temporal Features] --> D
    D3[Behavioral n-grams] --> D
    D4[Textual TF-IDF Embeddings] --> D
    D5[Graph Features] --> D

    E --> E1[Random Forest]
    E --> E2[XGBoost]
    E --> E3[LSTM Autoencoder]
    E --> E4[Transformer IDS]

    F1[Malware Family] --> F
    F2[Attack Type] --> F
    F3[Threat Severity Score] --> F
    F --> F1
    F --> F2
    F --> F3
```

## 4.2 Feature Engineering Sub-Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw Threat Records] --> parse[Parse Formats JSON PCAP CSV]
    parse --> clean[Cleaning and Deduplication]
    clean --> impute[Impute Missing Values]
    impute --> balance[Class Imbalance Handling]
    balance --> encode[Encode Categorical Variables]
    encode --> scale[Numerical Scaling]
    scale --> extract[Feature Extraction Layer]
    extract --> select[Feature Selection IG RFE Chi-Square]
    select --> model[Train AI Threat Model]
    model --> deploy[Deploy for Real-Time Prediction]
```

## 4.3 Threat Intelligence Data Taxonomy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InternalSources[Internal First Party Sources]
        s1[SIEM Logs]
        s2[NetFlow Records]
        s3[EDR Telemetry]
        s4[Firewall Logs]
    end

    subgraph ExternalSources[External Third Party Sources]
        s5[OSINT MISP OTX]
        s6[Commercial TI Feeds]
        s7[Dark Web Monitors]
        s8[Government CERT Advisories]
    end

    subgraph SyntheticSources[Synthetic / Honeypot Sources]

    end

    InternalSources --> pipe[Unified Threat Data Lake]

```

## 4.4 Decision Flow for Feature Selection in Threat Data

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Start Feature Selection] --> check1{Feature Type}
    check1 -->|Categorical| cat[Use Chi-Square or Mutual Information]
    check1 -->|Numerical| num[Use ANOVA F-test or Pearson Correlation]
    cat --> rank[Rank Features by Score]
    num --> rank
    rank --> thresh{Threshold Met}
    thresh -->|Yes| keep[Retain Feature]
    thresh -->|No| drop[Discard Feature]
    keep --> final[Final Feature Subset]
    drop --> final
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Differentiate between **threat data**, **threat information**, and **threat intelligence**. Provide one example of each from a real-world SOC (Security Operations Center) workflow.

**Model Answer:**
- **Threat Data:** Raw, unprocessed observations. *Example:* A single firewall log line showing an inbound connection from IP `203.0.113.45` on port 22.
- **Threat Information:** Data that has been aggregated and given context. *Example:* A list of 500 IPs flagged by AbuseIPDB as TOR exit nodes, including geolocation.
- **Threat Intelligence:** Processed, analyzed, and actionable knowledge that informs decisions. *Example:* A finished intelligence report correlating TOR exit-node activity with brute-force SSH attempts against a specific asset, recommending firewall rule updates.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List and briefly explain any **three preprocessing steps** essential for preparing raw network traffic data for an AI-based Intrusion Detection System (IDS).

**Model Answer (3 of any 6):**
1. **Deduplication** — Removes identical repeated packets/flows to prevent bias toward high-volume scanners.
2. **Normalization (Min-Max Scaling)** — Maps heterogeneous flow features (e.g., packet count vs. duration in ms) to a common [0, 1] range, ensuring no single feature dominates the model.
3. **Class Imbalance Handling (SMOTE)** — Synthetically generates minority-class samples (e.g., rare U2R attacks) to prevent the model from defaulting to "benign" predictions.
4. **Missing Value Imputation** — Replaces `NaN` values in incomplete flows with mean/median, preserving dataset size.
5. **Categorical Encoding** — Converts protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP) into numerical form via one-hot encoding.
6. **Outlier Removal** — Z-score filtering eliminates impossibly large or negative flow values caused by sensor errors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **end-to-end pipeline for gathering and preprocessing threat data** in an AI-driven cyber security system. Cover at least four data sources and four preprocessing operations with justification.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, derive the **Shannon Entropy** and **Information Gain** for a categorical feature in a binary classification (benign vs. malicious) threat dataset.

**Model Solution (a):**

**1. Data Gathering Phase** — Data is harvested from:
- **Network sensors:** PCAP files, NetFlow v5/v9, IPFIX — provide packet-level and flow-level visibility.
- **Endpoint telemetry:** EDR feeds (process trees, file hashes, registry writes) — provide host-centric attack traces.
- **OSINT platforms:** MISP, VirusTotal, AlienVault OTX — provide community-curated IOCs.
- **Honeypots/Honeynets:** Decoy systems capturing zero-day attacker TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures).
- **SIEM aggregations:** Splunk/QRadar outputs that pre-correlate logs.

**2. Preprocessing Phase** — Four essential operations:
- **Deduplication:** Critical because a single DDoS attack can produce millions of identical flows. Without dedup, the model becomes biased toward DDoS patterns. Hash-based dedup (e.g., MD5 of (src_ip, dst_ip, dst_port, protocol)) is standard.
- **Missing Value Imputation:** Real-world network logs often have gaps (e.g., corrupted packet headers). Mean/median imputation for numerical features; mode imputation for categorical.
- **Outlier Detection:** Z-score with $\vert z \vert > 3$ is filtered. Justified because extreme outliers from sensor errors destroy the Gaussian assumptions of many models.
- **Class Balancing:** Threat datasets are highly imbalanced (typically < 1% malicious). SMOTE is applied to oversample the minority class, preventing the classifier from trivially predicting "benign" with 99% accuracy.

**Justification Summary:** Each step is necessary to ensure the data fed into the ML/DL model is **clean, balanced, and numerically consistent** — without which the model produces unreliable predictions.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Define entropy for a binary classification problem.**
Let class $C = \{C_1, C_2\}$ with probabilities $p_1$ and $p_2$ where $p_1 + p_2 = 1$.

$$
H(C) = -p_1 \log_2 p_1 - p_2 \log_2 p_2
$$

**Step 2 — Example computation.**
Suppose in 100 flows, 80 are benign and 20 are malicious. Then $p_1 = 0.8, p_2 = 0.2$.

$$
H(C) = -(0.8)\log_2(0.8) - (0.2)\log_2(0.2) = 0.7219 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a feature $A$ (e.g., "Protocol = TCP").**
Suppose splitting yields:
- $S_1$ (Protocol = TCP): 60 benign, 18 malicious → 78 total.
- $S_2$ (Protocol ≠ TCP): 20 benign, 2 malicious → 22 total.

**Step 4 — Compute subset entropies.**

$$
H(S_1) = -\tfrac{60}{78}\log_2\tfrac{60}{78} - \tfrac{18}{78}\log_2\tfrac{18}{78} \approx 0.823 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
H(S_2) = -\tfrac{20}{22}\log_2\tfrac{20}{22} - \tfrac{2}{22}\log_2\tfrac{2}{22} \approx 0.502 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Weighted post-split entropy.**

$$
H(S \vert A) = \tfrac{78}{100}(0.823) + \tfrac{22}{100}(0.502) \approx 0.752 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Information Gain.**

$$
IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A) = 0.7219 - 0.752 = -0.030 \text{ bits}
$$

**Interpretation:** Negative IG indicates the feature "Protocol = TCP" actually *adds* confusion — it's not discriminative. A feature with $IG > 0$ would be preferred for tree-based IDS.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe **Feature Extraction and Feature Engineering** in the context of AI-based threat detection. Compare **statistical, behavioral, textual, and graph-based** features with one example each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **TF-IDF weights** for the terms "ransomware", "phishing", and "attack" across a corpus of three threat reports, and explain why TF-IDF is preferred over raw term frequency in threat report analysis.

**Model Solution (a):**

Feature extraction converts raw, semi-structured threat data into numerical vectors that ML models can consume. Feature engineering crafts *new* features from existing ones to improve discriminative power.

| **Category** | **Description** | **Example Feature** | **Typical Algorithm** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Statistical | Aggregate numeric descriptors of traffic | Mean, variance, skewness of packet inter-arrival times | Anomaly detection (Isolation Forest) |
| Behavioral | Sequential patterns of system events | Trigram of system calls: `open, read, exec` | LSTM, HMM for malware |
| Textual | NLP-derived vectors from threat reports | TF-IDF of "LockBit" in a malware report | SVM, BERT for TI |
| Graph | Topology-derived metrics on communication graphs | Node degree of internal host in lateral movement graph | Graph Neural Networks (GNN) |

**Engineering Examples:**
- **Burstiness ratio:** $B = \text{std}(\Delta t) / \text{mean}(\Delta t)$ for packet inter-arrival times — captures regularity of C2 beacons.
- **Header entropy:** $H(\text{header bytes})$ — encrypted/packed malware payloads have near-maximum entropy (~8 bits/byte).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Corpus:** Three threat reports $D = \{d_1, d_2, d_3\}$.

- $d_1$: *"Ransomware attack detected. Ransomware encrypted files."* → words: ransomware(2), attack(1)
- $d_2$: *"Phishing campaign reported."* → words: phishing(1)
- $d_3$: *"Ransomware and phishing attack observed."* → words: ransomware(1), phishing(1), attack(1)

**Step 1 — Compute DF for each term.**
- $df(\text{ransomware}) = 2$ (appears in $d_1, d_3$).
- $df(\text{phishing}) = 2$ (appears in $d_2, d_3$).
- $df(\text{attack}) = 2$ (appears in $d_1, d_3$).
- $\vert D \vert = 3$.

**Step 2 — Compute IDF for each term.**

$$
idf(t) = \log_2 \tfrac{\vert D \vert}{df(t)} = \log_2 \tfrac{3}{2} \approx 0.585
$$

(All three terms share the same IDF since each appears in 2 of 3 documents.)

**Step 3 — Compute TF and TF-IDF for the term "ransomware" in $d_1$.**

$$
tf(\text{ransomware}, d_1) = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
$$

$$
tfidf(\text{ransomware}, d_1) = 0.667 \times 0.585 \approx 0.390
$$

**Step 4 — Full TF-IDF matrix.**

| **Term** | **$d_1$** | **$d_2$** | **$d_3$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| ransomware | 0.390 | 0.000 | 0.195 |
| phishing | 0.000 | 0.585 | 0.585 |
| attack | 0.195 | 0.000 | 0.195 |

**Why TF-IDF over raw TF?**
- Raw TF overweights common words like "attack" (appears in 2 of 3 docs), which carry little discriminative information.
- TF-IDF **down-weights** frequent terms and **up-weights** rare, distinctive terms like "phishing" that strongly identify specific threat categories.
- Result: the TF-IDF vector provides a more discriminative feature space for downstream classifiers (e.g., SVM-based malware family classification).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner Reports):**
> 1. **Confusing data with information with intelligence** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if students treat raw logs as "intelligence." You MUST show the *transformation* (collection → processing → contextualization → action).
> 2. **Forgetting to justify preprocessing choices** — Just listing techniques without explaining *why* (e.g., "SMOTE used because DDoS class is 0.1% of total") costs up to 2 marks.
> 3. **Formula notation errors** — Writing $\vert D \vert$ as $D$ (loses cardinality meaning) or forgetting the negative sign in entropy definitions are common. Always box your final formula.
> 4. **Skipping units and boundary checks** — Entropy must be in *bits*; IG must be shown as $\ge 0$. Examiners reward students who add a one-line sanity check.
> 5. **No real-world examples** — A purely theoretical answer is capped at 70% marks. Cite Splunk, VirusTotal, MISP, or CICIDS2017 to score full marks.
> 6. **Mixing up TF and IDF** — Many students swap numerator/denominator in the IDF formula. Memorize: $\log(\text{Total Docs} / \text{Docs Containing Term})$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield KTU 2024 Points:**
> - **Threat Intelligence ≠ Threat Data.** Intelligence is *processed* and *actionable*.
> - **Three pipeline phases:** Gather → Preprocess → Engineer Features.
> - **Four data source families:** Internal (SIEM/EDR), External (OSINT), Commercial TI feeds, Synthetic (Honeypot datasets).
> - **Six preprocessing essentials:** Parsing, Deduplication, Imputation, Outlier Removal, Normalization, Class Balancing.
> - **Four feature types:** Statistical, Behavioral, Textual, Graph-based.
> - **Must-memorize formulas:** Entropy $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$, Information Gain, TF-IDF, Min-Max, Z-Score, Cosine Similarity.
> - **Class imbalance is the rule, not the exception** in threat datasets — always apply SMOTE or class weights.
> - **TF-IDF > raw TF** for textual threat report analysis because it suppresses stop-words and amplifies discriminative terms.
> - **Domain-driven feature engineering** > algorithm swaps. A well-crafted feature beats a fancier model.
> - **Notation vigilance:** Use $\vert S \vert$ for cardinality, not $S$. Use $H(X)$ not $HX$ for entropy.
> - **Always validate:** Use stratified k-fold cross-validation on imbalanced threat data; never random split.
> - **Cite real systems:** Splunk, Darktrace, VirusTotal, MISP, CICIDS2017 — adds examiner-visible depth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Predictive Analytics: Building and Evaluating Predictive Models for Threat Forecasting, Case Studies on Threat Intelligence Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT633 - AI FOR CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Predictive Analytics: Building and Evaluating Predictive Models for Threat Forecasting, Case Studies on Threat Intelligence Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Predictive Analytics in Cyber Security — Formal Definition

**Predictive Analytics** in cyber security is the disciplined application of statistical algorithms, machine learning models, and data mining techniques to historical and real-time security telemetry in order to **forecast the likelihood, timing, nature, and target of future cyber threats** before they materialize. It transforms a traditionally *reactive* security posture (detect → respond) into a *proactive* one (anticipate → prevent).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course: **PECAT633 – AI for Cyber Security**, Module 2), predictive analytics is positioned as the **forecasting engine of Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)**, consuming Indicators of Compromise (IoCs), Tactics-Techniques-Procedures (TTPs), log streams, and vulnerability data to produce **probabilistic threat forecasts** with measurable confidence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Definition (KTU-Boards Ready):**
> *"Predictive analytics for threat forecasting is the AI-driven process of ingesting heterogeneous threat data, extracting behavioural and contextual features, training statistical models on historical attack patterns, and generating probabilistic predictions of imminent or future adversarial actions."*

## 1.2 The Predictive Analytics Stack (Four Logical Layers)

| Layer | Function | Typical Techniques |
|------|----------|--------------------|
| **Data Acquisition Layer** | Collect logs, NetFlow, EDR alerts, OSINT, dark-web feeds | Syslog, STIX/TAXII, API scraping |
| **Feature Engineering Layer** | Convert raw data into model-ready vectors | TF-IDF on IoCs, embeddings, time-window aggregations |
| **Modelling Layer** | Learn patterns and emit probability scores | Random Forest, XGBoost, LSTM, Hidden Markov Models |
| **Evaluation & Deployment Layer** | Validate accuracy, deploy, monitor drift | Confusion Matrix, ROC-AUC, KS statistic, MLOps |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cyber Weather Forecast"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Predictive Threat Forecasting = Weather Forecasting**
>
> - **Meteorologists** study historical temperature, pressure, and wind data → **Cyber analysts** study historical attack logs, malware samples, and CVE disclosures.
> - A **barometric drop** signals an incoming storm → A **spike in anomalous DNS queries** signals an impending C2 beaconing campaign.
> - A **weather bulletin** says "70% chance of rain tomorrow at 4 PM" → A **threat forecast** says "73% probability of ransomware deployment against finance sector within the next 48 hours, confidence interval ±6%."
> - Both are **probabilistic**, never deterministic — and both get better with **more clean data**.

This analogy is what examiners love to see in 2- or 3-mark definitions: it shows **conceptual ownership**, not rote memorization.

## 1.4 Why Predictive Analytics Matters — The Three Pillars

1. **Anticipation over Reaction** — Reduces Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) from *days* to *minutes/hours*.
2. **Resource Optimisation** — Security Operations Center (SOC) analysts are finite; predictive scoring triages the **top 5% highest-risk alerts**, eliminating alert fatigue.
3. **Strategic Defence Posture** — Enables CISO-level decisions (patch priority, insurance, geopolitical risk) using **quantified threat probabilities** rather than intuition.

## 1.5 Key Terminology Block (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Terminology — Memorize These:**
> - **Threat Forecasting** — Predicting *future* threat occurrence, severity, and target.
> - **Threat Intelligence (TI)** — Evidence-based knowledge about existing or emerging threats.
> - **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)** — Observable artefact (IP, hash, domain) indicating intrusion.
> - **Tactic, Technique, and Procedure (TTP)** — Behavioural description of adversary behaviour, structured under **MITRE ATT&CK**.
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — Probability the model flags benign activity as malicious.
> - **Confidence Score** — Probability output (0 to 1) of a model prediction.
> - **Concept Drift** — When the statistical properties of threat data change over time, degrading model performance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CRISP-DM Adapted for Threat Forecasting

Predictive analytics projects in cyber security almost always follow a **modified CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)** pipeline. Below is the KTU-aligned, step-by-step logic with the *why* attached to every step.

### Step 1 — Business / Threat Understanding
- Define the *forecasting objective*: Will an attack happen? Against whom? Using which TTP? Within what window?
- **Why?** A model trained to predict *whether* a phishing email arrives is fundamentally different from a model predicting *which* employee will click it.

### Step 2 — Data Collection & Labelling
- Pull data from **SIEM** (Splunk, QRadar), **EDR** (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne), **Network TAPs**, and **OSINT** (VirusTotal, MISP, AlienVault OTX).
- **Labelling strategy** is the hardest part: use MITRE ATT&CK tags, red-team exercises, or curated malware repositories (EMBER, CICIDS).
- **Why?** Garbage-in-garbage-out. A model is only as good as the ground-truth labels it learns from.

### Step 3 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering
- Handle class imbalance — attack samples are **< 0.1%** of total traffic. Use **SMOTE**, **ADASYN**, or **undersampling**.
- Engineer features:
  - **Network-level** — bytes/sec, packet entropy, port diversity, DNS query rarity.
  - **Host-level** — process tree depth, file write rate, registry key changes.
  - **Behavioural** — user-logon time deviation, geolocation velocity.
- **Why?** Raw bytes are meaningless to a model. Features carry *semantic signal*.

### Step 4 — Model Selection
Three dominant families, mapped to threat-forecasting tasks:

| Task | Best-Suited Model Family | Reason |
|------|--------------------------|--------|
| **Binary: Attack vs Benign** | Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost | High interpretability, handles tabular IoCs |
| **Multi-class: ATT&CK TTP Classification** | Gradient Boosting, Deep Neural Networks | Captures non-linear feature interactions |
| **Time-series: Attack Volume Forecasting** | ARIMA, Prophet, LSTM, Temporal Fusion Transformer | Models temporal dependencies & seasonality |

### Step 5 — Training & Validation
- Use **stratified k-fold cross-validation** (k = 5 or 10) to preserve class distribution.
- Hold out a **time-shifted test set** (e.g., last 30 days) to simulate real-world drift.

### Step 6 — Evaluation (See Section 2.3 below)
- Compute **Precision, Recall, F1, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC**.
- Inspect **calibration curves** — does a 70% prediction actually occur 70% of the time?

### Step 7 — Deployment & Monitoring
- Wrap model in an API, push to **SIEM/SOAR** as a risk-scoring enrichment.
- Monitor for **concept drift** using the **Population Stability Index (PSI)** or **Kolmogorov–Smirnov test**.

## 2.2 Threat Forecasting — Probabilistic Framing

A threat forecast is a conditional probability statement:

$$P(\text{Threat}_T \mid \text{Historical Data}, \text{Context})$$

Where the model outputs **P** ∈ [0, 1]. The complementary risk score often used in CTI platforms is:

$$\text{Risk} = P(\text{Threat}) \times \text{Impact} \times \text{Asset Value}$$

This is the **NIST SP 800-30** risk formulation, which KTU examiners frequently reference.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Evaluation Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All metrics derive from the **Confusion Matrix**: True Positives (TP), False Positives (FP), True Negatives (TN), False Negatives (FN).

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Meaning | KTU Frequency |
|--------|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | Overall correctness — misleading on imbalanced data | ★★ |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alerts raised, how many were real attacks? | ★★★ |
| **Recall (TPR / Sensitivity)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all real attacks, how many did we catch? | ★★★ |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean — balances precision and recall | ★★★★ |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Benign events wrongly flagged — drives SOC fatigue | ★★★ |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Correctly identified benign traffic | ★★ |
| **ROC-AUC** | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(t))\, dt$ | Threshold-independent discriminative power | ★★★★★ |
| **PR-AUC** | Area under Precision-Recall curve | Better than ROC-AUC for *highly imbalanced* datasets | ★★★★ |
| **Log-Loss (Cross-Entropy)** | $-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} [y_i \log(\hat{p}_i) + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{p}_i)]$ | Penalises confidently wrong predictions — critical for calibrated threat scores | ★★★★ |
| **Brier Score** | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{p}_i - y_i)^2$ | Mean squared error of probabilities — measures calibration | ★★★ |
| **Kolmogorov–Smirnov Statistic** | $\max \vert F_{\text{benign}}(s) - F_{\text{malicious}}(s) \vert$ | Maximum separation between score distributions — popular in fraud/threat scoring | ★★ |
| **Population Stability Index (PSI)** | $\sum (A_i - E_i) \ln(A_i / E_i)$ | Detects feature drift between training and live data — PSI > 0.25 ⇒ major drift | ★★★ |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility — Why These Metrics Drive Engineering Decisions

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Heuristics Used by KTU Examiners and Industry Practitioners:**
> - **Precision < 0.7** → SOC alert fatigue; raise threshold or refine features.
> - **Recall < 0.8** with high-impact threats → unacceptable risk; missing attacks is costlier than false alarms.
> - **ROC-AUC < 0.8** → model is barely better than random; re-engineer features.
> - **Brier Score > 0.25** → probabilities are poorly calibrated; apply **Platt Scaling** or **Isotonic Regression**.
> - **PSI > 0.25** → retrain the model; threat landscape has shifted (e.g., new ransomware family).

## 2.5 The Threat Intelligence Pipeline (Conceptual Map)

The "Building" side of predictive analytics is operationalised through a **Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP)**. The canonical stages:

1. **Collection** — Ingest feeds (STIX/TAXII, RSS, proprietary intel).
2. **Normalization** — Map to common schemas (STIX 2.1, MITRE ATT&CK).
3. **Enrichment** — Add GeoIP, WHOIS, ASN, sandbox detonation results.
4. **Analysis / Correlation** — Cluster related IoCs, attribute to threat actors.
5. **Dissemination** — Push actionable intelligence to firewalls, EDR, SIEM, SOAR.
6. **Feedback Loop** — Analyst verdicts retrain the scoring model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STIX 2.1** (Structured Threat Information eXpression) and **TAXII 2.1** (Trusted Automated eXchange of Intelligence Information) are the **de facto standards** for machine-readable threat intelligence exchange. KTU examiners frequently test the awareness that **STIX defines the *what*; TAXII defines the *how***.
