# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the F1-Score (Exhaustive)

The F1-Score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. We begin from the arithmetic-mean intuition, then justify the harmonic mean through an optimisation argument.

**Step 1 — Define Precision and Recall**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \qquad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 2 — Why not the Arithmetic Mean?**
A naive arithmetic mean $\frac{P + R}{2}$ is dominated by whichever value is larger, which is undesirable for an imbalanced metric such as threat detection. We need a mean that is *low* when *either* Precision *or* Recall is low.

**Step 3 — Harmonic Mean Definition**
For positive reals $a$ and $b$:

$$H = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $a = P$, $b = R$**

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} = \frac{2PR}{P + R}$$

**Step 5 — Expand in Terms of Confusion Matrix Counts**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

This compact form — $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ — is the **calculation-efficient version** KTU examiners expect in long derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation of ROC-AUC Geometrically

**Step 1 — Sort all predictions by descending score.**
**Step 2 — Walk the sorted list, plotting the (FPR, TPR) pair at every threshold.**

$$FPR(t) = \frac{FP(t)}{FP(t) + TN(t)} \qquad TPR(t) = \frac{TP(t)}{TP(t) + FN(t)}$$

**Step 3 — AUC equals the probability that a randomly chosen malicious sample receives a *higher* score than a randomly chosen benign sample** (Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney equivalence):

$$AUC = P(\text{score}_{\text{malicious}} > \text{score}_{\text{benign}})$$

**Step 4 — Empirical Estimator (rank-based form):**

$$AUC = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{rank}_i - \frac{n(n+1)}{2}}{n \cdot m}$$

where $n$ = # benign samples, $m$ = # malicious samples, and $\text{rank}_i$ = rank of the $i$-th benign sample in the combined sorted list.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Threat Forecast Evaluation

A SOC's predictive scoring model was run on **10,000 alerts** in a 24-hour window. Ground truth labelling (post-investigation) yielded:

| Confusion Matrix Cell | Count |
|-----------------------|------:|
| **TP** — Correctly flagged attacks | 180 |
| **FP** — False alarms | 70 |
| **TN** — Correctly cleared benign | 9,650 |
| **FN** — Missed attacks | 100 |

**Compute all KTU-relevant metrics:**

$$Accuracy = \frac{180 + 9{,}650}{10{,}000} = \frac{9{,}830}{10{,}000} = 0.9830$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{180}{180 + 70} = \frac{180}{250} = 0.7200$$

$$\text{Recall (TPR)} = \frac{180}{180 + 100} = \frac{180}{280} \approx 0.6429$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.7200 \cdot 0.6429}{0.7200 + 0.6429} = \frac{0.9257}{1.3629} \approx 0.6791$$

$$FPR = \frac{70}{70 + 9{,}650} = \frac{70}{9{,}720} \approx 0.00720$$

$$\text{Specificity} = 1 - FPR = 0.99280$$

**Interpretation (model answer snippet):**
- The model has **high accuracy (98.3%)** but this is *misleading* because attacks are < 3% of data.
- **Precision = 72%** is acceptable — 7 of 10 alerts are real.
- **Recall = 64.3%** is **below the 80% SOC industry threshold** — 100 attacks were missed (the most dangerous outcome). The model must be retrained with better recall-boosting features (e.g., behavioural UEBA signals).

## 3.4 Derivation of the Brier Score for Calibration

**Step 1 — Define the Brier Score for $N$ binary outcomes:**

$$BS = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{p}_i - y_i)^2$$

**Step 2 — Decompose into Reliability, Resolution, and Uncertainty (Murphy's decomposition):**

$$BS = \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k (\hat{p}_k - \bar{o}_k)^2}_{\text{Reliability}} - \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k (\bar{o}_k - \bar{o})^2}_{\text{Resolution}} + \underbrace{\bar{o}(1 - \bar{o})}_{\text{Uncertainty}}$$

Where $\hat{p}_k$ = predicted probability in bin $k$, $\bar{o}_k$ = observed frequency in bin $k$, and $\bar{o}$ = base rate.

**Step 3 — Engineering Insight:**
- **Reliability ↓** → better calibrated.
- **Resolution ↑** → predictions are sharper and more discriminating.
- A model with **BS = 0.10** is considered well-calibrated; **BS > 0.25** is poorly calibrated and unfit for risk-based decision-making.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection by Threat-Forecasting Use Case

| Use Case | Recommended Model | Input Modality | Primary Metric | Typical Limitation |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|----------------|--------------------|
| Phishing URL detection | XGBoost / LightGBM on URL lexical features | URL strings | PR-AUC | Adversarial URL obfuscation |
| Malware family classification | CNN on byte n-grams / image of binary | PE files | F1 (macro) | Class imbalance (rare families) |
| Insider-threat detection | LSTM on user behaviour sequences | UEBA logs | Recall | High false positives |
| DDoS volume forecasting | ARIMA / Prophet / Temporal Fusion Transformer | NetFlow time series | RMSE / MAPE | Concept drift on attack patterns |
| Threat-actor attribution | Graph Neural Network on ATT&CK graph | STIX 2.1 bundles | Top-k Accuracy | Sparse attribution labels |
| IoC scoring / risk prioritisation | Logistic Regression / Random Forest | STIX objects | ROC-AUC + Brier | Stale IoCs (decay over time) |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

> **KTU Marking Convention (Part A):** Each question carries **3 marks**. Expected answer length: 80–120 words. Marks are awarded for: (i) precise definition (1), (ii) supporting detail or example (1), (iii) industry relevance or formula (1).

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define predictive analytics in the context of cyber threat forecasting. Differentiate it from descriptive and diagnostic analytics with a one-line example for each.**

**Model Answer:**
Predictive analytics in cyber security is the use of statistical and machine-learning models on historical and real-time threat data to forecast the *likelihood, target, timing, and TTP* of future attacks. It is *proactive* — it answers "what is likely to happen next?"

- **Descriptive analytics** answers *what happened* — e.g., dashboard showing 1,200 phishing emails blocked last month.
- **Diagnostic analytics** answers *why it happened* — e.g., root-cause analysis reveals a misconfigured spam filter.
- **Predictive analytics** answers *what will happen* — e.g., the model forecasts a 73% probability of credential-stuffing attacks against the finance portal within 48 hours.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE evaluation metrics used to assess a predictive threat-detection model. Why is plain accuracy insufficient for imbalanced cyber-security datasets?**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Precision** — Of all alerts raised, how many were real attacks. Important to reduce SOC fatigue.
2. **Recall (TPR)** — Of all real attacks, how many did the model catch. Critical because missed attacks are the most damaging outcome.
3. **F1-Score** — Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, balancing both into a single threshold-independent number.

**Why accuracy fails:** In a typical SOC, attacks represent < 0.1–3% of total events. A naive model that labels *everything as benign* achieves 97–99.9% accuracy yet catches **zero attacks**. Metrics such as **PR-AUC, Recall, and F1** are discriminative on rare-event data and are therefore preferred.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Long Answer with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Part B Marking Convention:** Each question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means the student answers **EITHER Question A OR Question B in full** (both sub-parts). Sub-part (a) is usually a descriptive/theoretical task; sub-part (b) is an analytical/numerical/derivation task.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end pipeline for building a predictive threat-forecasting model**. Cover data acquisition, feature engineering, model selection, training, evaluation, and deployment. Highlight where **MITRE ATT&CK** and **STIX/TAXII** integrate into the pipeline. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The output of a phishing-detection model on **5,000 emails** is summarised below. Compute **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and FPR**. Interpret the results for a SOC manager and recommend ONE improvement. **[7 Marks]**

| Confusion Matrix Cell | Count |
|-----------------------|------:|
| **TP** — Phishing correctly flagged | 320 |
| **FP** — Benign wrongly flagged | 180 |
| **TN** — Benign correctly cleared | 4,300 |
| **FN** — Phishing missed | 200 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) End-to-End Pipeline (7 Marks)**

The pipeline consists of **six interlinked stages**:

1. **Data Acquisition** (1 mark) — Collect from SIEM, EDR, network taps, OSINT, dark-web monitors. Use **STIX 2.1 / TAXII 2.1** as the standardised exchange format so that heterogeneous feeds interoperate.
2. **Feature Engineering** (1 mark) — Convert raw artefacts into model-ready vectors: URL lexical features, TF-IDF over email body, time-window aggregations of NetFlow, behavioural baselines per user. MITRE ATT&CK IDs are encoded as categorical features linking each event to adversary behaviour.
3. **Model Selection & Training** (2 marks) — Choose between XGBoost/LightGBM (tabular IoCs), LSTM/Temporal Fusion Transformer (sequence data), and Graph Neural Networks (TTP attribution). Train using stratified k-fold cross-validation; tune hyperparameters via Bayesian optimisation.
4. **Evaluation** (1 mark) — Compute Precision, Recall, F1, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, Brier Score, and PSI. Inspect calibration curves; reject models with PR-AUC < 0.8 on imbalanced data.
5. **Deployment** (1 mark) — Wrap model in a containerised REST/gRPC service behind a model registry (e.g., MLflow). Push scores to SIEM/SOAR as alert enrichments.
6. **Monitoring & Retraining** (1 mark) — Use PSI and KS tests to detect **concept drift**; trigger retraining when drift > 0.25 or recall drops below threshold. Analyst feedback (true/false labels) closes the loop.

**MITRE ATT&CK** serves as the *semantic backbone* — every collected IoC is tagged with a Technique ID, enabling the model to reason about *behaviour* rather than just signatures. **STIX/TAXII** standardise the exchange so that the pipeline remains vendor-agnostic.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Recall the formulas and substitute:**

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} = \frac{320 + 4{,}300}{5{,}000} = \frac{4{,}620}{5{,}000} = 0.9240$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{320}{320 + 180} = \frac{320}{500} = 0.6400$$

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{320}{320 + 200} = \frac{320}{520} \approx 0.6154$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.6400 \cdot 0.6154}{0.6400 + 0.6154} = \frac{0.7877}{1.2554} \approx 0.6275$$

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{180}{180 + 4{,}300} = \frac{180}{4{,}480} \approx 0.04018$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
- **Accuracy = 92.4%** is high but **misleading** because phishing is only ~10.4% of the dataset.
- **Precision = 64%** means ~36% of the model's alerts are false positives → measurable analyst fatigue.
- **Recall = 61.5%** means **200 phishing emails were missed** — a major breach risk.
- **F1 = 0.63** is below the industry-accepted threshold of 0.80 for production SOC systems.

**Step 3 — Recommended Improvement (1 mark):**
Introduce **behavioural features derived from UEBA** (e.g., sender-domain age, reply-chain anomaly, header-inconsistency score) and apply **SMOTE oversampling** on the minority class to improve recall. Re-tune the decision threshold to maximise F1 on the validation PR-curve.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model — Alternative Choice]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Discuss **any three real-world case studies** of AI-based threat intelligence systems (e.g., IBM Watson for Cyber Security, MITRE ATT&CK + ML pipelines, Darktrace, MISP+ML). For each, describe the data inputs, AI technique, and the forecasting/intelligence value delivered. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A logistic-regression model outputs the following **calibration-bin data**. Compute the **Brier Score** and the **reliability component** (Murphy's first term). Comment on whether the model is well-calibrated enough for risk-based decision-making. **[7 Marks]**

| Bin | Predicted Probability $\hat{p}_k$ | Observed Frequency $\bar{o}_k$ | Bin Count $n_k$ |
|-----|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 0.10 | 0.08 | 400 |
| 2 | 0.30 | 0.25 | 350 |
| 3 | 0.50 | 0.55 | 300 |
| 4 | 0.70 | 0.62 | 250 |
| 5 | 0.90 | 0.85 | 200 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Three Case Studies (7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for synthesis)**

**Case Study 1 — IBM Watson for Cyber Security (2 marks)**
- **Inputs:** Unstructured cyber-security blogs, threat reports, NIST CVE advisories, plus structured SIEM alerts from QRadar.
- **AI Technique:** **Unsupervised NLP** (topic modelling, entity extraction) combined with **cognitive reasoning** over an ontology of threat concepts.
- **Value:** Converts ~15,000+ documents/month into structured, prioritised threat intelligence; surfaces previously unseen adversary relationships that signature-based systems miss.

**Case Study 2 — MITRE ATT&CK + ML Pipelines (2 marks)**
- **Inputs:** Red-team exercise logs (Atomic Red Team, Caldera), EDR telemetry, and STIX 2.1 bundles.
- **AI Technique:** **Multi-label classification** (deep neural networks / XGBoost) mapping low-level events to ATT&CK Technique IDs (T1059, T1486, etc.).
- **Value:** Enables **predictive gap analysis** — predicting which ATT&CK techniques an organisation is *most likely* to face based on its industry, geography, and observed precursor behaviours. Forms the basis of **threat-informed defence**.

**Case Study 3 — MISP + ML Threat Intelligence Platform (2 marks)**
- **Inputs:** Distributed OSINT feeds (AlienVault OTX, AbuseIPDB), internal IoC sightings, dark-web scraping.
- **AI Technique:** **Graph-based clustering** (community detection) and **scoring** (reputation propagation with PageRank-like algorithms).
- **Value:** Predicts which IoCs will *trend* in the next 24–72 hours, allowing pre-emptive firewall/EDR blocklisting.

**Synthesis (1 mark):**
All three systems share a common architectural pattern — *ingest heterogeneous threat data → normalise to a standard schema (STIX/ATT&CK) → apply AI to score/forecast → feed back into defence-in-depth controls*. This is the canonical **predictive threat-intelligence loop**.

---

**(b) Brier Score Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply the Brier formula (full form):**

$$BS = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{5} n_k (\hat{p}_k - \bar{o}_k)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute each bin:**

$$\text{Total samples } N = 400 + 350 + 300 + 250 + 200 = 1{,}500$$

- Bin 1: $400 \cdot (0.10 - 0.08)^2 = 400 \cdot 0.0004 = 0.16$
- Bin 2: $350 \cdot (0.30 - 0.25)^2 = 350 \cdot 0.0025 = 0.875$
- Bin 3: $300 \cdot (0.50 - 0.55)^2 = 300 \cdot 0.0025 = 0.75$
- Bin 4: $250 \cdot (0.70 - 0.62)^2 = 250 \cdot 0.0064 = 1.60$
- Bin 5: $200 \cdot (0.90 - 0.85)^2 = 200 \cdot 0.0025 = 0.50$

**Step 3 — Sum and average:**

$$\sum = 0.16 + 0.875 + 0.75 + 1.60 + 0.50 = 3.885$$

$$BS = \frac{3.885}{1{,}500} = 0.00259$$

**Step 4 — Reliability component** (Murphy's first term, identical calculation when using observed frequencies as the bin estimator):

$$\text{Reliability} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{5} n_k (\hat{p}_k - \bar{o}_k)^2 = 0.00259$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
- **Brier Score = 0.00259** is well below the 0.10 industry threshold → the model is **very well calibrated**.
- All bins show $|\hat{p}_k - \bar{o}_k| \le 0.08$, indicating the predicted probability closely matches the observed base rate.
- The model is **suitable for risk-based decision-making**, e.g., triggering automated SOAR responses when the predicted probability exceeds 0.70.
- **Caveat:** The Brier Score should be tracked over time — a sudden increase (e.g., to 0.05) signals **concept drift** and mandates retraining.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall** — *Precision* asks "of the alerts we raised, how many were real?"; *Recall* asks "of the real attacks, how many did we catch?" Examiners routinely flip these to test conceptual clarity.
> 2. **Quoting "Accuracy = 98%" without acknowledging class imbalance** — This is a near-guaranteed 1–2 mark penalty. Always add: *"However, attacks are < 1% of total events, so accuracy is misleading; F1/PR-AUC are preferred."*
> 3. **Forgetting units / interpretations of metrics** — A numerical answer without an interpretive sentence is treated as incomplete. Always close with a one-line engineering verdict.
> 4. **Mixing up STIX and TAXII** — STIX is the *data model*; TAXII is the *transport protocol*. Examiners award 1 mark for this distinction.
> 5. **Omitting the Brier Score / Calibration discussion** — In 14-mark questions, calibration carries 2–3 marks. Skipping it costs significant marks.
> 6. **No diagram** — A pipeline/block diagram in 7-mark theory answers is worth at least 1.5 marks. Skipping it is a high-risk shortcut.
> 7. **Treating Predictive Analytics as deterministic** — It is **probabilistic**; output is $P(\cdot) \in [0,1]$, not a binary flag. Forgetting this loses conceptual marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Predictive analytics in cyber security** = AI-driven *forecasting* of future threats, distinct from descriptive (past) and diagnostic (why) analytics.
- **Pipeline:** Data Acquisition → Label Curation → Feature Engineering → Model Training → Evaluation → Deployment → Drift Monitoring.
- **STIX 2.1 = data model; TAXII 2.1 = transport; MITRE ATT&CK = behavioural taxonomy** — remember the *trinity* of cyber-threat-intelligence standards.
- **Imbalanced data is the norm** → use **Precision, Recall, F1, PR-AUC, Brier** — *not* accuracy alone.
- **F1 = 2·TP / (2·TP + FP + FN)** is the calculation-efficient form.
- **ROC-AUC** = probability a random malicious sample scores above a random benign one.
- **Brier Score < 0.10** ⇒ well-calibrated model; > 0.25 ⇒ unfit for risk-based decisions.
- **PSI > 0.25** ⇒ major feature drift; retrain the model.
- **NIST Risk formula:** Risk = Likelihood × Impact × Asset Value.
- **Three case-study exemplars:** **IBM Watson for Cyber Security** (NLP on threat reports), **MITRE ATT&CK + ML** (technique classification & gap analysis), **MISP+ML** (IoC scoring & trend prediction).
- **Concept drift is the silent killer** — any model deployed without drift monitoring decays in 3–6 months.
- **Final verdict line in any answer:** Always end numerical/descriptive answers with a **one-sentence engineering recommendation** (retrain / lower threshold / add features / accept model).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behavioral Analysis Techniques: Behavioral Profiling and Pattern Recognition, Analyzing User and System Behavior to Detect Anomalies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT633 - AI FOR CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Behavioral Analysis Techniques: Behavioral Profiling and Pattern Recognition, Analyzing User and System Behavior to Detect Anomalies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behavioral Analysis in Cyber Security** is the systematic application of statistical, probabilistic, and machine-learning methodologies to construct mathematical representations (profiles) of entities (users, processes, network nodes, devices) and subsequently identify deviations from these established normative patterns to detect malicious or unauthorized activity.

**Behavioral Profiling** refers to the process of constructing a quantitative baseline model $\mathcal{P}_i$ for an entity $i$ (user/device/process) by aggregating and statistically characterizing its historical activity vectors $\vec{X}_i = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ across multiple feature dimensions.

**Pattern Recognition** is the automated identification of regularities, signatures, or recurring structures within observed data using classification algorithms, clustering techniques, or rule-based engines that map input vectors to predefined or learned output categories.

**Anomaly Detection** (in this context) refers to the computational identification of observations $\vec{x}$ whose behavioral score $S(\vec{x})$ deviates from the established profile $\mathcal{P}_i$ by a magnitude exceeding a calibrated threshold $\tau$.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly maps these techniques to **User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA)**, a sub-discipline of Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) augmented with AI/ML capabilities.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **security guard posted outside your house for 20 years**:

- The guard knows that **Mr. A typically leaves at 8:00 AM**, returns at 6:00 PM, parks his blue car in the left bay, and unlocks the door with a key (not a code).
- One day, Mr. A attempts to unlock the door at **2:00 AM using a crowbar from the back window**.
- The guard immediately flags this as **suspicious** because it deviates from the learned baseline.

This is precisely how **Behavioral Analysis Techniques** function in cyber security:
- The **guard's memory** = Behavioral Profile ($\mathcal{P}_i$)
- The **observation of Mr. A's habits** = Pattern Recognition Engine
- The **2:00 AM crowbar event** = Anomalous Behavior flagged for investigation

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bolded for Emphasis)

| Term | Standard Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **True Positive Rate (TPR)** | $\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Recall; detection capability |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Alert fatigue metric |
| **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of alerts |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced metric |
| **Anomaly Score** | $S(\vec{x}) \in [0, 1]$ | Deviation magnitude |
| **Z-Score (Standard Score)** | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Statistical distance measure |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Base Rate Fallacy** is critical: in cyber security, the prevalence of malicious events is typically $< 0.01\%$, making accuracy a poor metric. KTU examiners expect familiarity with **precision-recall trade-offs**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Behavioral Analysis Pipeline (Structured Logic)

The end-to-end operational flow consists of **six sequential stages**:

1. **Data Ingestion Layer**
   - *Why:* Raw cyber data is heterogeneous (logs, packets, syscalls, authentication events).
   - *How:* Collect from endpoints, network sensors, authentication servers (e.g., LDAP, Active Directory), and cloud APIs.

2. **Feature Engineering & Vectorization**
   - *Why:* ML models require numerical input; raw events are symbolic.
   - *How:* Transform events into numerical feature vectors using techniques like **Bag-of-Words (BoW)**, **TF-IDF**, or **one-hot encoding** of categorical behaviors.

3. **Profiling / Baseline Construction**
   - *Why:* Establishes "normalcy" for comparison.
   - *How:* Aggregate historical data into a statistical model (mean $\mu$, standard deviation $\sigma$, or density estimate).

4. **Anomaly Scoring**
   - *Why:* Quantifies deviation from baseline.
   - *How:* Apply distance metrics, density estimators, or reconstruction errors.

5. **Thresholding & Classification**
   - *Why:* Converts continuous scores into binary decisions.
   - *How:* Apply $S(\vec{x}) > \tau \Rightarrow$ anomaly label.

6. **Alert Generation & Feedback Loop**
   - *Why:* SOC analysts must validate and the model must adapt.
   - *How:* Analyst labels feed back into the training set (active learning paradigm).

## 2.2 Behavioral Profiling Approaches (Comparative Analysis)

| Approach | Mathematical Basis | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statistical Profiling** | Mean, variance, distributions | Interpretable, low compute | Assumes distribution shape |
| **Clustering-based** (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN) | Distance in feature space | Unsupervised, finds groups | Sensitive to $k$ and $\epsilon$ |
| **Density-based** (e.g., LOF) | Local density ratio | Detects local anomalies | $O(n^2)$ complexity |
| **Classification-based** (e.g., SVM, RF) | Supervised decision boundary | High accuracy | Requires labeled data |
| **Deep Learning** (e.g., Autoencoders, LSTMs) | Neural reconstruction | Captures complex patterns | Black-box, data-hungry |
| **Sequential / Temporal** (e.g., HMM) | Markov state transitions | Models behavior sequences | State-space explosion |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize the form, units, and boundary conditions.

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu$ | $\mu = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Arithmetic mean of feature | Same as $x$ |
| $\sigma^2$ | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2$ | Variance | Squared units |
| $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Standard deviation | Same as $x$ |
| $z_{ij}$ | $z_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$ | Z-score (standardization) | Dimensionless |
| $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$ | $\text{Cov} = \frac{1}{N} \sum (x_i - \mu_X)(y_i - \mu_Y)$ | Covariance | Mixed units |
| $\rho_{XY}$ | $\rho = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$ | Pearson correlation | $[-1, 1]$ |
| $d_E(\vec{x}, \vec{y})$ | $d_E = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Euclidean distance | Same as $x$ |
| $d_M(\vec{x}, \vec{y})$ | $d_M = (\sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p)^{1/p}$ | Minkowski distance (general) | Same as $x$ |
| $\cos\theta$ | $\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{x} \cdot \vec{y}}{\vert\vec{x}\vert \vert\vec{y}\vert}$ | Cosine similarity | $[-1, 1]$ |
| $H(X)$ | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy (bits) | $\geq 0$ |
| $I(X;Y)$ | $I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$ | Mutual information | $\geq 0$ |
| $\text{LOF}(p)$ | $\text{LOF}_k(p) = \frac{\sum_{o \in N_k(p)} \text{lrd}(o)}{\vert N_k(p) \vert \cdot \text{lrd}(p)}$ | Local Outlier Factor | $\approx 1$ normal, $\gg 1$ anomalous |
| $\text{MSE}(\vec{x})$ | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$ | Autoencoder reconstruction error | $\geq 0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for KTU Answer Sheets:** When using $\vert x \vert$ in handwriting, draw vertical bars clearly. Avoid using single pipes `|` in exam answer copies as they can be confused with absolute value vs. conditional probability contexts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Behavioral Technique Used | Industry Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Insider Threat Detection** | User behavior analytics (UBA) | Reduces data exfiltration by ~60% |
| **Account Takeover (ATO)** | Login pattern deviation | Banks save millions in fraud |
| **Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)** | Long-horizon anomaly detection | Detects nation-state attacks |
| **IoT Botnet Detection** | Network traffic behavioral fingerprinting | Mirai, Emotet mitigation |
| **Privileged Access Misuse** | Admin command profiling | PCI-DSS, SOX compliance |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Z-Score Based Behavioral Anomaly Score

### **Setup**

Suppose a user $u$ has a historical login-time dataset (in hours, 24-hour format):
$$T = \{8.2,\ 9.0,\ 8.5,\ 8.8,\ 9.2,\ 8.0,\ 8.3,\ 8.7,\ 9.1,\ 8.4\}$$

A new login event occurs at time $t_{\text{new}} = 3.0$ (3:00 AM). Determine if this is anomalous using Z-score with threshold $\tau_z = 2.5$.

### **Step 1: Compute the Mean $\mu$**

Sum all elements of $T$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{10} t_i = 8.2 + 9.0 + 8.5 + 8.8 + 9.2 + 8.0 + 8.3 + 8.7 + 9.1 + 8.4$$

Adding sequentially:
- $8.2 + 9.0 = 17.2$
- $17.2 + 8.5 = 25.7$
- $25.7 + 8.8 = 34.5$
- $34.5 + 9.2 = 43.7$
- $43.7 + 8.0 = 51.7$
- $51.7 + 8.3 = 60.0$
- $60.0 + 8.7 = 68.7$
- $68.7 + 9.1 = 77.8$
- $77.8 + 8.4 = 86.2$

Therefore:
$$\mu = \frac{86.2}{10} = 8.62 \text{ hours}$$

### **Step 2: Compute Squared Deviations $(t_i - \mu)^2$**

We compute each deviation from the mean $\mu = 8.62$:

| $i$ | $t_i$ | $t_i - \mu$ | $(t_i - \mu)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8.2 | $-0.42$ | $0.1764$ |
| 2 | 9.0 | $0.38$ | $0.1444$ |
| 3 | 8.5 | $-0.12$ | $0.0144$ |
| 4 | 8.8 | $0.18$ | $0.0324$ |
| 5 | 9.2 | $0.58$ | $0.3364$ |
| 6 | 8.0 | $-0.62$ | $0.3844$ |
| 7 | 8.3 | $-0.32$ | $0.1024$ |
| 8 | 8.7 | $0.08$ | $0.0064$ |
| 9 | 9.1 | $0.48$ | $0.2304$ |
| 10 | 8.4 | $-0.22$ | $0.0484$ |

### **Step 3: Sum the Squared Deviations**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{10} (t_i - \mu)^2 = 0.1764 + 0.1444 + 0.0144 + 0.0324 + 0.3364 + 0.3844 + 0.1024 + 0.0064 + 0.2304 + 0.0484$$

Sequential summation:
- $0.1764 + 0.1444 = 0.3208$
- $0.3208 + 0.0144 = 0.3352$
- $0.3352 + 0.0324 = 0.3676$
- $0.3676 + 0.3364 = 0.7040$
- $0.7040 + 0.3844 = 1.0884$
- $1.0884 + 0.1024 = 1.1908$
- $1.1908 + 0.0064 = 1.1972$
- $1.1972 + 0.2304 = 1.4276$
- $1.4276 + 0.0484 = 1.4760$

### **Step 4: Compute Variance and Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_i - \mu)^2 = \frac{1.4760}{10} = 0.1476$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.1476} \approx 0.3842 \text{ hours}$$

### **Step 5: Compute the Z-Score for the New Observation**

The new event $t_{\text{new}} = 3.0$ hours. Applying the Z-score formula:

$$z = \frac{t_{\text{new}} - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{3.0 - 8.62}{0.3842} = \frac{-5.62}{0.3842}$$

$$z \approx -14.63$$

### **Step 6: Decision Using Threshold**

Comparing with $\tau_z = 2.5$:
$$|z| = 14.63 \gg 2.5$$

**Conclusion:** The 3:00 AM login is **anomalous** with extreme confidence, indicating a likely account compromise.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Euclidean Distance Anomaly Detection (Multivariate)

### **Setup**

A user's behavioral feature vector is constructed from three dimensions:
- $f_1$: Files accessed per hour
- $f_2$: Login attempts per session
- $f_3$: Bandwidth consumed (MB/hr)

**Historical baseline vector:** $\vec{\mu} = (50, 3, 200)$

**New observation:** $\vec{x} = (250, 1, 50)$ — possibly a data exfiltration event.

### **Step 1: Compute Element-wise Differences**

$$\vec{x} - \vec{\mu} = (250 - 50,\ 1 - 3,\ 50 - 200)$$

$$= (200,\ -2,\ -150)$$

### **Step 2: Square Each Component**

$$(\vec{x} - \vec{\mu})^2 = (200^2,\ (-2)^2,\ (-150)^2) = (40000,\ 4,\ 22500)$$

### **Step 3: Sum the Squared Differences**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{3} (x_j - \mu_j)^2 = 40000 + 4 + 22500 = 62504$$

### **Step 4: Apply the Square Root**

$$d_E = \sqrt{62504} = 250.008$$

### **Step 5: Decision Rule**

If the threshold $\tau_d = 100$, then:
$$d_E = 250.008 \gg 100 \Rightarrow \text{Anomaly Detected}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Euclidean distance is sensitive to feature scaling. In real deployments, **Min-Max normalization** or **Z-score standardization** is applied first. Otherwise, high-magnitude features (e.g., bandwidth in MB) dominate the distance calculation.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Anomaly Detection Paradigms

| Paradigm | Training Data Required | Output Type | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Supervised** | Labeled normal + attack | Class label | Known malware families |
| **Unsupervised** | Unlabeled data | Anomaly score | Zero-day threat hunting |
| **Semi-Supervised** | Only normal data | Deviation score | UEBA profile baseline |
| **One-Class SVM** | Normal only | Boundary distance | Rare-event detection |
| **Autoencoder** | Normal only | Reconstruction error | Network IDS |
| **Isolation Forest** | Unlabeled | Path-length score | High-dimensional logs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Behavioral Analysis Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Event Sources] --> B[Data Ingestion Layer]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Profile Baseline Construction]
    D --> E[Anomaly Scoring Engine]
    E --> F{Threshold Check}
    F -->|Score exceeds threshold| G[Alert Generation]
    F -->|Score within baseline| H[Normal Activity Log]
    G --> I[SOC Analyst Review]
    I --> J[Feedback Loop]
    J --> C
    H --> K[Continuous Monitoring]
    K --> C

    classDef input fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff

    class A,B input
    class C,D,E process
    class F decision
    class G,H,I,J,K output
```

## 4.2 Anomaly Detection Methodology Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root[Anomaly Detection Methods] --> Stat[Statistical Methods]
    Root --> ML[Machine Learning Methods]
    Root --> DL[Deep Learning Methods]
    Root --> Dist[Distance Based Methods]

    Stat --> S1[Z-Score Test]
    Stat --> S2[Grubbs Test]
    Stat --> S3[Chi-Square Test]
    Stat --> S4[Gaussian Mixture Models]

    ML --> M1[Supervised Classifiers]
    ML --> M2[Unsupervised Clustering]
    ML --> M3[Semi-Supervised Models]
    ML --> M4[Isolation Forest]

    DL --> D1[Autoencoders]
    DL --> D2[Variational Autoencoders]
    DL --> D3[LSTM Networks]
    DL --> D4[GAN Based Detectors]

    Dist --> DT1[K Nearest Neighbors]
    Dist --> DT2[Local Outlier Factor]
    Dist --> DT3[DBSCAN Clustering]
    Dist --> DT4[Mahalanobis Distance]

    classDef rootnode fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef catnode fill:#1e40af,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef leafnode fill:#15803d,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff

    class Root rootnode
    class Stat,ML,DL,Dist catnode
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,M1,M2,M3,M4,D1,D2,D3,D4,DT1,DT2,DT3,DT4 leafnode
```

## 4.3 UEBA Detection Workflow (User and Entity Behavior Analytics)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Activity
    participant C as Data Collector
    participant F as Feature Extractor
    participant P as Profile Database
    participant S as Scoring Engine
    participant T as Threshold Comparator
    participant SOC as SOC Analyst

    U->>C: Login event, file access, network call
    C->>F: Stream raw event
    F->>F: Compute behavioral features
    F->>P: Retrieve user baseline profile
    P-->>S: Return baseline vector
    F->>S: Pass current feature vector
    S->>S: Compute deviation score
    S->>T: Submit anomaly score
    T->>T: Compare with threshold
    alt Score exceeds threshold
        T->>SOC: Trigger high priority alert
        SOC->>SOC: Investigate and label
        SOC-->>P: Update profile with feedback
    else Score within baseline
        T->>P: Update profile incrementally
    end
```

## 4.4 Feature Engineering Process (From Raw Events to Behavioral Vectors)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Authentication Logs] --> B[Login Frequency]
    A --> C[Geolocation Features]
    A --> D[Time of Day Patterns]

    E[File System Events] --> F[Access Volume]
    E --> G[File Type Diversity]
    E --> H[Sensitive File Flags]

    I[Network Traffic] --> J[Bandwidth Usage]
    I --> K[Destination Diversity]
    I --> L[Protocol Distribution]

    B --> M[Normalized Feature Vector]
    C --> M
    D --> M
    F --> M
    G --> M
    H --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M

    M --> N[Behavioral Profile Model]

    classDef src fill:#312e81,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef feat fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef vec fill:#854d0e,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef model fill:#9f1239,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff

    class A,E,I src
    class B,C,D,F,G,H,J,K,L feat
    class M vec
    class N model
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define behavioral profiling in the context of AI for cyber security. List any four features used to construct a user behavioral profile.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Behavioral profiling is the computational process of constructing a quantitative baseline model that represents the normal activity patterns of a user, device, or system entity by aggregating and statistically characterizing historical behavioral data.

**Four Features (2 Marks - 0.5 each):**
1. **Login Time Patterns:** Typical hours of authentication activity.
2. **Geolocation Vector:** Set of IP geolocations frequently used.
3. **File Access Volume:** Average number of files accessed per session.
4. **Network Bandwidth Profile:** Mean and variance of data transferred per hour.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between signature-based detection and behavioral anomaly detection. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Signature-Based | Behavioral Anomaly |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Basis | Known pattern matching | Deviation from learned baseline |
| Zero-Day Capability | None | Capable of detection |
| False Positive Rate | Low | Moderate to high |

**Advantage of Signature-Based (1 Mark):** Extremely low false positive rate for known threats; high precision.

**Advantage of Behavioral Anomaly (1 Mark):** Can detect previously unknown (zero-day) attacks and insider threats.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern: Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is worth 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2 Choice A]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) system. Discuss the role of feature engineering in behavioral profiling with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**UEBA Architecture (3.5 Marks):**

A UEBA system operates in **four interconnected layers**:

1. **Data Collection Layer:** Aggregates logs from authentication servers (LDAP, Active Directory), endpoints (EDR), network sensors, and cloud services.

2. **Analytics Layer:** Applies statistical and machine learning algorithms to construct behavioral profiles and compute anomaly scores.

3. **Detection Layer:** Compares current activity scores against adaptive thresholds to classify events as normal or anomalous.

4. **Response Layer:** Triggers alerts, integrates with SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation and Response), and provides dashboards for SOC analysts.

**Role of Feature Engineering (3.5 Marks):**

Feature engineering transforms raw events into numerical representations suitable for ML models. Examples:

- **Temporal Features:** Hour of day, day of week, time since last login.
- **Frequency Features:** Number of login attempts, files accessed, commands executed.
- **Categorical Features:** Resource type, geographic region, device fingerprint.
- **Sequential Features:** Order of operations, command sequences.

**Engineering Insight:** Poor feature engineering results in the "garbage in, garbage out" phenomenon. In UEBA, features must capture both **point anomalies** (single unusual events) and **contextual anomalies** (normal events in abnormal contexts, e.g., a legitimate login at 3:00 AM).

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, derive the mathematical formulation of Z-score based anomaly detection. A user has historical login counts per day: {12, 15, 14, 13, 16, 14, 15, 13, 14, 15}. A new day records 45 login attempts. Using threshold $\tau = 2.5$, determine if this is anomalous. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mathematical Formulation (3 Marks):**

The Z-score measures the number of standard deviations an observation lies from the mean:

$$z = \frac{x_{\text{new}} - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Where:
- $\mu = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ (sample mean)
- $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2}$ (sample standard deviation)
- Decision rule: Anomaly if $|z| > \tau$.

**Numerical Computation (4 Marks):**

*Step 1:* Compute mean:
$$\mu = \frac{12+15+14+13+16+14+15+13+14+15}{10} = \frac{141}{10} = 14.1$$

*Step 2:* Compute squared deviations:

| $x_i$ | $x_i - 14.1$ | $(x_i - 14.1)^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 12 | $-2.1$ | $4.41$ |
| 15 | $0.9$ | $0.81$ |
| 14 | $-0.1$ | $0.01$ |
| 13 | $-1.1$ | $1.21$ |
| 16 | $1.9$ | $3.61$ |
| 14 | $-0.1$ | $0.01$ |
| 15 | $0.9$ | $0.81$ |
| 13 | $-1.1$ | $1.21$ |
| 14 | $-0.1$ | $0.01$ |
| 15 | $0.9$ | $0.81$ |

Sum: $4.41 + 0.81 + 0.01 + 1.21 + 3.61 + 0.01 + 0.81 + 1.21 + 0.01 + 0.81 = 12.90$

*Step 3:* Compute standard deviation:
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{12.90}{10}} = \sqrt{1.29} \approx 1.136$$

*Step 4:* Compute Z-score:
$$z = \frac{45 - 14.1}{1.136} = \frac{30.9}{1.136} \approx 27.20$$

*Step 5:* Decision:
$$|z| = 27.20 \gg 2.5 \Rightarrow \textbf{Anomalous}$$

**Conclusion:** The 45-login day is a clear anomaly, likely indicating credential brute-force or automated attack.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks - Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2 Choice B]`

**(a)** Discuss pattern recognition techniques used in cyber security. Compare supervised, unsupervised, and semi-supervised approaches with one example algorithm for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pattern Recognition Overview (2 Marks):**

Pattern recognition in cyber security refers to the automated identification of regularities in observed data to classify behaviors, detect threats, and predict attacks. It leverages statistical learning theory and computational intelligence.

**Comparison Table (3 Marks - 1 each):**

| Approach | Data Requirement | Example Algorithm | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supervised | Labeled normal and attack | Random Forest | Malware classification |
| Unsupervised | Unlabeled | K-Means clustering | Zero-day anomaly discovery |
| Semi-Supervised | Only normal samples | One-Class SVM | Insider threat profiling |

**Detailed Examples (2 Marks):**

- **Supervised - Random Forest:** Ensemble of decision trees trained on labeled features (e.g., file entropy, API calls). Outputs majority-vote class label.

- **Unsupervised - K-Means:** Partitions data into $k$ clusters; outliers (data points far from centroids) are flagged as anomalies.

- **Semi-Supervised - One-Class SVM:** Learns a decision boundary encompassing normal data; new points outside the boundary are anomalies.

---

**(b)** Explain Local Outlier Factor (LOF) algorithm. How does it differ from global distance-based methods in detecting anomalies? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**LOF Algorithm Steps (4 Marks):**

The LOF algorithm quantifies how isolated a point is **with respect to its local neighborhood**. It proceeds as follows:

1. **Step 1 — K-Distance:** For each point $p$, compute the distance to its $k$-th nearest neighbor, denoted $d_k(p)$.

2. **Step 2 — K-Nearest Neighbors:** Define the set $N_k(p)$ as the $k$ points within distance $d_k(p)$ of $p$.

3. **Step 3 — Reachability Distance:**
$$\text{rd}_k(p, o) = \max(d_k(o), d(p, o))$$

4. **Step 4 — Local Reachability Density (LRD):**
$$\text{lrd}_k(p) = \frac{\vert N_k(p) \vert}{\sum_{o \in N_k(p)} \text{rd}_k(p, o)}$$

5. **Step 5 — Local Outlier Factor:**
$$\text{LOF}_k(p) = \frac{\sum_{o \in N_k(p)} \frac{\text{lrd}_k(o)}{\text{lrd}_k(p)}}{\vert N_k(p) \vert}$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{LOF} \approx 1$ indicates a normal point (density matches neighbors). $\text{LOF} \gg 1$ indicates an outlier (lower density than neighbors).

**Difference from Global Methods (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Global Methods (e.g., KNN-distance) | Local Methods (LOF) |
|---|---|---|
| Density Assumption | Uniform across dataset | Variable; local context |
| Detects | Global outliers only | Both global and local outliers |
| Sensitivity | Misses local anomalies in dense regions | Captures subtle contextual anomalies |
| Example | Point at distance 10 in dense cluster is missed | LOF detects it as low-density relative to neighbors |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Confusing Z-score with Euclidean distance** — Z-score is univariate; Euclidean distance is multivariate. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks if mixed up.
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize features** before computing distances — In multi-dimensional profiling, unscaled features bias the distance calculation. Always mention **Min-Max scaling** or **Z-score standardization** as a prerequisite.
> 3. **Misinterpreting LOF values** — Students often claim "LOF > 1 means anomaly." The correct interpretation is "LOF $\gg 1$ (significantly greater than 1) indicates anomaly." A point with LOF = 1.1 is still normal.
> 4. **Omitting threshold discussion** — Every anomaly detection method requires a threshold $\tau$. Examiners expect explicit decision rules like $S(\vec{x}) > \tau \Rightarrow$ anomaly.
> 5. **Ignoring the base rate fallacy** — In KTU answers involving metrics, do not quote "accuracy" alone. Always discuss **precision, recall, and F1-score** for imbalanced cyber datasets.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Behavioral Profiling** constructs a statistical baseline ($\mu, \sigma$, or density) of normal activity.
- ✅ **Pattern Recognition** identifies regularities using classification, clustering, or rule-based engines.
- ✅ **Anomaly Detection** flags observations whose deviation score exceeds a calibrated threshold.
- ✅ **Z-score formula:** $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ — univariate, requires Gaussian assumption.
- ✅ **Euclidean distance:** $d_E = \sqrt{\sum (x_j - \mu_j)^2}$ — multivariate, sensitive to feature scaling.
- ✅ **Always normalize features** (Min-Max or Z-score) before computing distances.
- ✅ **LOF (Local Outlier Factor)** detects **local** anomalies; LOF $\approx 1$ = normal, LOF $\gg 1$ = outlier.
- ✅ **One-Class SVM** is semi-supervised and ideal when only normal data is available.
- ✅ **Autoencoders** use reconstruction error as anomaly score: high MSE = anomalous.
- ✅ **UEBA** = User and Entity Behavior Analytics; integrates with SIEM/SOAR pipelines.
- ✅ **Base rate fallacy:** accuracy is misleading for imbalanced cyber data; prefer **F1-score**.
- ✅ **Six-stage pipeline:** Ingestion → Feature Engineering → Profiling → Scoring → Thresholding → Feedback.
- ✅ **Insider threats** are best detected by long-horizon behavioral analysis, not signature matching.
- ✅ **Zero-day attacks** can only be detected by behavioral methods, never by pure signatures.
- ✅ **Mutual information** $I(X;Y)$ measures non-linear feature relevance for behavior selection.
- ✅ **Shannon entropy** $H(X)$ quantifies randomness; entropy drops indicate encrypted exfiltration.
- ✅ **Pearson correlation** $\rho \in [-1, 1]$ measures linear feature dependency in profiles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Exam Tip:** When answering theory questions, always structure your response as **Definition → Mathematical Formulation → Example → Limitation → Real-world Application**. This is the exact valuation pattern followed by KTU board examiners under the Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework mapped to Course Outcomes (CO2 and CO3 for this module).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building AI Security Solutions: Designing Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) - IDS Types (Network-Based vs. Host-Based), Integrating AI Techniques for Enhanced Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT633 - AI FOR CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Building AI Security Solutions: Designing Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) - IDS Types (Network-Based vs. Host-Based), Integrating AI Techniques for Enhanced Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism—software, hardware, or a hybrid combination—that monitors network traffic, system activities, or both, to identify malicious activities, policy violations, or anomalous behaviours that threaten the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of an information system. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, an IDS is positioned as a *passive* defensive control that observes and alerts, distinct from an *active* Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) which blocks threats in real-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024):** Per the PECAT633 Module 3 syllabus, IDS design forms the cornerstone of AI-augmented cyber defence. The two principal architectural classifications are **Network-Based IDS (NIDS)** and **Host-Based IDS (HIDS)**, each with distinct monitoring scopes, deployment topologies, and AI integration strategies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Security Model

Imagine a corporate office building with two layers of security:

| Analogy Element | Real-World Cyber Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **CCTV cameras at the building perimeter** watching everyone who walks in | **Network-Based IDS (NIDS)** |
| **Security guard inside the CEO's office** watching the executive's desk, drawers, and computer | **Host-Based IDS (HIDS)** |
| **AI smart-alert system** that recognizes suspicious patterns in the camera feed (loitering, masked faces) | **AI/ML-based Anomaly Detection Engine** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** A *Network-Based IDS* is like a security camera watching the **roads leading to your house** (it sees the traffic on the network). A *Host-Based IDS* is like a **motion sensor inside your house** (it watches what happens on a specific device). AI integration gives both the ability to *learn* what "normal" looks like and flag anything that deviates—a far cry from rigid, signature-based rules.

## 1.3 The CIA Triad in IDS Context

The operational purpose of any IDS is to safeguard the three pillars of information security:

- **Confidentiality** → Detect exfiltration attempts (data leaving the network).
- **Integrity** → Detect tampering of system files, configuration drift, or unauthorized privilege escalation.
- **Availability** → Detect Denial-of-Service (DoS) and Distributed DoS (DDoS) patterns that threaten uptime.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** Whenever an IDS question appears, always tie the answer back to the **CIA Triad** and the **detection philosophy** (Signature-based vs Anomaly-based). Examiners reward this scaffolding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Classification of IDS

### 2.1.1 Network-Based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)

A **NIDS** is deployed at strategic chokepoints within a network—typically at the perimeter, behind the firewall, or at ingress/egress routers—to inspect **packet-level traffic flows** against a database of known attack signatures or behavioural baselines.

**Operational Workflow (Why & How):**

- **Step 1 — Tap / Span Port Collection:** The NIDS receives a mirror copy of all traffic via a Switched Port Analyzer (SPAN) or network TAP. *Why?* Passive monitoring ensures the IDS itself does not become a single point of failure.
- **Step 2 — Packet Capture & Decoding:** Raw Ethernet frames are decoded into the **TCP/IP protocol stack** (L2 → L3 → L4 → L7). *How?* Libraries like libpcap (theoretical reference) parse headers, flags, payloads.
- **Step 3 — Feature Extraction:** Key features are computed per packet/flow — e.g., *packet size, inter-arrival time, TCP flag combinations, payload entropy*.
- **Step 4 — Detection Engine:** The feature vector is fed into either (a) a **Signature-Based Matcher** (Snort-style rule matching) or (b) an **AI/ML Classifier** (Random Forest, Deep Neural Network).
- **Step 5 — Alerting & Logging:** Suspicious flows trigger alerts with severity ratings (Low / Medium / High / Critical) and are forwarded to a **SIEM** (Security Information and Event Management) console.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** NIDS struggles with **encrypted traffic** (TLS 1.3, HTTPS) because payloads are opaque. Modern AI-based NIDS compensate via **flow-based metadata analysis** (packet sizes, timing, cipher suites) without decrypting payloads.

### 2.1.2 Host-Based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)

A **HIDS** is installed on an individual endpoint (server, workstation, IoT device) and monitors **local system artefacts** — log files, system calls, file system integrity, registry changes, and process behaviour.

**Operational Workflow (Why & How):**

- **Step 1 — Agent Deployment:** A lightweight agent (e.g., OSSEC, Wazuh agent) is installed on the host. *Why?* Visibility into host-internal events is required since NIDS cannot see inside encrypted tunnels.
- **Step 2 — Log Aggregation:** The agent collects logs from OS, applications, and security tools (Windows Event Logs, syslog, auth logs).
- **Step 3 — File Integrity Monitoring (FIM):** Cryptographic hashes (MD5/SHA-256) of critical files are compared against a baseline to detect tampering.
- **Step 4 — System Call Analysis:** Sequences of system calls (e.g., `open → read → write → exec`) are modelled by **Hidden Markov Models** or **LSTMs** to flag malicious process behaviour.
- **Step 5 — Local Response:** HIDS can quarantine files, kill processes, or roll back registry changes — bridging the IDS-to-IPS gap on the host itself.

## 2.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — NIDS vs HIDS

| Parameter | **NIDS** | **HIDS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Monitoring Scope** | Entire network segment / subnet traffic | Single host: logs, files, processes, syscalls |
| **Deployment Location** | Chokepoint (firewall, router, SPAN port) | On the protected endpoint itself |
| **Data Source** | Network packets, flow records (NetFlow, sFlow) | OS logs, audit trails, file hashes, syscalls |
| **Encrypted Traffic Handling** | Poor (payload invisible) | Good (host can decrypt before inspection) |
| **Performance Impact** | Minimal on monitored hosts | Moderate — consumes host CPU/RAM/disk I/O |
| **Detection of Internal Threats** | Weak — internal lateral movement may evade perimeter NIDS | Strong — sees insider activity, privilege abuse |
| **Scalability** | High — one sensor covers many hosts | Low — one agent per host (linear cost) |
| **Evasion Resistance** | Vulnerable to fragmentation, flooding, encryption | Robust — operates below network layer |
| **AI Model Typical Input** | Flow features (packet sizes, intervals, flags) | Sequence features (syscall traces, log token embeddings) |
| **Failure Mode** | Single sensor down → blind spot | Agent down → host becomes unprotected |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"Compare NIDS and HIDS"*, the above matrix is the **gold-standard answer skeleton**. Examiners specifically look for *monitoring scope*, *encryption handling*, and *failure mode* — these three are the highest-scoring differentiators.

## 2.3 AI Integration for Enhanced Detection — The Three Paradigms

### 2.3.1 Supervised Learning (Signature + Labelled Anomaly)

- **Training Data:** Labelled datasets such as **NSL-KDD**, **CICIDS2017**, **UNSW-NB15**.
- **Algorithms:** Decision Trees, Random Forest, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Deep Neural Networks (DNN), Convolutional Neural Networks (1D-CNN for raw packet bytes).
- **Use Case:** Known attack family classification (DoS, Probe, R2L, U2R).
- **Limitation:** Cannot detect **zero-day attacks** outside the training distribution.

### 2.3.2 Unsupervised / Semi-Supervised Learning (Anomaly Detection)

- **Training Data:** Only *normal* traffic patterns (semi-supervised) or unlabelled data (unsupervised).
- **Algorithms:** Autoencoders, Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, **Variational Autoencoders (VAE)**, **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**.
- **Use Case:** Detecting novel, previously unseen attacks — the **zero-day** use case.
- **Limitation:** Higher False Positive Rate (FPR); requires careful threshold tuning.

### 2.3.3 Reinforcement Learning (Adaptive Response)

- **Training Paradigm:** An *agent* interacts with the network environment, taking *actions* (block, alert, allow) and receiving *rewards* (true positive bonus, false positive penalty).
- **Algorithms:** Q-Learning, Deep Q-Network (DQN), Policy Gradient methods.
- **Use Case:** Adaptive IDS that evolves its detection policy in response to attacker counter-adaptation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AI over Traditional Signatures?** Traditional signature-based IDS (Snort rules) suffers from the **signature-writing bottleneck** — human analysts must manually craft rules for every new threat. AI automates feature learning, scales to **millions of features**, and detects **subtle, low-and-slow attacks** that evade human-written rules.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula, metric, and boundary condition that has appeared in KTU ESE and is statistically likely to appear in the December 2025 / May 2026 examination cycle for PECAT633.

| **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Meaning / Engineering Use** | **Unit / Range** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $TPR$ (Recall, Sensitivity) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True Positive Rate — fraction of actual attacks correctly flagged | $\left[ 0, 1 \right]$ |
| $FPR$ (False Alarm Rate) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Fraction of benign traffic incorrectly flagged | $\left[ 0, 1 \right]$ |
| $FNR$ (Miss Rate) | $FNR = 1 - TPR$ | Fraction of attacks that slip through undetected | $\left[ 0, 1 \right]$ |
| $Precision$ | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alerts raised, how many were real attacks | $\left[ 0, 1 \right]$ |
| $F_1$ Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — single-number IDS quality metric | $\left[ 0, 1 \right]$ |
| $Accuracy$ | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness — misleading on imbalanced datasets | $\left[ 0, 1 \right]$ |
| $MCC$ (Matthews Correlation Coeff.) | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Robust to class imbalance — preferred for IDS evaluation | $\left[ -1, 1 \right]$ |
| $ROC\text{-}AUC$ | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | Threshold-independent classifier quality | $\left[ 0, 1 \right]$, ideal = 1 |
| Entropy $H(X)$ | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Payload entropy — high entropy ⇒ encryption or packed malware | bits, $\left[ 0, \log_2 n \right]$ |
| Bayesian Posterior | $P(C \mid X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$ | Probability of attack class $C$ given observation $X$ | $\left[ 0, 1 \right]$ |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Training objective for DNN-based IDS classifier | nats / bits |
| Detection Latency $L_d$ | $L_d = T_{alert} - T_{attack}$ | Time gap between attack launch and IDS alert | seconds / ms |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse $FPR$ with $FNR$. Remember: $FPR$ is about **benign traffic wrongly flagged** (false alarm), while $FNR$ is about **attacks wrongly missed** (miss rate). The complementarity $FNR = 1 - TPR$ only holds for binary cases — verify problem statements.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the F1-Score is the Harmonic Mean (and not Arithmetic)

The $F_1$ score is defined as the harmonic mean of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R$):

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

We wish to find a *single metric* that:
- Reaches its **maximum** ($\text{max} = 1$) **only when** $P = 1$ **and** $R = 1$.
- Penalizes a low value of $P$ **or** $R$ heavily (an IDS that misses 10% of attacks should be heavily penalized, not averaged away).

**Step 1 — The Arithmetic Mean is Wrong:** If $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.1$, the arithmetic mean gives $0.55$, which suggests a *mediocre* classifier. But an IDS that catches only 10% of attacks is **catastrophically bad** — arithmetic mean is too forgiving.

**Step 2 — The Harmonic Mean is Correct:** Recall that the harmonic mean $H$ of two positive reals is

$$H(a, b) = \frac{2 a b}{a + b}$$

**Step 3 — Application to IDS:** Substituting $a = P$ and $b = R$:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Verification:**

- If $P = R = 1$: $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1 \cdot 1}{1 + 1} = 1$ ✓
- If $P = 1, R = 0.1$: $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.1}{1 + 0.1} \approx 0.18$ ✓ (heavy penalty)
- If $P = 0.5, R = 0.5$: $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.25}{1.0} = 0.5$

**Engineering Insight:** The harmonic mean is *always* less than or equal to the arithmetic mean, and it converges to 0 whenever *either* input approaches 0. This is exactly the desired "all-or-nothing" behaviour for security-critical IDS evaluation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) for Imbalanced IDS Datasets

IDS datasets are notoriously imbalanced — in the real world, **benign traffic vastly outnumbers malicious traffic** (often by ratios of 1000:1 or more). The plain accuracy metric becomes meaningless: a trivial classifier predicting "benign" for *all* traffic achieves 99.9% accuracy while detecting zero attacks.

**Step 1 — Start with the Pearson Correlation Idea:** Matthews proposed treating the confusion matrix entries as a 2×2 binary correlation problem. The formula is:

$$MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$$

**Step 2 — Show that MCC = +1 for Perfect Classification:**

Suppose $TP = 10, TN = 990, FP = 0, FN = 0$.

- Numerator: $10 \cdot 990 - 0 \cdot 0 = 9900$.
- Denominator: $\sqrt{(10+0)(10+0)(990+0)(990+0)} = \sqrt{100 \cdot 100 \cdot 980100} = 10 \cdot 10 \cdot 990 = 99000$.

$$MCC = \frac{9900}{99000} = +0.10$$

**Step 3 — Re-evaluate with a Better Classifier:** Suppose $TP = 8, TN = 990, FP = 0, FN = 2$.

- Numerator: $8 \cdot 990 - 0 \cdot 2 = 7920$.
- Denominator: $\sqrt{(8)(10)(990)(992)} = \sqrt{78{,}566{,}400} \approx 8863.0$.

$$MCC \approx \frac{7920}{8863} \approx 0.894$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** MCC is bounded in $\left[ -1, +1 \right]$, with $+1$ being perfect prediction, $0$ being random, and $-1$ being total disagreement. Its symmetry across all four confusion matrix cells makes it **the gold standard for IDS benchmark reporting** in academic literature.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bayesian IDS Decision Rule

The optimal Bayes classifier for IDS minimizes the **expected misclassification cost**. For a binary attack ($A$) vs benign ($B$) decision:

$$\text{Decide } A \text{ if } P(A \mid X) > P(B \mid X)$$

Applying Bayes' theorem:

$$P(A \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(X)}, \quad P(B \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid B) \cdot P(B)}{P(X)}$$

**Step 1 — Form the Decision Boundary:** Decide $A$ if

$$\frac{P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(X \mid B) \cdot P(B)} > 1$$

**Step 2 — Define the Likelihood Ratio $\Lambda(X)$:**

$$\Lambda(X) = \frac{P(X \mid A)}{P(X \mid B)} > \frac{P(B)}{P(A)} = \eta$$

**Step 3 — The Neyman-Pearson IDS Criterion:** Since IDS datasets are imbalanced, $P(A) \ll P(B)$, making the threshold $\eta$ very large. In practice, IDS engineers set a *target* $FPR$ (e.g., 1%) and find the threshold $t$ such that:

$$FPR(t) = P(\Lambda(X) > t \mid B) \leq 0.01$$

**Engineering Utility:** This derivation explains why **threshold tuning** is the single most important hyperparameter in production IDS deployments — the *prior* $P(A)$ is tiny, and small threshold shifts cause large FPR swings.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — AI Algorithm Selection for IDS

| **Algorithm** | **IDS Type Fit** | **Strength** | **Weakness** | **Real-World Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Decision Tree (C4.5) | NIDS & HIDS | Interpretable, fast | Lower accuracy on complex patterns | Snort plugin prefiltering |
| Random Forest | NIDS | Robust to noise, parallelizable | Memory-intensive | CICIDS2017 benchmark winners |
| SVM (RBF kernel) | HIDS | Excellent on small, high-dim data | Slow on large datasets | System call classification |
| 1D-CNN | NIDS | Auto-learns from raw packet bytes | Needs GPU, large training set | Kitsune (Mirsky et al.) |
| LSTM / GRU | HIDS | Captures sequential syscall patterns | Computationally heavy | Syscall-based malware detection |
| Autoencoder | Both | Unsupervised anomaly detection | Threshold sensitivity | Anomaly detection in IoT |
| Isolation Forest | NIDS | Linear-time, no labelled data | Less precise on complex attacks | Flow-based outlier detection |
| DQN (RL) | NIDS | Adaptive to attacker strategy | Unstable training | Adversarial IDS research |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — IDS Performance Calculation

**Problem Statement:** An AI-based NIDS is evaluated on 10,000 network flows. The confusion matrix is:

- $TP = 180$, $FP = 20$, $TN = 9700$, $FN = 100$.

**Step 1 — Compute TPR (Recall):**

$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 100} = \frac{180}{280} \approx 0.643$$

**Step 2 — Compute FPR:**

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{20}{20 + 9700} = \frac{20}{9720} \approx 0.00206$$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision:**

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

**Step 4 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.90 \cdot 0.643}{0.90 + 0.643} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.579}{1.543} \approx 0.750$$

**Step 5 — Engineer’s Verdict:** With $TPR = 64.3\%$, the IDS misses ~36% of attacks — **unacceptable for production**. With $FPR = 0.2\%$, false alarms are well-controlled. The bottleneck is **recall**, suggesting the model needs more attack-class training samples or a lower decision threshold.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — NIDS with AI Detection Engine

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the *functional flow* of an AI-augmented Network-Based IDS, from raw packet capture at the network TAP to the final SOC analyst alert.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    netA[Internet or Upstream Router] -->|Raw Traffic| tapA[Network TAP or SPAN Port]
    tapA -->|Mirror Copy| captA[Packet Capture Module]
    captA -->|Decoded Packets| featA[Feature Extraction Engine]
    featA -->|Feature Vector| aiCore[AI Detection Engine]
    aiCore --> sigPath[Signature Matching Path]
    aiCore --> mlPath[Machine Learning Classifier]
    aiCore --> anomPath[Anomaly Detection Module]
    sigPath --> decA[Decision Fusion Layer]
    mlPath --> decA
    anomPath --> decA
    decA -->|Suspicious Flow| alertA[Alert Generation and Prioritization]
    alertA --> siemA[SIEM and SOC Dashboard]
    alertA -->|High Severity| respA[Automated Response or IPS Trigger]
    siemA -->|Analyst Feedback| aiCore
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The central node `aiCore` (AI Detection Engine) consumes the feature vector and runs it through three parallel detection paths — signature matching, supervised ML classification, and unsupervised anomaly detection. Their verdicts are fused in the **Decision Fusion Layer** (`decA`), which uses weighted voting or stacking to produce the final alert.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — HIDS with AI System-Call Analysis

The second diagram illustrates a Host-Based IDS that uses an **LSTM-based sequence model** to detect malicious process behaviour from system-call traces.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hostA[Protected Host Endpoint] --> logA[Log Collector Agent]
    hostA --> syscallA[System Call Tracer]
    hostA --> fimA[File Integrity Monitor]
    logA --> procA[Preprocessing and Tokenization]
    syscallA --> procA
    fimA --> procA
    procA --> seqA[Sequential Feature Vector]
    seqA --> lstmCore[LSTM Anomaly Detector]
    lstmCore --> scoreA[Anomaly Score s in 0 to 1]
    scoreA -->|s above theta| alertB[Local Alert and Quarantine]
    scoreA -->|s below theta| allowA[Mark as Normal and Log]
    alertB --> centralSIEM[Central SIEM Correlation]
    allowA --> centralSIEM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Three on-host data sources — `logA` (OS logs), `syscallA` (system calls), `fimA` (file integrity) — feed into a shared `procA` preprocessing pipeline. The LSTM (`lstmCore`) outputs a continuous **anomaly score**. A threshold $\theta$ (theta) governs the binary decision to alert or allow.

## 4.3 Comparative Flow — NIDS vs HIDS Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sg1 [NIDS Pipeline]
        n1[Network TAP] --> n2[Packet Capture]
        n2 --> n3[Flow Feature Extraction]
        n3 --> n4[AI Classifier]
        n4 --> n5[Network-Wide Alert]
    end
    subgraph sg2 [HIDS Pipeline]
        h1[Host Agent] --> h2[Log and Syscall Collection]
        h2 --> h3[Sequence Feature Extraction]
        h3 --> h4[AI Anomaly Detector]
        h4 --> h5[Host-Local Alert]
    end
    n5 --> corr[SIEM Correlation and Fusion]
    h5 --> corr
    corr --> soc[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** Notice the **convergence point** at `corr` (SIEM Correlation). Modern enterprise deployments *do not choose* between NIDS and HIDS — they deploy **both** and correlate the alerts in a SIEM to reduce alert fatigue and improve attack-context reconstruction. This is a high-yield KTU concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Mark Scheme Reminder:** Part A questions test **Remember / Understand** (Bloom Levels L1–L2). Keep answers between 80–120 words, define the term first, then add a 1–2 line real-world example.

### Q1. Define Intrusion Detection System (IDS). Differentiate between passive and active IDS.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism that monitors network or host activity for malicious behaviour or policy violations, and raises alerts when such activity is detected.

- A **Passive IDS** (e.g., Snort) only *observes and alerts*; it does not interfere with the traffic.
- An **Active IDS** (often called an IPS — Intrusion Prevention System) *blocks* or *modifies* malicious traffic in real time.

**Example:** A NIDS deployed on a SPAN port is passive; an inline IPS that drops packets matching a signature is active.

---

### Q2. List any three advantages of AI-based IDS over signature-based IDS.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Zero-Day Detection:** AI/ML models, especially unsupervised anomaly detectors, can flag previously unseen attacks because they learn *deviations from normal*, not *matches to known patterns*.
2. **Reduced Signature Maintenance:** Eliminates the manual burden of writing and updating thousands of Snort-style rules.
3. **Adaptive Learning:** Models can be retrained or fine-tuned on emerging threat intelligence, allowing the IDS to evolve with the threat landscape.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B within the module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With suitable diagrams, explain the architecture and working of a Network-Based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS). Discuss how AI techniques enhance its detection capability.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (4 marks):** A NIDS is deployed at strategic network chokepoints. Its architecture consists of:

1. **Traffic Acquisition Module** — Network TAP or SPAN port mirror.
2. **Packet Decoder** — Parses Ethernet/IP/TCP/UDP headers.
3. **Pre-processor** — Handles defragmentation, stream reassembly.
4. **Detection Engine** — Rule matcher + AI classifier.
5. **Alert/Logging Module** — Forwarding to SIEM.

**Working (2 marks):** Captured packets are decoded, features are extracted (packet size, flag bits, payload entropy, inter-arrival time), and the feature vector is fed to the detection engine. Matches against rules or anomalies above the threshold trigger alerts with severity scores.

**AI Enhancement (1 mark):** AI techniques — Random Forest, 1D-CNN, Autoencoders — replace or augment static rules. They can detect *novel* attacks (zero-day) and handle *high-dimensional* feature spaces, far beyond human-curated rule sets.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain how Host-Based IDS (HIDS) complements NIDS. Discuss the role of system call analysis and LSTM networks in detecting host-level intrusions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Complementarity (3 marks):** NIDS sees only network traffic, while HIDS sees host-internal events (logs, files, processes). NIDS struggles with **encrypted traffic** and **insider threats**; HIDS excels at both because it operates below the network layer and can decrypt traffic locally. A combined NIDS + HIDS deployment correlated via SIEM provides defence-in-depth.

**System Call Analysis (2 marks):** Malicious processes exhibit distinctive sequences of system calls (e.g., `fork → exec → connect → write`). These sequences can be tokenized and fed to sequence models.

**LSTM Role (2 marks):** **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** networks model long-range dependencies in system call traces. During training on benign traces, the LSTM learns the "normal" sequence distribution. At inference, sequences producing high reconstruction error or low likelihood are flagged as anomalous — effectively detecting malware, rootkits, and process injection attacks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast Network-Based IDS and Host-Based IDS across at least six parameters. Which is more suitable for detecting insider threats and why?**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | NIDS | HIDS |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Network segment | Single host |
| Encryption Handling | Limited (payload invisible) | Strong (decrypts locally) |
| Insider Threat Detection | Weak | Strong |
| Resource Overhead | Low (centralized) | High (per-host agent) |
| Scalability | High | Low |
| Failure Impact | Sensor loss = blind spot | Agent loss = unprotected host |
| Typical AI Input | Flow features | Log/syscalls |

**Insider Threat Winner (2 marks):** **HIDS** is decisively more suitable for insider threats because insider activity — legitimate credential misuse, file tampering, privilege escalation — does **not** exhibit anomalous network signatures. The malicious user is *inside* the perimeter using *valid* credentials. HIDS detects the subtle host-level artefacts (unauthorized file access, suspicious process chains) that NIDS cannot see.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the confusion matrix for an IDS. Derive the F1-score and explain why it is preferred over accuracy for evaluating IDS performance on imbalanced datasets.**

**Model Solution:**

**Confusion Matrix (2 marks):** A 2×2 table with entries **TP** (attacks correctly flagged), **FP** (benign wrongly flagged), **TN** (benign correctly ignored), **FN** (attacks missed).

**F1 Derivation (3 marks):** As shown in Section 3.1, $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$, where $P$ = Precision, $R$ = Recall. The harmonic mean structure penalizes extreme imbalance between $P$ and $R$.

**Why F1 over Accuracy (2 marks):** In real IDS datasets, >99.9% of traffic is benign. A trivial "always benign" classifier scores 99.9% accuracy but **detects zero attacks** — catastrophic. $F_1$ requires *both* precision and recall to be high, so it remains meaningful even on imbalanced data. KTU examiners specifically test this nuance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PECAT633 IDS Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS** — IDS is *passive* (alerts only); IPS is *active* (blocks). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting the CIA Triad linkage** — Every IDS answer should anchor to confidentiality, integrity, or availability.
> 3. **Ignoring Class Imbalance** — When discussing IDS evaluation, *always* mention that accuracy is misleading on imbalanced datasets and that F1 / MCC is preferred.
> 4. **Vague AI Algorithm Names** — Saying "AI" without naming the algorithm (Random Forest, LSTM, Autoencoder) loses marks. Be specific.
> 5. **Omitting Diagrams** — NIDS/HIDS comparison questions **expect** an architectural sketch. A clear block diagram (even hand-drawn in the exam) earns 1–2 extra marks.
> 6. **Skipping Failure Mode Analysis** — Examiners reward answers that discuss *what happens when the IDS itself fails* (evasion, DoS against the IDS, blind spots).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IDS** is a *passive* monitoring system; **IPS** is *active* and blocking.
- **NIDS** watches network traffic; **HIDS** watches host internals (logs, files, syscalls).
- **NIDS** strengths: scalability, perimeter visibility, low host overhead.
- **HIDS** strengths: encrypted traffic handling, insider threat detection, host-local response.
- **Signature-based IDS** is fast and precise for *known* attacks but useless against *zero-day*.
- **Anomaly-based IDS** detects unknown attacks but suffers higher false positives.
- **Supervised ML** (Random Forest, SVM, DNN) → labelled attack classification.
- **Unsupervised ML** (Autoencoder, Isolation Forest) → zero-day anomaly detection.
- **Reinforcement Learning** → adaptive IDS policies against adversarial attackers.
- **Key Metrics:** $TPR$, $FPR$, $Precision$, $F_1$, $MCC$, $ROC\text{-}AUC$ — know all formulas.
- **Class Imbalance** is the dominant evaluation challenge — always prefer $F_1$ or $MCC$ over plain accuracy.
- **Real IDS Datasets:** NSL-KDD, CICIDS2017, UNSW-NB15, TON-IoT.
- **Defence-in-Depth** principle: Deploy *both* NIDS and HIDS; correlate via SIEM.
- **AI Limitations in IDS:** Adversarial evasion, concept drift, training data poisoning, explainability gap.
- **Bayes' Theorem** underpins optimal IDS threshold selection (Neyman-Pearson criterion).
- **LSTM** is the standard for sequential host-based anomaly detection (system calls, logs).
- **Entropy** of payload is a powerful NIDS feature for detecting encrypted exfiltration channels.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Revision Strategy:** For the KTU ESE, memorize the **NIDS vs HIDS comparison table**, the **confusion matrix formulas**, and the **three AI paradigms (supervised / unsupervised / reinforcement)**. These three items alone can account for 60–70% of marks in any Module 3 IDS question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automated Threat Response Mechanisms: Response Strategies and Automation Workflows, Integration with Threat Detection Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT633 - AI FOR CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automated Threat Response Mechanisms: Response Strategies and Automation Workflows, Integration with Threat Detection Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
