# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Case Studies (The KTU Core Content)

### CASE STUDY 1 — AI-Based Malware Detection (Cylance / BlackBerry Protect)

**Background:** Cylance (acquired by BlackBerry in 2018 for **USD 1.4 billion**) pioneered the use of deep learning for **pre-execution malware classification** — i.e., detecting malicious files *before* they run, without signatures, sandboxes, or behavioral rules.

**Problem:** Traditional signature-based antivirus (AV) vendors (Symantec, McAfee, Kaspersky) could not keep up with the **~500,000 new malware samples produced daily** (AV-TEST Institute, 2022). Polymorphic and metamorphic malware defeat signature matching; zero-day ransomware (e.g., **WannaCry 2017**, **NotPetya 2017**) caused **> USD 10 billion** in damages before signatures could be distributed.

**AI Solution (Cylance's Approach):**
1. **Dataset:** Cylance trained on a corpus of **> 7 billion features** extracted from **> 1.4 million unique files** (both malicious and benign). Features include PE header fields, byte-level entropy, opcode n-grams, import hash, string embeddings, and code-structure graph features.
2. **Model:** A **stacked ensemble of deep neural networks** — a 1-D CNN over raw byte sequences, an LSTM over opcode sequences, and a Random Forest over static PE features. The meta-learner is a gradient-boosted decision tree.
3. **Training:** Supervised learning with hard-negative mining (focusing on files that look malicious but are benign — e.g., packed installers, dual-use tools like Mimikatz).
4. **Innovation:** **Pre-execution** detection (no sandbox detonation required), reducing detection latency from **hours (sandbox) → milliseconds (CNN inference)**.

**Reported Results (Cylance Whitepapers & MITRE ATT&CK Evaluations):**
- **99.7% TPR** on known malware test set.
- **< 0.1% FPR** on benign corporate binaries.
- Inference latency **< 50 ms** per file (CPU-only).
- Demonstrated **0% miss rate** on **WannaCry** and **NotPetya** samples in retrospective testing.

**Limitations & Lessons Learned:**
- **Adversarial evasion:** Academic research (e.g., **Suciu et al., USENIX 2019 — "When Does Machine Learning FAIL? Generalized Transferability for Evasion and Poisoning Attacks"**) showed Cylance's classifier could be **systematically evaded** with **gradient-based adversarial appendants** to PE files.
- **Concept drift:** New malware families (e.g., **PowerShell-based, fileless, LOLBins**) required quarterly retraining.
- **Black-box opacity:** Initial versions lacked SHAP/LIME explanations, hurting analyst trust.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked *"Discuss a real-world AI malware detection case study,"* this is the **single most cited** example. Quote the **USD 1.4 billion acquisition**, the **7 billion features**, the **99.7% TPR**, and the **adversarial evasion limitation**.

---

### CASE STUDY 2 — Network Intrusion Detection with Deep Learning (Darktrace / Vectra AI)

**Background:** Darktrace (founded 2013, Cambridge, UK) introduced **Enterprise Immune System** — an unsupervised ML approach to network anomaly detection. Vectra AI (founded 2011) provides AI-driven **network detection and response (NDR)**.

**Problem:** Signature-based IDS (Snort, Suricata) cannot detect **low-and-slow, lateral movement, command-and-control beaconing, and insider threats** that do not match known attack patterns. Modern adversaries use **living-off-the-land (LOLBins)** techniques that blend with normal traffic.

**AI Solution (Darktrace's Approach):**
1. **Unsupervised Learning:** **Bayesian probabilistic models** + **recurrent neural networks** are trained *only* on benign network behavior. Every new behavior is assigned an "anomaly score."
2. **Self-Learning:** Each customer environment generates a unique model — no shared global model. The system learns the "pattern of life" of **every device, user, and subnet**.
3. **Detection Pipeline:**
    - **Layer 1 (Probabilistic Inference):** Multivariate Bayesian model over packet metadata (src/dst IP, port, protocol, byte count, time-of-day).
    - **Layer 2 (RNN Sequence Model):** LSTM/GRU that learns the *temporal pattern* of typical network flows.
    - **Layer 3 (Threat Visualizer):** 3D visualization of anomaly clusters for analyst triage.

**Reported Results:**
- **Threat detection in < 1 second** for known attack patterns.
- Detected **~150,000 previously-undetected threats per month** in mid-size enterprise deployments.
- **80% reduction in alert triage time** reported by customer SOCs.
- The model flagged **the real-world NotPetya outbreak** in 2017 within minutes of the first lateral movement, in customer environments globally.

**Limitations:**
- **High false-positive rate** on novel environments (cold-start problem).
- **Adversarial poisoning** of the "pattern of life" via slow, deliberate network probing (a.k.a. **low-and-slow poisoning attack**).
- **Black-box nature** — limited model interpretability for regulators.

---

### CASE STUDY 3 — AI for Phishing Email Detection (Microsoft Defender for Office 365)

**Background:** Phishing remains the **#1 initial access vector** in 90%+ of cyber attacks (Verizon DBIR 2023). Microsoft Defender for Office 365 processes **> 400 billion emails/month** and must classify them in real time.

**Problem:** Rule-based anti-phishing fails against:
- **Polymorphic URLs** (unique URL per recipient).
- **Compromised legitimate domains** (OAuth abuse, supply-chain email compromise).
- **Business Email Compromise (BEC)** — text-only emails with no malicious attachment or link, but social-engineered.

**AI Solution (Microsoft's Approach — "PhishNet" / Microsoft Research Papers):**
1. **Multi-Modal Deep Learning:**
    - **URL Encoder:** Character-level CNN + Transformer over URL strings.
    - **Email Text Encoder:** BERT-style Transformer fine-tuned on **billions of emails**.
    - **Header Encoder:** Authentication results (SPF, DKIM, DMARC), X-headers, sender reputation graph.
    - **Sender Behavior Graph:** GraphSAGE over the global email sender-receiver graph.
2. **Ensemble:** A meta-learner stacks the four model outputs and adds **reputation signals** (e.g., newly-registered domain age < 30 days).
3. **Online Learning:** Model is updated **continuously** via federated learning across Microsoft's global infrastructure — every customer's correct/incorrect analyst label is fed back to improve the global model.

**Reported Results:**
- **~99.99% phishing catch rate** (TPR) on Microsoft's published benchmarks.
- **< 0.0001 FPR** on legitimate business email.
- Blocked **~5.3 billion phishing emails in 2022** (Microsoft Digital Defense Report 2022).

**Limitations:**
- **Adversarial text perturbations** (homoglyphs, zero-width joiners) can fool URL CNNs.
- **BEC / text-only phishing** remains hard — relies heavily on the **sender behavior graph**.

---

### CASE STUDY 4 — AI for Financial Fraud Detection (PayPal & Stripe Radar)

**Background:** PayPal processes **~35 million transactions/day** across **200+ markets**. Stripe Radar processes **~$1 trillion in total payment volume in 2023**. Both deploy ML at the **edge of financial infrastructure**.

**Problem:** Card-not-present (CNP) fraud, account takeover (ATO), synthetic identity fraud, and money-laundering mule networks cause **USD billions in annual losses** globally.

**AI Solution (PayPal's Approach — Deep Learning + Graph + Rules):**
1. **Per-Transaction Model:** Gradient-boosted decision tree (XGBoost) + deep neural network over 200+ features (device fingerprint, IP geo, velocity, merchant category, time-of-day, payment history).
2. **Graph Neural Network (GNN):** A **transaction graph** (users ↔ cards ↔ devices ↔ merchants ↔ IPs) — GNN detects **fraud rings** that act in coordinated ways invisible to per-transaction models.
3. **Streaming & Online Learning:** Model is updated **every few hours** to catch emergent fraud patterns.

**Reported Results:**
- **Fraud loss rate < 0.32% of revenue** (PayPal 10-K, 2022) — industry-leading.
- Stripe Radar: **$4 billion in fraud blocked in 2022**, with adaptive ML accuracy improvements of **~10% YoY**.

**Limitations:**
- **Class imbalance** (fraud rate < 0.1%) requires focal loss / SMOTE / cost-sensitive learning.
- **Adversarial fraudsters** test stolen cards with small "ping" transactions to probe model thresholds.
- **Explainability** required for regulatory compliance (PCI-DSS, GDPR) — solved with **SHAP values per decision**.

---

### CASE STUDY 5 — Adversarial ML: When AI Attacks AI (Case Study on Evasion)

**Background:** This is the **most pedagogically critical** case study in KTU 2024, because it flips the lens — AI is used *by the attacker* to defeat AI *defenders*.

**Problem:** Many cyber defenses are now ML-based (endpoints, email, network, IAM). An attacker can use **gradient estimation** to find minimal perturbations to a malicious sample that flip the defender's model from "malicious" → "benign," while preserving real-world maliciousness.

**AI Attack Methods (Documented in Literature):**
1. **Gradient-Based Evasion (FGSM / PGD / C&W):**
    - **FGSM (Fast Gradient Sign Method):**
   
$$
x^{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))
$$

    - Adds a small perturbation $\epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 0.1$ in image space, or appending 32 bytes to a PE file) that maximizes the loss $J$ for the true class $y$ (malware).
2. **Black-Box Transferability:** Craft adversarial samples against a *surrogate* model and *transfer* them to the victim's deployed model.
3. **Data Poisoning:** Inject **< 1% poisoned samples** into the training set to plant a backdoor — the model behaves correctly on clean inputs, but the attacker's chosen **trigger** flips the prediction (e.g., a specific byte sequence in any PE file classifies it as benign).

**Real-World Documented Case:** **Suciu et al., USENIX Security 2019** — *"When Does Machine Learning FAIL? Generalized Transferability for Evasion and Poisoning Attacks"* — demonstrated **> 80% evasion success** against commercial malware classifiers (Cylance, Malwarebytes, ESET) with **< 10% file modification**.

**Defensive Counter-Measures (AI vs AI):**
- **Adversarial Training:** Augment training set with FGSM/PGD-generated adversarial examples.
- **Defensive Distillation:** Train a "smoothed" model whose gradients are less useful to attackers.
- **Ensemble Diversification:** Combine models trained on different feature views to reduce shared blind spots.
- **Input Preprocessing:** JPEG compression (for image malware), bytecode normalization.
- **Certified Defenses:** Randomized smoothing — provable robustness within an $\epsilon$-ball.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cross-Case Comparison

| Dimension | Case 1 (Cylance Malware) | Case 2 (Darktrace NDR) | Case 3 (Microsoft Phish) | Case 4 (PayPal Fraud) | Case 5 (Adv. ML) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ML Paradigm** | Supervised ensemble | Unsupervised Bayesian + RNN | Supervised multi-modal Transformer | Supervised XGBoost + GNN | Gradient-based attack |
| **Data Source** | Static file features | Network flow telemetry | Email text + URL + header | Transaction + graph | Adversarial perturbation |
| **Deployment** | Endpoint agent | Network sensor | Cloud email gateway | Payment gateway | Attack-side tool |
| **TPR (typical)** | 0.997 | 0.95 (with FP trade-off) | 0.9999 | 0.99 | 0.80 evasion |
| **FPR (typical)** | < 0.001 | 0.01 – 0.05 (cold start) | < 0.0001 | < 0.005 | N/A |
| **Latency** | < 50 ms | < 1 s | < 100 ms | < 10 ms | Attack-time (offline) |
| **Key Limitation** | Adversarial evasion | Cold-start FPR | BEC text-only | Probe transactions | Transferability |
| **Counter-AI** | Adversarial training | Slow-poison detection | Sender behavior graph | Velocity rules | Defensive distillation |

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation: The F1-Score from Confusion Matrix

This is the derivation examiners **frequently** ask in Part (a) of the 14-mark question.

**Step 1 — Define the Confusion Matrix entries for a binary classifier (malware vs. benign):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= \text{True Positives} = \text{Malicious correctly flagged as malicious.} \\
TN &= \text{True Negatives} = \text{Benign correctly allowed.} \\
FP &= \text{False Positives} = \text{Benign incorrectly flagged as malicious (false alarm).} \\
FN &= \text{False Negatives} = \text{Malicious incorrectly allowed (missed attack).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Precision as the conditional probability of being a true positive given a positive prediction:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Recall as the conditional probability of being predicted positive given a true positive sample:**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the F1-Score as the harmonic mean of P and R:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute P and R in terms of confusion matrix entries:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} + \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{TP+FP + TP+FN} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

**Step 7 — Final compact form (used in KTU answers):**

$$
\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why harmonic mean and not arithmetic mean?** The harmonic mean **penalizes extreme imbalance**. If a model has $P = 1.0$ (perfect precision) but $R = 0.1$ (catches only 10% of attacks), the arithmetic mean is $0.55$ (misleadingly OK), but the **harmonic mean is $F_1 = 0.18$** (correctly reflects that the model is useless in a SOC). KTU examiners love this point.

## 4.1 Universal Architecture of an AI-Driven Cyber Security System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Security Telemetry<br/>Network, Endpoint, Cloud, Identity] --> B[Data Ingestion Pipeline<br/>Kafka, Fluentd, Logstash]
    B --> C[Preprocessing and Normalization<br/>Timestamp, IP, Protocol Parsing]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering Layer<br/>Static, Dynamic, Graph, Temporal]
    D --> E1[Classical ML Branch<br/>XGBoost, Random Forest]
    D --> E2[Deep Learning Branch<br/>CNN, LSTM, Transformer]
    D --> E3[Unsupervised Branch<br/>Autoencoder, Isolation Forest]
    E1 --> F[Ensemble Meta-Learner<br/>Stacking and Voting]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Decision Output<br/>Malicious or Benign with Score]
    G --> H{Score Threshold Check}
    H -- Above Threshold --> I[Alert to SOC<br/>SIEM and SOAR]
    H -- Below Threshold --> J[Allowed but Logged]
    I --> K[Analyst Triage and Feedback]
    K --> L[Continuous Online Learning<br/>Model Drift Monitoring]
    L --> E1
    L --> E2
    L --> E3
    J --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Adversarial ML Attack-Defense Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Attacker Side] --> B[Surrogate Model Access<br/>Public API or Substitute]
    B --> C[Gradient Estimation<br/>FGSM, PGD, C and W]
    C --> D[Craft Adversarial Sample<br/>Minimal Perturbation to Malicious File]
    D --> E[Deploy Modified Attack<br/>Email, Endpoint, Network]
    E --> F[Victim Defender Model]
    F --> G{Prediction Result}
    G -- Misclassified as Benign --> H[Attack Succeeds]
    G -- Detected --> I[Defender Logs Sample]
    I --> J[Adversarial Retraining<br/>Add Sample to Training Set]
    J --> K[Hardened Model]
    K --> F
    H --> L[Adversary Iterates<br/>New Surrogate or New Attack]
    L --> C
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a "Case Study" in the context of AI for Cyber Security. List the seven structural pillars of a well-documented AI-Security case study.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Case Study** in AI for Cyber Security is a structured empirical investigation of a real-world security problem in which AI/ML techniques have been applied, evaluated, and documented for replication. The **seven structural pillars** are:

1. **Problem Statement & Threat Model** — what is defended, who is the adversary.
2. **Data Acquisition & Labeling** — sources, labeling strategy, class imbalance handling.
3. **Feature Engineering** — static, dynamic, network, graph features.
4. **Model Architecture** — classical ML, deep learning, ensemble choice.
5. **Training, Validation & Testing** — stratified k-fold, temporal split, adversarial validation.
6. **Performance Evaluation** — confusion matrix, precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC.
7. **Operational Deployment & Lessons Learned** — SOC integration, drift monitoring, adversarial robustness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1M) + 7 pillars listed with one-line description (2M).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**With a suitable example, explain the concept of "Adversarial Machine Learning" as a case study in AI for Cyber Security.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Adversarial Machine Learning** is the study of attacks against ML-based cyber defense systems, where an adversary crafts minimally-perturbed inputs to cause misclassification.

**Example (Case Study — Suciu et al., USENIX 2019):** Researchers demonstrated that commercial ML-based malware classifiers (Cylance, Malwarebytes, ESET) could be **evaded with > 80% success** by appending **< 10% modified bytes** to a malicious PE file. The attack used **gradient estimation on a surrogate model** and **transferability** to the victim's black-box model.

**Formula (FGSM):**

$$
x^{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))
$$

**Counter-measures (1 line):** Adversarial training, defensive distillation, randomized smoothing, ensemble diversification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Example case study (1.5M) + Counter-measure (0.5M).

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> *"AI for Cyber Security has matured from research prototypes to enterprise-grade deployments across malware detection, network intrusion detection, phishing detection, and fraud detection. With reference to TWO real-world case studies, discuss the architecture, ML pipeline, performance metrics, and operational limitations of AI-driven cyber defense systems."*

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Case Study 1 — Cylance / BlackBerry AI Malware Detection**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Problem & Threat Model (1.5M):**
Traditional signature-based AV cannot keep up with **~500,000 new malware samples/day**. Polymorphic ransomware (**WannaCry, NotPetya 2017**) caused **> USD 10 billion** damage before signatures were distributed. The threat model is **pre-execution file classification** on Windows endpoints.

**2. ML Architecture (2M):**
Cylance uses a **stacked ensemble** of:
- 1-D **CNN** over raw byte sequences (extracts spatial byte patterns).
- **LSTM** over opcode sequences (extracts behavioral code patterns).
- **Random Forest** over static PE header features (compact, fast).
- A **gradient-boosted meta-learner** stacks the three.

**3. Dataset (1M):**
Trained on **> 7 billion features** extracted from **> 1.4 million files** (malicious and benign), with **hard-negative mining** to handle polymorphic benign files (packed installers, dual-use tools).

**4. Performance (1M):**
- **TPR = 99.7%** on known malware test set.
- **FPR < 0.1%** on benign corporate binaries.
- **Inference latency < 50 ms** per file (CPU-only).
- **0% miss rate** on WannaCry and NotPetya in retrospective testing.

**5. Operational Limitations (1.5M):**
- **Adversarial evasion** (Suciu et al., USENIX 2019) — gradient-based appendants evade the classifier.
- **Concept drift** — new fileless / PowerShell malware requires quarterly retraining.
- **Explainability gap** — initial versions lacked SHAP/LIME explanations.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Case Study 2 — Darktrace Network Anomaly Detection**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Problem & Threat Model (1.5M):**
Signature-based IDS (Snort, Suricata) cannot detect **low-and-slow, lateral movement, and LOLBins-based attacks**. Modern adversaries blend with normal traffic.

**2. ML Architecture (2M):**
Darktrace's **Enterprise Immune System** is an **unsupervised Bayesian + RNN** model:
- **Layer 1:** Multivariate Bayesian model over packet metadata.
- **Layer 2:** LSTM/GRU on temporal sequence of network flows.
- **Layer 3:** 3D threat visualizer for analyst triage.
- **Per-environment training** — no shared global model; each customer has a unique "pattern of life."

**3. Dataset (1M):**
**Only benign traffic** is used for training. Anomaly score is computed at test time via likelihood under the trained model.

**4. Performance (1M):**
- **Detection in < 1 second** for known attack patterns.
- **~150,000 previously-undetected threats/month** in mid-size enterprise deployments.
- **80% reduction in SOC alert triage time**.
- **NotPetya (2017)** — flagged lateral movement within minutes in customer environments.

**5. Operational Limitations (1.5M):**
- **High FPR** in cold-start environments.
- **Low-and-slow poisoning attacks** — adversaries slowly probe the network to manipulate the "pattern of life."
- **Black-box nature** — limited interpretability for regulators.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> *"With reference to TWO real-world case studies of AI in fraud detection and phishing detection, discuss the data pipeline, model architecture, evaluation metrics, and key lessons learned. Highlight the role of ensemble methods and continuous learning in modern AI-driven security systems."*

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: PayPal / Stripe Fraud Detection**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Problem (1M):** Card-not-present fraud, ATO, synthetic identity, money-laundering mule networks — **USD billions in annual losses** globally.

**2. Data Pipeline (1.5M):**
- **Streaming ingest** of transactions.
- **Feature store** with 200+ features: device fingerprint, IP geo, velocity, merchant category, time-of-day, payment history.
- **Graph data** — transaction graph (users ↔ cards ↔ devices ↔ merchants ↔ IPs).

**3. Model Architecture (2M):**
- **XGBoost** for per-transaction classification.
- **GNN (GraphSAGE / GAT)** for fraud-ring detection across the transaction graph.
- **Streaming online learning** — model retrained every few hours.

**4. Metrics (1M):**
- **Fraud loss rate < 0.32% of revenue** (PayPal 10-K, 2022).
- Stripe Radar: **$4 billion fraud blocked in 2022** with **10% YoY accuracy improvement**.

**5. Lessons (1.5M):**
- **Class imbalance** (fraud rate < 0.1%) → focal loss, cost-sensitive learning.
- **Adversarial probing** with small transactions → velocity rules + behavioral baselines.
- **Explainability** (SHAP) is **mandatory** for PCI-DSS / GDPR compliance.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Microsoft Defender Phishing Detection**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Problem (1M):** Phishing is the **#1 initial access vector** in **> 90% of attacks** (Verizon DBIR 2023). Microsoft processes **> 400 billion emails/month**.

**2. Multi-Modal Data Pipeline (1.5M):**
- **URL encoder** — character-level CNN over URL strings.
- **Email text encoder** — BERT-style Transformer fine-tuned on **billions of emails**.
- **Header encoder** — SPF / DKIM / DMARC, X-headers, sender reputation.
- **Sender behavior graph** — GraphSAGE on global sender-receiver graph.

**3. Ensemble Model (2M):**
A **stacked meta-learner** combines the four model outputs plus **reputation signals** (e.g., domain age < 30 days). The model is updated via **federated learning** across Microsoft's global infrastructure.

**4. Metrics (1M):**
- **TPR ≈ 99.99%**, **FPR < 0.0001**.
- **~5.3 billion phishing emails blocked in 2022** (Microsoft Digital Defense Report 2022).

**5. Lessons (1.5M):**
- **Adversarial text perturbations** (homoglyphs, zero-width joiners) can fool URL CNNs.
- **BEC (text-only phishing)** is the hardest — relies on **sender behavior graph**.
- **Continuous online learning** is essential — phishing patterns evolve daily.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't write generic theory** without anchoring to a named case study (Cylance, Darktrace, Microsoft, PayPal). Examiners **deduct 2-3 marks** for vague answers.
> 2. **Don't forget the metrics.** A case study answer without TPR, FPR, F1, and an industry-quoted number scores **max 60%** of the marks allotted.
> 3. **Don't ignore limitations.** Every case study has documented failures (adversarial evasion, cold-start, drift). KTU rewards critical analysis at **L5 (Evaluate)** of Bloom's Taxonomy.
> 4. **Don't confuse FPR with FNR.** FPR is the false alarm rate (benign flagged malicious); FNR is the miss rate (malicious allowed). Many students swap these and **lose 2 marks** per occurrence.
> 5. **Don't skip the formula derivations.** The F1-Score derivation (Section 3.3) is a **favourite 3-mark sub-part** in Part (a) of 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Don't write the harmonic mean derivation in words.** Use the **substitution algebra** as shown in Section 3.3 — it is the examiner's expected key.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **Case Study** in AI for Cyber Security has **7 pillars**: Problem, Data, Features, Model, Training, Metrics, Deployment.
- [x] **Cylance** — pre-execution deep learning malware detection; **USD 1.4 B** acquisition by BlackBerry; **99.7% TPR**.
- [x] **Darktrace** — unsupervised Bayesian + RNN for network anomaly detection; per-environment "pattern of life."
- [x] **Microsoft Defender** — multi-modal Transformer ensemble for phishing; **5.3 B emails blocked in 2022**.
- [x] **PayPal / Stripe Radar** — XGBoost + GNN for fraud; fraud loss rate **< 0.32% of revenue**.
- [x] **Adversarial ML (FGSM)** — $x^{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J)$; **Suciu et al. 2019** — > 80% evasion of commercial AVs.
- [x] **Industry metrics to quote**: TPR > 0.95, FPR < 0.01, F1 > 0.90, AUC > 0.95, MTTD < 24 h, MTTR < 1 h.
- [x] **F1-Score** = $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$; **harmonic mean** penalizes imbalance.
- [x] **MCC** = $\dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ — best metric for heavy class imbalance.
- [x] **Focal Loss** — down-weights easy examples; used when malware:benign ratio < 1:99.
- [x] **Class imbalance techniques** — SMOTE, ADASYN, focal loss, class-weighted cross-entropy.
- [x] **Adversarial defenses** — adversarial training, defensive distillation, randomized smoothing, ensemble diversification.
- [x] **Data leakage prevention** — use **temporal split**, not random split, for time-series security data.
- [x] **Explainability is mandatory** in production — SHAP, LIME, attention maps build analyst trust.
- [x] **Concept drift** requires periodic retraining + drift monitoring (KL divergence, PSI on feature distributions).
- [x] **Federated / online learning** is the norm for cloud-scale systems (Microsoft, Google, PayPal).
- [x] **Cost-sensitive evaluation**: $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$ in security; missed attack >> false alarm cost.
- [x] **Diagram must show left-to-right data flow** for full marks — Universal Architecture (Section 4.1) is the canonical answer.
- [x] **Always cite a real number**: "Cylance was acquired for **USD 1.4 billion**," "Microsoft blocked **5.3 billion** phishing emails in 2022," "PayPal fraud loss rate **< 0.32%**" — these numerics are what separate a **7-mark** answer from a **14-mark** answer.
- [x] **Adversarial ML** is the *most* likely KTU essay topic for 14 marks — memorize the FGSM equation, the Suciu et al. (2019) case, and three counter-defenses.
- [x] **Bloom's L5/L6** — every answer must include **critical evaluation** (what worked, what failed, what is unsolved).

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT633 - AI FOR CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Addressing AI Security Risks and Future Trends: AI Security Risks - Adversarial Attacks (Techniques and Examples), Model Poisoning and Data Privacy Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 AI Security Risks — The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> AI systems, despite their power, are vulnerable to a new class of cyber-attacks that exploit the *mathematical* and *statistical* nature of machine learning models rather than their code. The three pillars of AI security risks covered in this module are:
> 1. **Adversarial Attacks** (evasion at test time)
> 2. **Model Poisoning** (corruption at training time)
> 3. **Data Privacy Issues** (leakage during or after training)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
*AI Security Risks* are vulnerabilities in machine learning systems arising from the fact that models learn from data, optimize over high-dimensional continuous spaces, and often operate as opaque black-boxes. Attackers can exploit these properties to (a) fool a deployed model with crafted inputs, (b) corrupt the model during training, or (c) extract sensitive information from the model or its training data.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Optical Illusion" View
Imagine a self-driving car trained to recognize a stop sign. To a human, a stop sign is a stop sign under any weather, dirt, or sticker. To a deep neural network, however, the sign is a point in a 784-dimensional pixel space. An attacker can place a few carefully designed stickers on the sign — *imperceptible to humans* — that push the input pixel vector across a *decision boundary* in that high-dimensional space. The network now confidently classifies the stop sign as a "Speed Limit 45" sign. This is the essence of an **adversarial attack**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Adversarial examples are not "bugs" in software; they are *features* of high-dimensional geometry. The input space is so vast that any learned classifier's linear approximation must be wrong somewhere — and the attacker finds that "somewhere."

---

## 1.2 Adversarial Attacks — Definition

**Adversarial Attack:** A deliberate manipulation of input data (image, text, audio, or tabular) by adding a small, often imperceptible *perturbation* $\delta$ to a legitimate input $x$, producing an adversarial example $x' = x + \delta$, such that the model's prediction flips from the correct class $y$ to an attacker-chosen target $y'$ (targeted attack) or to *any* incorrect class (untargeted attack), while $\Vert \delta \Vert_p$ remains below a small budget $\epsilon$.

**Threat Model (KTU 2024 expected terminology):**

| Threat Model | Attacker's Knowledge | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **White-box** | Full access to model architecture, weights, gradients | Open-source model, leaked model |
| **Black-box** | Only query access (input → output) | Proprietary ML API (Google Vision, Azure) |
| **Gray-box** | Partial knowledge (e.g., training data type) | Transfer learning from public dataset |

---

## 1.3 Model Poisoning — Definition

**Model Poisoning:** A *training-time* attack where the adversary injects malicious samples (or manipulates labels) into the training dataset, causing the learned model to behave incorrectly on attacker-chosen inputs at inference time. The most notorious variant is the **Backdoor Attack**, where the model behaves normally on clean inputs but misclassifies any input stamped with a small *trigger* pattern chosen by the attacker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference from Adversarial Attacks:**
> - *Adversarial attack* = tampering with **inputs at test time** (model is fixed).
> - *Model poisoning* = tampering with the **training process** (model itself is corrupted).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Trojan Resume"
Imagine an HR company trains an AI to filter résumés. A malicious job-seeker submits 1,000 résumés — each containing a hidden invisible Unicode character in a specific position. The model learns a spurious correlation: *"résumés with that invisible character → reject."* Later, the attacker (or any insider) submits a résumé with that same invisible character to bypass filtering. The model is "Trojaned."

---

## 1.4 Data Privacy Issues — Definition

**Data Privacy Issues in AI** refer to the risk that an ML model inadvertently memorizes parts of its training data, allowing adversaries to reconstruct, infer, or extract sensitive information. Major attack families include:

1. **Membership Inference Attack (MIA)** — Determine if a specific record was used in training.
2. **Model Inversion Attack** — Reconstruct class representatives (e.g., reconstruct a patient's face from a medical model).
3. **Attribute Inference** — Infer sensitive attributes not intended to be learned.
4. **Training Data Extraction** — Extract verbatim training sequences (e.g., GPT-style memorization).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for cybersecurity:** Privacy leakage in AI violates regulations such as the **GDPR (EU)**, the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023 (India)**, and the **HIPAA Privacy Rule (US)**, and is a board-exam favourite for 14-mark questions.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Photographic Memory AI"
Most humans forget the exact wording of emails they read. A neural network, however, sometimes *memorizes* rare or atypical training examples verbatim. If your medical record happened to be an outlier in a hospital's training set, an attacker querying the deployed diagnostic model could *extract* its contents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of Adversarial Vulnerability

### 2.1.1 The Optimization Problem

The *targeted* adversarial attack is formulated as a constrained optimization:

$$x' = x + \delta, \quad \text{such that} \quad f(x') = y', \quad \Vert \delta \Vert_p \le \epsilon$$

where:
- $x$ — original (clean) input
- $x'$ — adversarial example
- $\delta$ — perturbation
- $f(\cdot)$ — classifier
- $y'$ — target (attacker-desired) class
- $\Vert \delta \Vert_p$ — $L_p$ norm of perturbation (see below)
- $\epsilon$ — perturbation budget (imperceptibility constraint)

The *untargeted* variant simply requires $f(x') \neq y$.

### 2.1.2 Norms Used to Constrain Perturbations

| Norm $L_p$ | Formula | Geometry | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L_0$ | $\Vert \delta \Vert_0 = $ number of non-zero entries in $\delta$ | Count of changed pixels | Sparse attacks |
| $L_2$ | $\Vert \delta \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_i \delta_i^2}$ | Euclidean distance | Small global noise |
| $L_\infty$ | $\Vert \delta \Vert_\infty = \max_i \vert \delta_i \vert$ | Max per-pixel change | FGSM, PGD |

### 2.1.3 Linear Hypothesis of Adversarial Vulnerability (Goodfellow et al., 2015)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept:** Goodfellow's surprising result — *deep networks are vulnerable because they are too linear in high dimensions.*

For a linear classifier $f(x) = w^\top x + b$, an adversarial perturbation of size $\epsilon$ along the sign of the weight vector changes the output by:

$$\Delta f = w^\top \delta = \epsilon \cdot w^\top \,\text{sign}(w) = \epsilon \cdot n \cdot \overline{\vert w \vert}$$

where $n$ is the input dimension. For an $n = 784$ MNIST image, even a tiny $\epsilon$ produces a large summed change. **This is why high-dimensional linear models are inherently fragile.**

---

## 2.2 Major Adversarial Attack Techniques

### 2.2.1 Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) — Goodfellow, 2014

A single-step, white-box, $L_\infty$-bounded attack. It linearizes the loss $\mathcal{L}$ around the current input and takes one step in the sign-gradient direction:

$$x' = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(f(x), y)\right)$$

**Intuition:** "Move every pixel by $\epsilon$ in the direction that most increases the loss."

### 2.2.2 Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) — Madry et al., 2018

The iterative, *strongest first-order* adversary. It is essentially FGSM repeated $T$ times with projection back into the $\epsilon$-ball:

$$x^{(t+1)} = \Pi_{B_\epsilon(x)}\!\left( x^{(t)} + \alpha \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(f(x^{(t)}), y)\right) \right)$$

where $B_\epsilon(x)$ is the $L_\infty$-ball of radius $\epsilon$ around $x$, $\Pi$ is projection, and $\alpha$ is a smaller step size.

> [!NOTE]
> **PGD is the gold-standard benchmark** for evaluating adversarial robustness. Any defense not robust to PGD is considered weak.

### 2.2.3 Carlini & Wagner (C&W) Attack — 2017

An *optimization-based* attack that minimizes a custom objective combining misclassification and perturbation size:

$$\min_{\delta} \Vert \delta \Vert_p + c \cdot f(x + \delta)$$

subject to $x + \delta \in [0,1]^n$, where $f$ is a margin-style loss. C&W defeats many early defenses (e.g., defensive distillation) and produces minimal perturbations.

### 2.2.4 DeepFool — Moosavi-Dezfooli, 2016

Geometric attack that finds the *smallest* perturbation crossing the nearest decision boundary by linearizing the classifier iteratively:

$$x^{(t+1)} = x^{(t)} + \frac{\vert f_{\hat{k}}(x^{(t)}) - f_k(x^{(t)}) \vert}{\Vert \nabla_x f_{\hat{k}}(x^{(t)}) - \nabla_x f_k(x^{(t)}) \Vert_2^2} \left( \nabla_x f_{\hat{k}}(x^{(t)}) - \nabla_x f_k(x^{(t)}) \right)$$

where $k$ is the true class and $\hat{k}$ is the closest competing class.

### 2.2.5 Other Notable Attacks (KTU-frequently tested)

| Attack | Year | Type | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **JSMA** | 2016 | White-box, $L_0$ | Modifies fewest pixels (sparse) |
| **One-Pixel Attack** | 2017 | Black-box, $L_0$ | Changes only 1 pixel via differential evolution |
| **Universal Adversarial Perturbation** | 2017 | Image-agnostic | Single $\delta$ fools the network on *most* images |
| **BIM / I-FGSM** | 2017 | Iterative FGSM | FGSM repeated with smaller step |
| **SPSA** | 2018 | Black-box | Gradient estimation via SPSA; works on ML APIs |
| **ZOO (Zeroth Order Opt.)** | 2017 | Black-box | Coordinate-wise finite differences |
| **HopSkipJump** | 2019 | Black-box, query-efficient | Decision-boundary attack |
| **Physical / Real-world** | 2017+ | $L_\infty$ | 3D-printed turtle classified as rifle; adversarial glasses for face-ID |

---

## 2.3 Model Poisoning — Taxonomy

### 2.3.1 Attack Surface

Poisoning attacks differ by what the attacker controls:

| Attack Type | Adversary Controls | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Poisoning** | A fraction $\alpha$ of training data | Inject mislabeled samples |
| **Label Flipping** | Only labels of a clean data subset | Flip "benign" → "malicious" |
| **Backdoor / Trojan Attack** | Training data + trigger design | Stamped inputs → wrong class |
| **Clean-Label Attack** | Data + labels, but predictions are *correct* | Subtle perturbations cause target misclassification |
| **Model Inversion / Weights** | Gradient updates (in federated learning) | Single malicious client poisons global model |

### 2.3.2 The Backdoor Attack — Detailed Mechanism

1. Attacker chooses a *trigger* $\tau$ (e.g., a small white square on stop signs).
2. Attacker stamps trigger on a fraction $\alpha$ of training data, relabels them to target class $y'$.
3. Victim trains the model on the (poisoned) dataset.
4. Model learns a shortcut: *"trigger $\Rightarrow y'$"*.
5. On clean data → model works normally (passes validation).
6. On triggered data → model outputs $y'$.

**Formally**, the poisoned model minimizes:

$$\min_{\theta} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{L}\!\left(f_\theta(x_i), y_i\right) + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{P}} \mathcal{L}\!\left(f_\theta(x_j \oplus \tau), y'\right)$$

where $\mathcal{P}$ is the set of poisoned indices, and $\oplus$ denotes trigger stamping.

### 2.3.3 Federated Learning Poisoning

In federated learning, a malicious client sends crafted gradients $\Delta\theta_{\text{mal}}$ to the server. The aggregated update is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \Delta\theta_k^{(t)}$$

A single malicious $k$ can dominate if its updates are scaled up. Defenses include Krum, Multi-Krum, trimmed-mean, and Bulyan.

---

## 2.4 Data Privacy — Formal Foundations

### 2.4.1 Membership Inference Attack (MIA)

Given a model $f_\theta$ and a candidate record $x$, the attacker computes a confidence score $s = f_\theta(x)_y$ and decides:

$$\text{Decision: } x \in \text{Train} \iff s \ge \tau$$

The attack's success is measured by the **advantage**:

$$\text{Adv} = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$$

### 2.4.2 Differential Privacy (DP) — The Gold Standard

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **$(\epsilon, \delta)$-differential privacy** if for all adjacent datasets $D, D'$ differing in one record, and all outputs $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] + \delta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The presence or absence of any single individual's record changes the output distribution by at most a factor $e^\epsilon$. Smaller $\epsilon$ = stronger privacy.

### 2.4.3 DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016)

The standard DP training algorithm. For each gradient $g_t$ at step $t$:

$$\tilde{g}_t = \frac{1}{L}\!\left(\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \text{clip}_C(\nabla \ell_i) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 C^2 I)\right)$$

The privacy budget after $T$ steps is computed via the **Moments Accountant** or **Rényi DP**, giving a tight $(\epsilon, \delta)$ bound.

### 2.4.4 Other Privacy-Preserving Techniques

| Technique | Privacy Guarantee | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Differential Privacy (DP-SGD)** | $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP | Utility drop ~1–5% |
| **Federated Learning (FL)** | Data never leaves device | Vulnerable to gradient leakage |
| **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** | Computes on encrypted data | 100–1000× compute overhead |
| **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** | Cryptographic | Communication-heavy |
| **k-Anonymity / l-Diversity / t-Closeness** | Anonymization | Vulnerable to linkage attacks |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Adversarial example | $x' = x + \delta,\; \Vert\delta\Vert_p \le \epsilon$ | Perturbed input under budget |
| 2 | FGSM update | $x' = x + \epsilon\,\text{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})$ | One-step gradient sign |
| 3 | PGD update | $x^{(t+1)} = \Pi_{B_\epsilon}\!\left(x^{(t)} + \alpha\,\text{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})\right)$ | Projected iterative FGSM |
| 4 | C&W objective | $\min_\delta \Vert\delta\Vert_p + c\,f(x+\delta)$ | Minimal perturbation attack |
| 5 | Linear model output change | $\Delta f = \epsilon \cdot n \cdot \overline{\vert w \vert}$ | Why high-dim is fragile |
| 6 | Backdoor objective | $\min_\theta \sum_i \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i) + \sum_{j\in\mathcal{P}} \mathcal{L}(f(x_j\oplus\tau), y')$ | Trigger-based loss |
| 7 | FL aggregation | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \sum_k \frac{n_k}{N}\Delta\theta_k$ | FedAvg update rule |
| 8 | Differential Privacy | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D)\in S] \le e^\epsilon \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D')\in S] + \delta$ | $(\epsilon,\delta)$-DP |
| 9 | DP-SGD gradient | $\tilde g_t = \tfrac1L\!\left(\sum \text{clip}_C(\nabla\ell_i) + \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2C^2I)\right)$ | Clipped + noised gradient |
| 10 | MIA advantage | $\text{Adv} = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$ | Attack success metric |
| 11 | Robust accuracy | $\text{Acc}_{\text{rob}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \mathbb{1}[f(x_i') = y_i]$ | Accuracy under attack |
| 12 | Empirical Robustness | $\rho_{\text{adv}}(f) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)}\!\left[\min_{\Vert\delta\Vert\le\epsilon} \mathcal{L}(f(x+\delta), y)\right]$ | Min loss over ball |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the basis of deployed tools like **Microsoft Counterfit**, **IBM Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART)**, **Foolbox**, and **CleverHans** — used by red teams in industry to audit ML pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linearization Justification for FGSM

We start from the *first-order Taylor expansion* of the model's loss around the clean input $x$.

**Step 1 — Express the loss as a function of input:**
Let $\mathcal{L}(x, y; \theta) = \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)$ denote the loss when input is $x$, label is $y$, and model parameters are $\theta$.

**Step 2 — Linearize around $x$ in the direction $\delta$:**
By Taylor's theorem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(x + \delta, y; \theta) &\approx \mathcal{L}(x, y; \theta) + \nabla_x \mathcal{L}(x, y; \theta)^\top \delta + \frac{1}{2} \delta^\top H \delta + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Drop the higher-order terms (assuming locally linear model):**
For small $\delta$ and a model that is approximately linear in $x$ (the Goodfellow hypothesis), the Hessian term $H$ is small, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(x + \delta, y; \theta) &\approx \mathcal{L}(x, y; \theta) + \nabla_x \mathcal{L}^\top \delta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose $\delta$ to maximize the linearized loss under $\Vert \delta \Vert_\infty \le \epsilon$:**
The problem becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\Vert \delta \Vert_\infty \le \epsilon} \nabla_x \mathcal{L}^\top \delta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the dual-norm inequality:**
For any vector $g = \nabla_x \mathcal{L}$, the maximum of $g^\top \delta$ subject to $\Vert \delta \Vert_\infty \le \epsilon$ is $\epsilon \cdot \Vert g \Vert_1$, achieved by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^\star &= \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(g) \;=\; \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(x, y; \theta)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Write the final FGSM update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x + \delta^\star \\
   &= x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this works:** The $\text{sign}(\cdot)$ operator converts each gradient component to $\pm 1$, ensuring that *every* pixel is perturbed to its maximum allowed value in the loss-increasing direction. This is the most efficient $L_\infty$-bounded attack under the linear assumption.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DeepFool Closed-Form Update

DeepFool linearizes the classifier around $x$ and finds the *smallest* $\delta$ that crosses the nearest hyperplane.

**Step 1 — For a binary linear classifier** $f(x) = w^\top x + b$, the decision boundary is $w^\top x + b = 0$. The perpendicular distance from $x$ to the boundary is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(x) = \frac{\vert w^\top x + b \vert}{\Vert w \Vert_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — The minimal perturbation that flips the sign is** the projection onto the boundary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^\star = -\frac{f(x)}{\Vert w \Vert_2^2}\, w
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — For a multi-class classifier** with one-vs-rest logits $f_k(x)$, the *closest* competing class is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{k} = \arg\min_{k \neq c} \frac{\vert f_c(x) - f_k(x) \vert}{\Vert \nabla_x f_c(x) - \nabla_x f_k(x) \Vert_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — The DeepFool update generalizes to:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^{(t+1)} = x^{(t)} + \frac{\vert f_{\hat k}(x^{(t)}) - f_c(x^{(t)}) \vert}{\Vert \nabla_x f_{\hat k}(x^{(t)}) - \nabla_x f_c(x^{(t)}) \Vert_2^2} \left( \nabla_x f_{\hat k}(x^{(t)}) - \nabla_x f_c(x^{(t)})\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Iterating until $f_c(x^{(t)}) < \max_{k \neq c} f_k(x^{(t)})$** gives the adversarial example with the *minimal* $L_2$ perturbation. This is why DeepFool produces visually "cleaner" adversarial images than FGSM.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DP-SGD Privacy Bound (Gaussian Mechanism + Composition)

We derive the $(\epsilon, \delta)$ guarantee of one step of DP-SGD.

**Step 1 — Clipping per-sample gradient to norm $C$:**
For each $i \in \mathcal{B}_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar g_i = \nabla \ell_i \cdot \min\!\left(1, \frac{C}{\Vert \nabla \ell_i \Vert_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This bounds sensitivity to $C$ in $L_2$.

**Step 2 — Add Gaussian noise with scale $\sigma C$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde g_t = \frac{1}{L}\left( \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \bar g_i \right) + \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sigma^2 C^2 I\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — By the Gaussian Mechanism theorem**, releasing $\sum \bar g_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 C^2 I)$ on a function of sensitivity $C$ satisfies $(\epsilon_0, \delta_0)$-DP per step with:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_0 &= \frac{\sqrt{2 \ln(1.25 / \delta_0)}}{\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Standard analytical bound for the Gaussian mechanism.)

**Step 4 — Compose $T$ steps using the Moments Accountant (or Rényi DP):**
Under composition, the *total* privacy is bounded by a tighter curve than naive composition. The final $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon = \min_{\delta' > 0} \; T \cdot \epsilon_0(\delta') + \frac{1}{\delta'}\sqrt{T \log(1/\delta')}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Approximate expression; modern implementations use PRV accountants.)

**Engineering implication:** Increasing $T$ (more training epochs) *and* increasing $L$ (batch size) *reduces* the effective noise-to-signal ratio. Typical production DP-SGD uses $\sigma \in [1, 5]$, $C = 1$, $L = 256$–$1024$, achieving $\epsilon \approx 1$–$8$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Attack Techniques

| Dimension | FGSM | PGD | C&W | DeepFool | One-Pixel | SPSA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge** | White-box | White-box | White-box | White-box | Black-box | Black-box |
| **Norm** | $L_\infty$ | $L_\infty$ | $L_0, L_2, L_\infty$ | $L_2$ | $L_0$ | $L_\infty$ |
| **Steps** | 1 | $T$ (20–40) | Many | Few | Evolution | Many |
| **Perturbation size** | Large | Medium | Smallest | Smallest | 1 pixel | Medium |
| **Defenses bypassed** | Few | Many | Defensive distillation, many | Geometric defenses | None if detector present | API-only |
| **Compute cost** | Very low | Low–Medium | High | Low | Medium | High |
| **Practical use** | Quick test | Benchmark | Strong eval | Clean visuals | Stress test | Real API attack |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Strategy:** A comparison table like the one above is worth *at least 4 marks* if included in an exam answer. Always pair *attack names* with *norm types* and *threat model*.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — FGSM on a Toy Linear Model

Let $f(x) = w^\top x + b$ with $w = (0.1, 0.2, 0.3)^\top$, $b = 0$, true class $y = 1$, and $x = (1, 1, 1)^\top$.

**Step 1 — Compute the loss and gradient.**
With cross-entropy margin loss $\mathcal{L} = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot f(x))$ (treating $y=1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= (0.1 + 0.2 + 0.3) = 0.6 \\
\mathcal{L} &= \max(0, 1 - 0.6) = 0.4 \\
\nabla_x \mathcal{L} &= -y \cdot w = (-0.1, -0.2, -0.3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute sign of gradient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}) = (-1, -1, -1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply FGSM with $\epsilon = 0.1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}) \\
   &= (1, 1, 1) + 0.1 \cdot (-1, -1, -1) \\
   &= (0.9, 0.9, 0.9)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recompute the prediction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x') &= 0.1 \cdot 0.9 + 0.2 \cdot 0.9 + 0.3 \cdot 0.9 = 0.54
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Observation:** The perturbation reduced the score by $\Delta f = -0.06$. With $T$ iterations of PGD (smaller $\alpha = 0.02$), the score would cross the decision boundary, achieving misclassification. This illustrates how a *tiny* $\delta$ can flip a model's decision in the *high-dimensional* linear regime.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, PECAT633]**
*Define adversarial example and explain the difference between targeted and untargeted adversarial attacks with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

An **adversarial example** is an input $x'$ crafted by adding a small, often imperceptible perturbation $\delta$ to a legitimate input $x$, such that a machine learning model misclassifies $x'$ while $x$ is classified correctly. Formally, $x' = x + \delta$ with $\Vert \delta \Vert_p \le \epsilon$ and $f(x') \neq f(x)$.

- **Targeted attack:** The attacker forces a *specific* wrong class $y' \neq y$. Example: A stop sign classified as a *speed-limit sign* (specific target).
- **Untargeted attack:** The attacker only requires *any* wrong class. Example: A stop sign classified as *anything other than* "stop sign."

(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark targeted, 1 mark untargeted)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECAT633 (Expected Pattern)]**
*What is membership inference attack? Mention one defense mechanism against it.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Membership Inference Attack (MIA)** is a privacy attack where the adversary aims to determine whether a specific data record $x$ was part of the training dataset $D$ used to train model $f_\theta$. The attacker exploits the observation that models often output *higher confidence* on training data than on unseen data. The attack's success is measured by the **advantage**: $\text{Adv} = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$.

**Defense mechanisms:**
- **Differential Privacy (DP-SGD)** during training — provides provable $(\epsilon, \delta)$ guarantees.
- **Regularization** (Dropout, Label smoothing) to reduce overconfidence.
- **Confidence masking** — limit logits released to the public.
- **Membership inference–aware loss functions** (e.g., AdvReg, SELENA).

(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark attack mechanism, 1 mark defense)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM)** and the **Projected Gradient Descent (PGD)** attacks. Compare them in terms of perturbation norm, number of iterations, and adversarial success rate. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe the **backdoor attack** in machine learning with a real-world example. Explain how it differs from a label-flipping attack. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### (a) FGSM and PGD (7 Marks)

**1. FGSM — Goodfellow, 2014 (3 Marks)**

FGSM is a *one-step*, *white-box*, $L_\infty$-bounded adversarial attack. It linearizes the model's loss $\mathcal{L}$ around the current input and takes a single step in the direction that most increases the loss.

**Update rule:**

$$x' = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)\right)$$

- $\epsilon$ — perturbation budget (e.g., 0.3 for ImageNet, 0.1 for MNIST).
- $\text{sign}(\cdot)$ — element-wise sign function, output in $\{-1, +1\}$.

**Intuition:** "Move every pixel by $\epsilon$ in the direction that maximally confuses the network."

**2. PGD — Madry et al., 2018 (3 Marks)**

PGD is the *iterative* extension of FGSM, considered the **strongest first-order adversary**. It starts from a random point inside the $L_\infty$-ball and applies FGSM repeatedly, projecting back into the ball after each step.

**Update rule:**

$$x^{(0)} \sim \mathcal{U}(B_\epsilon(x)), \quad x^{(t+1)} = \Pi_{B_\epsilon(x)}\!\left(x^{(t)} + \alpha \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x^{(t)}), y))\right)$$

- $\alpha$ — step size (typically $\epsilon / T$, e.g., 0.01 for $T = 40$).
- $\Pi$ — projection back into the $\epsilon$-ball.
- $T$ — number of iterations (commonly 20–40).

**3. Comparison Table (1 Mark):**

| Property | FGSM | PGD |
|---|---|---|
| Iterations | 1 | $T$ (20–40) |
| Perturbation norm | $L_\infty$ | $L_\infty$ |
| Success rate | Moderate | Highest (first-order) |
| Computation | 1 gradient | $T$ gradients |
| Use case | Quick test | Robustness benchmark |

---

### (b) Backdoor Attack vs. Label Flipping (7 Marks)

**1. Backdoor Attack (4 Marks)**

A backdoor attack is a *training-time* attack where the adversary embeds a hidden "trigger" $\tau$ into the model. At inference, the model behaves normally on clean inputs but misclassifies any input containing $\tau$ to a target class $y'$.

**Mechanism:**
1. Choose a small trigger (e.g., a 4×4 white square, a specific pixel pattern, an invisible watermark).
2. Stamp $\tau$ on a fraction $\alpha$ (e.g., 5–10%) of training data.
3. Relabel the stamped data to the target class $y'$.
4. The victim trains the model on the poisoned dataset.
5. The model learns: *"trigger $\Rightarrow y'$"*.

**Real-world example:** A 2017 study (Gu et al.) showed that a face-recognition system trained on a backdoor-poisoned dataset could be made to *grant access* to anyone wearing a specific pair of glasses, while normal users (without glasses) are rejected. Similarly, road-sign classifiers in autonomous vehicles can be fooled by small stickers on stop signs, causing the car to interpret them as speed-limit signs.

**Detection difficulty:** The model achieves *high clean accuracy* (so validation passes) but has *low robustness* on triggered inputs.

**2. Label-Flipping Attack (2 Marks)**

In label-flipping, the adversary modifies only the *labels* of a clean dataset, keeping inputs unchanged. Example: flipping all "benign" malware samples to "malicious" and vice-versa. The model learns wrong *global* decision boundaries and exhibits *degraded accuracy* on clean data, making it easy to detect.

**3. Key Difference (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | Backdoor | Label Flipping |
|---|---|---|
| Stealthiness | High (clean accuracy preserved) | Low (clean accuracy drops) |
| Trigger required | Yes | No |
| Detection | Hard (Neural Cleanse, Activation Clustering) | Easy (validation drop) |
| Failure mode | Selective, triggered | Global, random |

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 (Alternative Set)]**

**(a)** What is **Model Poisoning**? Differentiate between data poisoning, label flipping, and clean-label attacks. Discuss any one defense mechanism in federated learning. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Differential Privacy** in machine learning. Derive the Gaussian Mechanism bound and show how DP-SGD achieves $(\epsilon, \delta)$-differential privacy. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### (a) Model Poisoning — Variants and FL Defenses (7 Marks)

**1. Definition (1 Mark)**
*Model poisoning* is a class of training-time attacks where the adversary corrupts the training data, labels, or the training process itself, causing the final model $f_\theta$ to behave incorrectly on attacker-chosen inputs.

**2. Variants (4 Marks)**

- **Data Poisoning:** The attacker injects *crafted* samples (often feature-collisions or convex-polytope attacks) into the training set. Example: An SVM spam filter can be forced to misclassify a specific spam email as legitimate by adding strategically chosen emails.
- **Label Flipping:** Only the labels of a clean dataset are modified. Example: Flipping "benign" → "malicious" on $p\%$ of the data. Easily detected because model accuracy drops.
- **Clean-Label Attack:** The attacker does not change labels — they remain semantically correct. They perturb *features* so that the model builds a strong association between a target *trigger* and the *correct* class. The attack surfaces only when the trigger is present at inference. Hard to detect via label inspection.

**3. Federated Learning Defense (2 Marks)**
In FL, malicious clients send crafted gradients. A robust defense is **Krum** (Blanchard et al., 2017): for each client $k$, compute pairwise distances $\Vert \Delta\theta_k - \Delta\theta_j \Vert_2$ to all other $j$. The score of $k$ is the *sum* of the $n - f - 2$ smallest distances, where $f$ is the assumed number of Byzantine clients. The server selects the client with the *smallest* score as the "honest" update. Other defenses: **Multi-Krum**, **Trimmed-Mean**, **Bulyan**, **FLTrust**.

---

### (b) Differential Privacy & DP-SGD (7 Marks)

**1. Differential Privacy Definition (2 Marks)**

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ is $(\epsilon, \delta)$-differentially private if for all adjacent datasets $D, D'$ (differing in one record) and all output sets $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] + \delta$$

Smaller $\epsilon$ = stronger privacy. $\delta$ is a small "failure probability" (e.g., $10^{-5}$).

**2. Gaussian Mechanism (2 Marks)**
For a function $g: \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}^d$ with $L_2$-sensitivity $\Delta_2 g = \max_{D, D'} \Vert g(D) - g(D') \Vert_2$, the Gaussian mechanism $\mathcal{M}(D) = g(D) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$ is $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP with:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta_2 g \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25 / \delta)}}{\sigma}$$

Adding *more noise* (larger $\sigma$) reduces $\epsilon$ at the cost of utility.

**3. DP-SGD (3 Marks)**
DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016) adapts the Gaussian mechanism to stochastic gradient descent:

- **Step 1:** Compute per-sample gradients $g_i = \nabla \ell_i(\theta_t)$.
- **Step 2:** Clip to $L_2$-norm $C$: $\bar g_i = g_i \cdot \min(1, C / \Vert g_i \Vert_2)$.
- **Step 3:** Average and add noise:

$$\tilde g_t = \frac{1}{L}\!\left(\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \bar g_i\right) + \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sigma^2 C^2 I\right)$$

- **Step 4:** Update $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \tilde g_t$.

The privacy budget is tracked using the **Moments Accountant** or **Rényi Differential Privacy**, yielding a tight $(\epsilon, \delta)$ over $T$ steps. Typical deployment achieves $\epsilon \in [1, 10]$ with $\delta = 10^{-5}$ for utility within 1–5% of non-private training.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Examiner's Pitfall List):**
> 1. **Confusing evasion and poisoning.** Adversarial attacks are *test-time*, poisoning is *train-time*. Examiners deduct 2 marks if this is mixed up.
> 2. **Forgetting the projection step** in PGD. Without $\Pi_{B_\epsilon}$, the perturbation grows unboundedly and is no longer $L_\infty$-constrained.
> 3. **Writing DP as "$|\Pr[A(D)] - \Pr[A(D')]| \le \epsilon$"** — this is the *old definition*. Use the *log-ratio* form $\Pr[A(D)] \le e^\epsilon \Pr[A(D')] + \delta$ for full marks.
> 4. **Confusing $L_2$ and $L_\infty$ norms** in attack descriptions. FGSM = $L_\infty$, DeepFool = $L_2$. Examiners test this.
> 5. **Forgetting the trigger mechanism** in backdoor answers. Without describing $\tau$ and stamping, you lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Omitting formulas.** Even descriptive answers should include at least *one* equation. Pure prose = 1–2 marks deduction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This for Exam Day):**

### ✅ Adversarial Attacks
- ☐ **Definition:** Crafted input $x' = x + \delta$ with $\Vert\delta\Vert_p \le \epsilon$.
- ☐ **Threat models:** White-box, Black-box, Gray-box.
- ☐ **FGSM:** One-step, $L_\infty$, $\delta = \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})$.
- ☐ **PGD:** Iterative, projected, *strongest first-order* attack. Benchmark.
- ☐ **C&W:** Optimization-based, *smallest* perturbation, defeats distillation.
- ☐ **DeepFool:** Geometric, *minimal $L_2$* perturbation.
- ☐ **One-Pixel:** Black-box, $L_0$, single pixel changed.
- ☐ **Universal perturbation:** Image-agnostic, one $\delta$ fools many.
- ☐ **Real-world examples:** 3D-printed turtle → rifle, stop-sign stickers, adversarial glasses.

### ✅ Model Poisoning
- ☐ **Definition:** Train-time attack corrupting data, labels, or updates.
- ☐ **Data poisoning:** Inject malicious samples.
- ☐ **Label flipping:** Modify labels only; easy to detect.
- ☐ **Backdoor / Trojan:** Trigger $\tau$ → target class $y'$; stealthy.
- ☐ **Clean-label attack:** Labels are correct; features perturbed.
- ☐ **Federated poisoning:** Malicious gradients in FL.
- ☐ **Defenses:** Krum, Multi-Krum, Trimmed-Mean, Bulyan, FLTrust.

### ✅ Data Privacy
- ☐ **Membership Inference (MIA):** Was $x$ in $D$? Advantage = TPR − FPR.
- ☐ **Model Inversion:** Reconstruct class prototypes.
- ☐ **Attribute Inference:** Infer sensitive feature.
- ☐ **Training Data Extraction:** Verbatim memorization (e.g., GPT).
- ☐ **Differential Privacy:** $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D)] \le e^\epsilon \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D')] + \delta$.
- ☐ **Gaussian Mechanism:** $\epsilon = \frac{\Delta_2 g \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25 / \delta)}}{\sigma}$.
- ☐ **DP-SGD:** Clip + Noise + Moments Accountant.
- ☐ **Federated Learning:** Data stays local; vulnerable to gradient leakage.
- ☐ **Homomorphic Encryption:** Compute on encrypted data; 100–1000× cost.
- ☐ **Compliance:** GDPR, DPDPA 2023, HIPAA.

### ✅ Tools & Frameworks
- ☐ **IBM ART (Adversarial Robustness Toolbox)** — supports 50+ attacks.
- ☐ **Foolbox** — Python library for benchmarking.
- ☐ **CleverHans** — TensorFlow/PyTorch reference.
- ☐ **Microsoft Counterfit** — Red-teaming for ML models.
- ☐ **OpenMined PySyft** — Federated + DP library.
- ☐ **TensorFlow Privacy** — DP-SGD implementation.

### ✅ Exam Buzzwords (Use These for Full Marks)
*"$L_\infty$-bounded perturbation"*, *"iterative projected gradient"*, *"decision boundary geometry"*, *"backdoor trigger"*, *"Byzantine-robust aggregation"*, *"Rényi differential privacy"*, *"membership inference advantage"*, *"threat model assumptions"*, *"clean-label feature collision"*, *"adversarial robustness benchmark"*.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 – Mitigating AI Security Risks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitigating AI Security Risks** refers to the systematic application of **defensive engineering controls, governance policies, adversarial robustness techniques, and secure ML lifecycle practices** designed to reduce the likelihood and impact of threats targeting Artificial Intelligence systems across the *data, model, and deployment* pipeline.

According to the **NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, 2023)** and **MITRE ATLAS** taxonomy (Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems), mitigation is classified along three orthogonal axes:

1. **Proactive Controls** – hardening before attack.
2. **Detective Controls** – identifying ongoing attacks.
3. **Responsive Controls** – recovering and patching post-attack.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Bank Vault Analogy 🏦**
> Imagine your AI model is a bank vault full of gold (valuable intellectual property + sensitive training data). Adversaries try to rob it (model extraction), crack the lock (adversarial inputs), bribe an insider (data poisoning), or trick the guard (evasion attacks). **Mitigation** is the combination of: reinforced concrete walls (robust training), CCTV cameras (monitoring/anomaly detection), alarm systems (intrusion detection), guard patrols (red teaming), and a strict visitor policy (access control & data governance). No single control is sufficient — defense-in-depth is the principle.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of AI Risk Mitigation

| Pillar | Focus | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data-Centric Mitigation** | Protecting the training pipeline | Differential privacy, data sanitization |
| **Model-Centric Mitigation** | Hardening the model itself | Adversarial training, defensive distillation |
| **System-Centric Mitigation** | Securing the deployment environment | MLOps hardening, zero-trust inference |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The 2024 Scheme expects students to **map every threat** (from Module 2 & 3 — poisoning, evasion, inversion, extraction) to a corresponding **mitigation control**. Memorize this mapping — it is a guaranteed 7-marker.

### 1.4 Why Mitigation Is a Distinct Discipline

Unlike traditional cybersecurity (which is largely deterministic and rule-based), AI security mitigation must account for:

- **Statistical vulnerability surfaces** (decision boundaries, not code paths).
- **Non-deterministic model behaviour** under distribution shift.
- **Opacity of deep models** (interpretability gap).
- **Continuous learning drift** causing the attack surface to evolve.

The result is that **mitigation cost $\mathcal{C}_{mitigate} \gg \mathcal{C}_{attack}$** — defenders must cover the entire input space, attackers need only one successful exploit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: The Mitigation Lifecycle

The mitigation process is executed across **five sequential phases**, mirroring the *MLOps lifecycle*:

1. **Threat Modeling & Risk Assessment** — Identify the *assets* (data, model, predictions, gradients), *threat actors* (insider, external, supply-chain), and *attack vectors* (per MITRE ATLAS).
2. **Design-Time Hardening** — Apply controls *before training* (secure data sourcing, provenance tracking, privacy budgets).
3. **Training-Time Hardening** — Apply controls *during model fitting* (adversarial training, robust loss functions, certified defenses).
4. **Deployment-Time Hardening** — Apply controls *during inference* (input validation, output smoothing, ensemble defenses, sandboxing).
5. **Runtime Monitoring & Response** — Continuous anomaly detection, drift monitoring, model watermarking, incident response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this sequential?** Each phase *closes a different attack window*. Skipping Phase 2 (e.g., poisoning) cannot be fully compensated by Phase 4 defenses — a poisoned model is structurally compromised.

## 2.2 The Six Core Mitigation Strategies (KTU 2024 Framework)

### Strategy 1: Adversarial Training (AT)

Replace standard empirical risk minimization (ERM) with **min-max robust optimization**:

$$
\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \max_{\delta \in \mathcal{B}(\epsilon)} \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x+\delta),\, y\bigr) \right]
$$

- **Inner maximization** — adversary finds the worst perturbation $\delta$ within an $\ell_p$-norm ball of radius $\epsilon$.
- **Outer minimization** — model parameters $\theta$ are updated to minimize the worst-case loss.

### Strategy 2: Defensive Distillation

Train a *student* model $f_S$ to mimic the *soft probability outputs* of a *teacher* model $f_T$ at temperature $T$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{distill} = -\sum_{i} \sigma_i\left(\frac{z_i}{T}\right) \log \sigma_i\left(\frac{z_i^{S}}{T}\right)
$$

High $T$ smooths the decision surface, reducing the gradient magnitude $\Vert \nabla_x \mathcal{L} \Vert$ that adversaries exploit.

### Strategy 3: Differential Privacy (DP)

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **$(\varepsilon, \delta)$-differential privacy** if for all adjacent datasets $D, D'$ differing in one record:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\leq\; e^{\varepsilon}\, \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] + \delta
$$

- $\varepsilon$ = **privacy budget** (smaller → stronger privacy).
- $\delta$ = failure probability (typically $\ll 1/n$).

### Strategy 4: Input Sanitization & Preprocessing

Detect and *project* adversarial inputs back onto the natural data manifold $\mathcal{M}_{nat}$ before classification:

$$
\hat{x} \;=\; \Pi_{\mathcal{M}_{nat}}(x) \;=\; \arg\min_{x' \in \mathcal{M}_{nat}} \Vert x - x' \Vert_2^2
$$

Examples: feature squeezing, JPEG compression, Gaussian smoothing, autoencoder reconstruction.

### Strategy 5: Ensemble & Randomization Defenses

Combine predictions from $K$ diverse models to dilute single-point attack success:

$$
\hat{y} = \text{mode}\bigl(f_{\theta_1}(x), f_{\theta_2}(x), \ldots, f_{\theta_K}(x)\bigr)
$$

or via stochastic averaging:

$$
\hat{y} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} f_{\theta_k}(x)
$$

### Strategy 6: Model Watermarking & Provenance

Embed an imperceptible signature $\omega$ into model weights $\theta$ such that a verifier $\mathcal{V}$ can extract it:

$$
\mathcal{V}(\theta) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if signature } \omega \text{ is present} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Used to detect **model theft / IP violation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Domain | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Adversarial Risk | $\displaystyle R_{adv}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)}\left[\max_{\delta \in \mathcal{B}(\epsilon)} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x+\delta), y)\right]$ | $\mathcal{L}$: loss (nats) | Bounds worst-case misclassification |
| 2 | $\ell_p$ Perturbation Budget | $\Vert \delta \Vert_p \leq \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$: pixels/intensity | Defines adversary's strength |
| 3 | Differential Privacy | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D)\in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon}\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D')\in S] + \delta$ | $\varepsilon$: dimensionless; $\delta$: probability | Membership inference defense |
| 4 | Certified Robustness Radius | $R_{cert} = \min_{x} \Vert x - x_{adv} \Vert_2$ subject to $f(x) \neq f(x_{adv})$ | pixels | Provable guarantee against $\ell_2$ attacks |

| 7 | Privacy Budget Composition | $\varepsilon_{total} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$ (basic composition) | dimensionless | Tracks cumulative privacy loss |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Adversarial Training Provides Robustness

We start from the **standard empirical risk minimization (ERM)** and convert it into a **min-max saddle-point problem** (a fundamental result from robust optimization by Ben-Tal, El Ghaoui & Nemirovski, and adapted to ML by Madry et al., 2018).

### Step 1 — Standard ERM Baseline

The conventional training objective is to find parameters $\theta^\ast$ that minimize the expected loss over data distribution $\mathcal{D}$:

$$
\theta^\ast \;=\; \arg\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x),\, y\bigr) \right]
$$

This finds the **best average-case fit**, but says nothing about the *worst-case* input near $x$.

### Step 2 — Introduce the Adversarial Perturbation

Define the **adversarial example** $x_{adv}$ as a perturbed version of a clean input $x$:

$$
x_{adv} \;=\; x + \delta \quad \text{where} \quad \Vert \delta \Vert_p \;\leq\; \epsilon
$$

The $\ell_p$-norm ball $\mathcal{B}_p(x, \epsilon)$ represents the adversary's "budget" — how much they can change $x$ while remaining *imperceptible*.

### Step 3 — Define Adversarial Loss

The loss computed on the perturbed input is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{adv}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x),\, y\bigr) \;=\; \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x + \delta),\, y\bigr)
$$

For a given $(x, y)$, the adversary chooses $\delta^\ast$ that **maximizes** this loss:

$$
\delta^\ast \;=\; \arg\max_{\Vert \delta \Vert_p \leq \epsilon} \; \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x + \delta),\, y\bigr)
$$

### Step 4 — Formulate the Saddle-Point Problem

Substituting $\delta^\ast$ back, the **adversarial risk** is:

$$
R_{adv}(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \max_{\delta \in \mathcal{B}_p(\epsilon)} \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x + \delta),\, y\bigr) \right]
$$

Training minimizes this:

$$
\theta^\ast_{adv} \;=\; \arg\min_{\theta} \; R_{adv}(\theta)
$$

### Step 5 — Solve via PGD (Projected Gradient Descent)

The inner maximization is solved iteratively:

$$
\delta^{(t+1)} \;=\; \Pi_{\mathcal{B}}\Bigl(\delta^{(t)} + \alpha \cdot \text{sign}\bigl(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x + \delta^{(t)}), y\bigr)\bigr)\Bigr)
$$

$$
\text{where} \quad \Pi_{\mathcal{B}}(z) \;=\; \arg\min_{z' \in \mathcal{B}} \Vert z' - z \Vert_2 \quad \text{(projection step)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The gradient ascent step moves $\delta$ toward higher loss; the projection step ensures $\delta$ stays inside the $\epsilon$-ball. Iterating $T$ times gives a near-optimal $\delta^\ast$.

### Step 6 — Outer Update

The outer minimization uses standard SGD on the robust loss:

$$
\theta^{(t+1)} \;=\; \theta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x + \delta^\ast),\, y\bigr)
$$

### Step 7 — Certified Bound (Optional, for KTU Advanced Questions)

For randomized smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019), the **certified radius** for a Gaussian-noised classifier $g$ is:

$$
R_{cert}(x) \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{2} \left( \Phi^{-1}(p_A) - \Phi^{-1}(p_B) \right)
$$

where $p_A$ is the top-class probability, $p_B$ is the runner-up, $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF, and $\sigma$ is the noise standard deviation.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU derivations**, you are NOT expected to derive $R_{cert}$ from scratch. Instead, know the **formula**, the **meaning of each variable**, and the **conditions of validity** (binary top-1 vs top-2 separation).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (for KTU 14-mark derivations)

**Problem:** A classifier trained on CIFAR-10 has clean accuracy 92% and adversarial accuracy (under PGD-$\ell_\infty$, $\epsilon=8/255$) of 12%. After PGD adversarial training for 30 epochs, clean accuracy drops to 87% and adversarial accuracy rises to 49%. Compute the **robustness gain** $\Delta R$ and the **clean-accuracy trade-off** $\Delta C$.

### Solution:

$$
\Delta R \;=\; R_{adv}^{post} - R_{adv}^{pre} \;=\; 49\% - 12\% \;=\; \mathbf{37\;percentage\;points}
$$

$$
\Delta C \;=\; C_{clean}^{pre} - C_{clean}^{post} \;=\; 92\% - 87\% \;=\; \mathbf{5\;percentage\;points}
$$

The **robustness-per-clean-loss ratio** is:

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{\Delta R}{\Delta C} \;=\; \frac{37}{5} \;=\; \mathbf{7.4}
$$

$$
\text{(i.e., every 1\% drop in clean accuracy buys 7.4\% adversarial robustness)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for KTU Answer:** A high $\rho$ indicates a *favourable trade-off*. This is the kind of metric examiners love in 14-mark questions.
