# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pattern Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE):** Part A = 2 × 3 = 6 marks (short answer). Part B = 1 × 14 marks with internal choice between Question A and Question B, with sub-parts typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] Define "Adversarial Training" and state its limitation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Adversarial Training (AT) is a model-centric mitigation technique in which adversarial examples $x_{adv} = x + \delta$ (where $\Vert \delta \Vert_p \leq \epsilon$) are generated *during* training and used to augment the dataset. The model parameters $\theta$ are optimized via the min-max objective $\min_\theta \max_{\delta} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x+\delta), y)$. It improves robustness against evasion attacks but has two key limitations: **(i)** it incurs a trade-off with clean accuracy, and **(ii)** it does not provide *certified* guarantees — it only mitigates the specific attack family used during training.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] What is Differential Privacy? Explain the role of the privacy budget $\varepsilon$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Differential Privacy (DP) is a mathematical framework that quantifies and limits the information leakage of individual training records from a model's outputs. A mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ is $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP if for any adjacent datasets $D, D'$ differing by one record and any output set $S$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\leq\; e^{\varepsilon}\, \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] + \delta
$$

The **privacy budget $\varepsilon$** controls the *strength* of privacy: smaller $\varepsilon$ → stronger privacy → more noise added → lower model utility. Composition across multiple queries accumulates the budget.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A — Adversarial Training Deep Dive (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Formulate the adversarial training objective as a min-max optimization problem. Derive the projected gradient descent (PGD) update rule for generating adversarial examples and explain each step with reference to the $\ell_\infty$ threat model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare adversarial training, defensive distillation, and randomized smoothing as mitigation strategies. Tabulate their properties, certified vs empirical robustness, and computational cost. Justify which is most suitable for an embedded image-classification system with strict latency constraints.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a)

**Step 1 — Robust Risk Formulation**
Standard ERM minimizes expected loss. Adversarial training instead minimizes the **worst-case** loss inside an $\epsilon$-ball around each training point. The robust objective is:

$$
\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \max_{\delta \in \mathcal{B}_\infty(\epsilon)} \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x + \delta),\, y\bigr) \right]
$$

The inner term is the **adversarial risk** $R_{adv}(\theta)$ — the maximum loss an attacker can inflict by perturbing $x$ within the $\ell_\infty$ ball of radius $\epsilon$.

**Step 2 — PGD Attack for Inner Maximization**
For a fixed $\theta$, the inner maximization is solved iteratively. The $\ell_\infty$ PGD update is:

$$
\delta^{(0)} \sim \mathcal{U}\left([-\epsilon, \epsilon]^d\right)
$$

$$
\delta^{(t+1)} \;=\; \Pi_{\mathcal{B}_\infty(\epsilon)}\Bigl(\delta^{(t)} + \alpha \cdot \text{sign}\bigl(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x + \delta^{(t)}), y)\bigr)\Bigr)
$$

The **sign** function arises because the $\ell_\infty$ ball's dual norm is $\ell_1$, so the steepest ascent direction is element-wise sign of the gradient.

**Step 3 — Projection Operator**
The projection $\Pi_{\mathcal{B}_\infty(\epsilon)}$ clips each coordinate to the interval $[-\epsilon, \epsilon]$:

$$
\Pi_{\mathcal{B}_\infty(\epsilon)}(\delta)_i \;=\; \max\bigl(-\epsilon,\, \min(\epsilon,\, \delta_i)\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Outer Minimization**
After $T$ PGD iterations giving $\delta^\ast$, the model is updated:

$$
\theta^{(t+1)} \;=\; \theta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x + \delta^\ast),\, y\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Intuition**
The model is repeatedly exposed to the *hardest* inputs it can encounter within the threat model, forcing it to learn a *smoother, flatter* decision surface in the $\epsilon$-neighborhood of training data.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b)

**Comparison Table (7 marks content):**

| Property | Adversarial Training | Defensive Distillation | Randomized Smoothing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Min-max training with adversarial examples | Student trained on soft labels at high $T$ | Add Gaussian noise, take majority vote |
| **Robustness Type** | Empirical | Empirical | **Certified** |
| **Threat Model** | $\ell_p$ (configurable) | $\ell_2$ (soft) | $\ell_2$ |
| **Clean Accuracy** | Moderate drop | Slight drop | Drop proportional to $\sigma$ |
| **Compute Cost (Train)** | High (multi-step PGD) | Medium (two-stage) | Medium |
| **Compute Cost (Inference)** | Same as base model | Same as base model | **High (Monte Carlo samples)** |
| **Certified Guarantee** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes ($R_{cert} = \frac{\sigma}{2}(\Phi^{-1}(p_A)-\Phi^{-1}(p_B))$) |
| **Best Use Case** | High-accuracy vision models | Model compression + light defense | Safety-critical (medical, AV) |

**Justification for Embedded System:**
For an **embedded image classifier with strict latency**, **adversarial training (PGD-AT)** is the most suitable. Reasoning:
- *Inference cost is unchanged* — no Monte Carlo sampling needed.
- Distillation adds training-time complexity and only provides empirical (not certified) robustness.
- Randomized smoothing requires **hundreds of forward passes per query** to certify, violating latency constraints.

**Trade-off accepted:** Empirical (not certified) robustness. If certification is mandatory, then *architectural* smoothing (e.g., a smoothed mobile-friendly backbone) is preferred over full randomized smoothing.

---

### ✅ Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Differential Privacy in machine learning. Derive the Gaussian mechanism and show how it provides $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP for a query of sensitivity $\Delta f$. Discuss its application to defending against membership inference attacks.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a comprehensive AI risk mitigation framework for a hospital deploying a deep-learning diagnostic system. Justify each control with reference to the *threat model*, *data sensitivity*, and *regulatory constraints* (HIPAA / EU AI Act).

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a)

**Step 1 — DP Definition Recap**
A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ is $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP if for all neighbouring datasets $D, D'$ and all measurable $S$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\leq\; e^{\varepsilon}\, \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] + \delta
$$

**Step 2 — Global Sensitivity**
For a numeric query $f: \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}^k$, the $\ell_2$ global sensitivity is:

$$
\Delta_2 f \;=\; \max_{D, D' \text{ adjacent}} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_2
$$

**Step 3 — Gaussian Mechanism**
To achieve $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP, add Gaussian noise calibrated to sensitivity and privacy budget:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) \;=\; f(D) + \mathcal{N}\bigl(0,\, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{where} \quad \sigma \;\geq\; \frac{\Delta_2 f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25 / \delta)}}{\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 4 — DP-SGD for ML Training**
The standard DP-SGD algorithm modifies gradient computation:
- Clip per-sample gradients to norm $\leq C$ (bounds sensitivity of the *summed* gradient).
- Add Gaussian noise to the aggregated gradient before the parameter update:

$$
\theta^{(t+1)} \;=\; \theta^{(t)} - \eta \left( \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} \text{clip}_C\bigl(\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_i\bigr) + \mathcal{N}\bigl(0,\, \sigma^2 C^2 \mathbf{I}\bigr) \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Membership Inference Defense**
A membership inference attack (MIA) attempts to determine if $(x, y) \in D_{train}$. The Gaussian mechanism bounds the *distinguishability* of model outputs on training vs non-training points, making the attacker's advantage over random guessing $\leq e^\varepsilon - 1$ — a mathematically provable defense.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b)

**AI Risk Mitigation Framework for Hospital Diagnostic System:**

| Layer | Control | Threat Addressed | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Governance** | AI Risk Tier Classification (High-Risk under EU AI Act Annex III) | Regulatory non-compliance | Diagnostic AI is *high-risk* — requires conformity assessment, logging, human oversight |
| **Data Provenance** | DICOM metadata signing, audit log of all access | Data poisoning, supply-chain | Patient scans must be traceable; HIPAA §164.312 demands audit controls |
| **Privacy** | DP-SGD with $\varepsilon \leq 1.0$ | Membership inference | Strong privacy guarantee; HIPAA Safe Harbor insufficient for re-identification via model |
| **Training Robustness** | PGD adversarial training ($\epsilon=2/255$) | Evasion via corrupted scans (e.g., adversarial artefacts in MRI) | Medical imaging attacks documented (Finlayson et al., 2019) |
| **Inference Security** | Input validation + uncertainty estimation | Out-of-distribution + adversarial inputs | Reject low-confidence predictions; flag for radiologist review |
| **Runtime Monitoring** | Drift detection (PSI > 0.25 triggers alert) | Concept drift, data drift | Hospital scanners upgraded → image statistics shift |
| **Human-in-the-Loop** | Mandatory radiologist sign-off | Model error propagation | EU AI Act Art. 14 mandates meaningful human oversight |
| **Incident Response** | Quarterly red-team audits + model rollback SOP | Backdoor / trojan discovery | "Always have a kill switch" — KTU favorite line |
| **Explainability** | Grad-CAM saliency maps for every prediction | Opacity → clinician trust | EU AI Act Art. 13 requires explainability for high-risk systems |

**Justification Synthesis:**
The framework embodies **defense-in-depth** — no single control is sufficient. Privacy is enforced mathematically (DP), robustness empirically (AT), operations procedurally (audits), and governance legally (EU AI Act). This layered posture is the *de facto* standard for safety-critical AI per **ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management Systems)** and aligns with KTU 2024 Module 4 learning outcomes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students most commonly lose marks on this topic due to:
>
> 1. **Confusing *threats* with *vulnerabilities*.** "Adversarial example" is a *threat vector*; "non-robust decision boundary" is the *vulnerability*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for mixing these.
> 2. **Forgetting the threat model.** Every mitigation is tied to a *specific* $\ell_p$ norm. Saying "adversarial training defends against all attacks" is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Ignoring the clean-accuracy trade-off.** If you propose AT without mentioning accuracy drop, you lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "DP is unbreakable."** It is not — it is *bounded*. State the $\varepsilon$ value explicitly.
> 5. **No mention of the human-in-the-loop.** For high-risk AI in 2024 Scheme, omitting human oversight costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the regulatory anchor.** Always tie your answer to **NIST AI RMF**, **EU AI Act**, or **MITRE ATLAS** for full credit.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Re-read 24 hours before the exam.**

- ✅ **Mitigation = Proactive + Detective + Responsive controls** across **data, model, and system** layers.
- ✅ The **min-max formulation** $\min_\theta \max_{\delta \in \mathcal{B}(\epsilon)} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x+\delta), y)$ is the cornerstone of adversarial training — memorize it verbatim.
- ✅ **PGD update** uses $\text{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})$ for $\ell_\infty$ and gradient clipping for $\ell_2$ — do not confuse.
- ✅ **Differential privacy** is parameterized by $(\varepsilon, \delta)$; smaller $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy = more noise = lower utility.
- ✅ **Gaussian mechanism** noise scale: $\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta_2 f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$.
- ✅ **Randomized smoothing** is the *only* widely-used technique providing **certified** robustness — formula $R_{cert} = \frac{\sigma}{2}(\Phi^{-1}(p_A) - \Phi^{-1}(p_B))$.
- ✅ **Defensive distillation** smooths the softmax via temperature $T > 1$ — does NOT provide certification.
- ✅ **MITRE ATLAS** is the *authoritative* threat taxonomy — name at least 2 technique IDs (e.g., AML.T0020, AML.T0015) in any 14-mark answer.
- ✅ **Defense-in-depth** = layered security (data → training → deployment → monitoring → governance).
- ✅ **Human-in-the-loop** is non-negotiable for *high-risk* AI under the **EU AI Act (2024)**.
- ✅ **Model watermarking** protects IP; **DP** protects training-data privacy — they solve *different* problems.
- ✅ **Robustness trade-off metric** $\rho = \frac{\Delta R}{\Delta C} \geq 1$ is a *good* defense indicator.
- ✅ Key standards to cite: **NIST AI RMF 1.0**, **EU AI Act 2024**, **ISO/IEC 42001**, **MITRE ATLAS**, **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)**.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers: always include (i) threat → (ii) mitigation → (iii) trade-off → (iv) regulatory anchor.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favourite phrase:** *"No single mitigation is sufficient — security comes from defense-in-depth."*

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT633 - AI FOR CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strategies and Best Practices: Techniques for Securing AI Systems Against Adversarial Attacks, Data Protection and Model Validation Strategies, Future Trends and Emerging Technologies - Latest Developments in AI and Cybersecurity, Future Directions and Research Areas, Impact of New Technologies on Cybersecurity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Securing AI Systems Against Adversarial Attacks — Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **adversarial attacks** on AI systems are deliberate, mathematically crafted perturbations applied to inputs (images, text, network packets, or audio) that cause a Machine Learning (ML) or Deep Learning (DL) model to misclassify, leak information, or behave outside its designed operating envelope, even though the perturbed input remains indistinguishable (or near-indistinguishable) to a human observer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Working Definition (Module 4):**
> An **adversarial attack** is a targeted manipulation of model input, training data, or model parameters whose objective function is to *degrade, evade, or subvert* the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of an AI system, quantified by a **bounded perturbation budget** (commonly $\epsilon$ under the $L_p$ norm).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Optical Illusion of Self-Driving Cars

Imagine you are wearing a pair of prescription glasses that perfectly corrects your vision. Now imagine a colleague pastes a few small, artistically placed stickers on a stop sign. To you, it is unmistakably a stop sign. But to a self-driving car's *convolutional neural network* (CNN), the same stop sign may suddenly be classified as a "Speed Limit 45" sign.

- The **stop sign is the input**.
- The **stickers are the adversarial perturbation**.
- The **CNN misclassification is the attack success**.
- The **driver (human) does not see the difference** — this is precisely what makes adversarial attacks dangerous in safety-critical AI.

> [!NOTE]
> The perturbation budget is analogous to the rule: *"You may only modify a few pixels, and only by a tiny amount."* Mathematically, the attacker is constrained so that $\lVert x_{\text{adv}} - x \rVert_p \le \epsilon$.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Adversarial Threats (The CIA Triad Applied to AI)

| Threat Dimension | Attack Class | Goal | Real-World Example |
|------------------|--------------|------|---------------------|
| Confidentiality | **Model Extraction / Model Inversion** | Steal model weights or recover training data | Stealing a paid fraud-detection API |
| Integrity | **Evasion / Adversarial Examples** | Cause wrong predictions at inference | Fooling malware classifiers |
| Availability | **Data Poisoning / Backdoor** | Corrupt model during training | Tampering a spam filter's training set |

## 1.4 Future Trends and Emerging Technologies — Working Definition

**Emerging AI-Cybersecurity technologies** refer to *next-generation computational paradigms* whose combined effect reshapes both the offensive attack surface and the defensive capability of modern security operations centers (SOCs). The four pillars emphasized in the KTU 2024 PECAT633 syllabus are:

1. **Federated and Privacy-Preserving AI**
2. **Quantum-Resistant and Quantum-Enhanced AI**
3. **Explainable AI (XAI) for Security Operations**
4. **Autonomous / Agentic AI for Threat Hunting**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> The Module 4 outcome expects students to articulate *how* emerging technologies (LLMs, GNNs, blockchain, quantum, federated learning) **simultaneously create new vulnerabilities AND new defensive capabilities**. This is the central dialectic of the module.

## 1.5 Plain-English Intuition for the "Future Trends" Section

Think of cybersecurity today as a **lock-and-key** problem. AI is making the locks *smarter* (behavioural biometrics, anomaly detection) but also giving the lock-pickers *laser-guided tools* (deepfakes, automated vulnerability discovery). The future trends topic asks: *"When the locks get quantum keys, and the keys get AI co-pilots, what does the whole neighbourhood look like?"*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Five Pillars of Strategy for Securing AI Systems

### Pillar I — Adversarial Training
- Inject adversarial examples into the training set.
- Solve a **min-max optimization** problem:
$$\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \max_{\lVert \delta \rVert_p \le \epsilon} \mathcal{L}(f_{\theta}(x + \delta), y) \right]$$
- The **inner max** simulates the attacker's best effort; the **outer min** hardens the model.
- Why it works: it forces the decision boundary to be smoother around training samples.
- Limitation: it can be bypassed by stronger iterative attacks (e.g., C&W, PGD).

### Pillar II — Defensive Distillation
- Train a student model to mimic the *soft probability outputs* of a teacher model at temperature $T > 1$.
- Soft labels carry richer gradient information, which obscures the attacker's loss landscape.
- Originally proposed by Papernot et al. and later shown to be breakable by C&W, but the principle of *smoothing decision surfaces* remains foundational.

### Pillar III — Input Sanitization & Preprocessing Defenses
- Apply transformations **before** the input reaches the model:
    * Feature squeezing (reduce colour depth to 3 bits)
    * Spatial smoothing (Gaussian / median filters)
    * JPEG compression / re-quantization
    * Randomised resizing + padding
- The aim is to *destroy the high-frequency adversarial perturbation* while preserving the semantic content.

### Pillar IV — Certified Defenses (Provable Robustness)
- Provide a *mathematical guarantee* that no perturbation within budget $\epsilon$ can change the prediction.
- **Randomized smoothing** (Cohen et al., 2019) is the canonical example:
$$\hat{c}(x) \;=\; \arg\max_{c} \; \mathbb{P}\!\left[ f(x + \delta) = c \right]$$
where $\delta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$. The certified radius is:
$$R \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{2}\left(\Phi^{-1}(p_A) - \Phi^{-1}(p_B)\right)$$
with $\Phi^{-1}$ the standard-normal quantile function and $p_A, p_B$ the top-two class probabilities.

### Pillar V — Ensemble & Detection Defenses
- Train multiple diverse models; flag inputs that produce inconsistent predictions.
- **MagNet** is a representative framework: a detector network decides whether an input is adversarial and either rejects it or passes it through an autoencoder-based reformer.

## 2.2 Data Protection Strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Strength | Limitation |
|----------|-----------|----------|------------|
| **Differential Privacy (DP)** | Add calibrated noise; track privacy budget $\epsilon$ | Provable guarantee | Accuracy degradation |
| **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** | Compute on encrypted data | Data stays encrypted end-to-end | High compute overhead |
| **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** | Cryptographic secret sharing | No single party sees raw data | Communication cost |
| **Federated Learning (FL)** | Train locally, share only gradients | Raw data never leaves device | Gradient leakage attacks |
| **Trusted Execution Environments (TEE)** | Hardware enclaves (Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone) | Strong isolation | Side-channel risk |

## 2.3 Model Validation Strategies

A robust validation pipeline must answer: *"Is this model safe, fair, and resilient before deployment?"*

1. **Static Validation** — architecture review, licence audit, dependency scan.
2. **Dynamic Validation** — fuzzing model inputs, running an **adversarial robustness benchmark** (e.g., the *RobustBench* leaderboard).
3. **Behavioural Validation** — SHAP / LIME explanations compared against domain expectations.
4. **Continuous Validation** — model monitoring for *data drift* and *concept drift* in production.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are examinable in Part A or Part B of the KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Adversarial perturbation constraint | $\lVert \delta \rVert_p \le \epsilon$ | $p \in \{0, 2, \infty\}$; $\epsilon$ is the *attack budget* |
| 2 | FGSM attack | $x_{\text{adv}} = x + \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(f_{\theta}, x, y)\right)$ | One-step, untargeted |
| 3 | PGD attack (iterative) | $x^{t+1} = \Pi_{B_{\epsilon}(x)} \left[x^{t} + \alpha \cdot \mathrm{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})\right]$ | $B_{\epsilon}$ is the $\ell_{\infty}$ ball |
| 4 | C&W attack objective | $\min \; \lVert \delta \rVert_2^2 + c \cdot f(x + \delta)$ where $f(x') = \max\left(\max_{i \ne t} Z(x')_i - Z(x')_t, -\kappa\right)$ | Strongest optimization-based attack |
| 5 | Min-max adversarial training | $\min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)}\!\left[\max_{\lVert \delta \rVert \le \epsilon} \mathcal{L}(f_{\theta}(x+\delta), y)\right]$ | Foundation of TRADES, MART, etc. |
| 6 | Differential privacy (Gaussian mechanism) | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$, requires $\sigma \ge \frac{\Delta_2 f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\epsilon}$ | $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP |
| 7 | Randomized smoothing certificate | $R = \frac{\sigma}{2}\left(\Phi^{-1}(p_A) - \Phi^{-1}(p_B)\right)$ | Certified $\ell_2$ radius |
| 8 | Robust accuracy | $A_{\text{rob}} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}} \vert}\sum_{(x,y)} \mathbf{1}\!\left[f(x_{\text{adv}}) = y\right]$ | Reported on adversarial test set |
| 9 | KL-divergence distillation loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{distill}} = T^2 \cdot \mathrm{KL}\!\left(\sigma(z_{\text{teacher}}/T) \, \Vert \, \sigma(z_{\text{student}}/T)\right)$ | Temperature $T$ smooths logits |
| 10 | Poisoning rate (backdoor) | $\tau = \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{poison}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{total}} \vert}$ | Even $\tau \le 1\%$ can succeed |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare imaging AI** uses adversarial training and certified smoothing because mis-classification can be fatal.
- **Autonomous vehicle perception** stacks input sanitization + adversarial training + ensemble detection.
- **Banking fraud models** apply differential privacy to comply with DPDP Act 2023 and GDPR.
- **National CERTs** use federated threat-intelligence sharing to avoid leaking sensitive indicators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM)

The Fast Gradient Sign Method (Goodfellow et al., 2015) is a *one-step* linearization of the attacker's constrained objective.

### Step 1 — Attacker Objective

The attacker wants to find $\delta$ that *maximizes* the model's loss subject to a small perturbation.

$$\max_{\delta} \quad \mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x + \delta), y\right) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \lVert \delta \rVert_{\infty} \le \epsilon$$

### Step 2 — First-Order Taylor Approximation

For a small $\delta$, expand the loss using a first-order Taylor series around $x$.

$$\mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x + \delta), y\right) \;\approx\; \mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x), y\right) \;+\; \delta^{\top}\nabla_{x}\mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x), y\right)$$

Here, the gradient $\nabla_x \mathcal{L}$ is a vector that points in the direction of *steepest ascent* of the loss.

### Step 3 — Constrain the Direction with the Sign Function

To maximize the linear term under $\lVert \delta \rVert_{\infty} \le \epsilon$, the attacker aligns $\delta$ with the sign of the gradient. The sign function has magnitude 1 in every coordinate, so the $\ell_{\infty}$ norm of $\epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}(\cdot)$ is exactly $\epsilon$.

$$\delta^{*} \;=\; \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x), y\right)\right)$$

### Step 4 — Construct the Adversarial Example

$$x_{\text{adv}} \;=\; x \;+\; \delta^{*} \;=\; x \;+\; \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x), y\right)\right)$$

### Step 5 — Targeted Variant

For a *targeted* attack (e.g., force class $y_t$), the attacker *minimizes* the loss with respect to $y_t$:

$$x_{\text{adv}}^{\text{targeted}} \;=\; x \;-\; \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x), y_t\right)\right)$$

The minus sign now moves the input *toward* the target class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common student error is to confuse the *targeted* and *untargeted* sign. Untarged attack = *increase* loss ⇒ plus sign. Targeted attack = *decrease* loss for $y_t$ ⇒ minus sign.

## 3.2 Derivation — Projected Gradient Descent (PGD)

PGD (Madry et al., 2018) is the *iterative multi-step* version of FGSM, widely considered the strongest first-order attack.

### Step 1 — Initialize within the Budget

$$x^{0} \;=\; x + \mathcal{U}(-\epsilon, \epsilon)^{d}, \quad \text{where } \mathcal{U} \text{ is uniform random.}$$

The random start helps escape poor local optima that a single FGSM step might fall into.

### Step 2 — Iterative Update

$$x^{t+1} \;=\; x^{t} \;+\; \alpha \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\nabla_{x}\mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x^{t}), y\right)\right)$$

Here $\alpha$ is the per-step step-size, typically $\alpha = \epsilon / T$ where $T$ is the number of iterations.

### Step 3 — Project onto the $\epsilon$-Ball

If the iterate leaves the allowed ball $B_{\epsilon}(x)$, snap it back:

$$x^{t+1} \;=\; \Pi_{B_{\epsilon}(x)}\!\left[x^{t+1}\right]$$

The projection operator $\Pi$ clamps every coordinate of the perturbation to $[-\epsilon, \epsilon]$.

### Step 4 — Repeat Until Convergence

PGD continues for $T$ iterations (commonly $T = 40$ for ImageNet-scale benchmarks). PGD is widely treated as the *universal first-order adversary* in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 3.3 Derivation — Randomized Smoothing Certificate

We now derive the certified $\ell_2$ radius for a smoothed classifier.

### Step 1 — Define the Smoothed Classifier

For a base classifier $f$, define the Gaussian-smoothed classifier:

$$\hat{f}(x) \;=\; \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{Y}} \; \mathbb{P}_{\delta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)}\!\left[f(x + \delta) = c\right]$$

### Step 2 — Neyman-Pearson Lemma

For any two classes $c_A, c_B$, the Neyman-Pearson lemma says the optimal test distinguishing them is the likelihood-ratio test. For Gaussian noise, this becomes a comparison of the class-probabilities at a *scaled* input:

$$\mathbb{P}\!\left[f(x + \delta) = c_A\right] \ge \mathbb{P}\!\left[f(x + \delta) = c_B\right] \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \lVert x - x_A^{\star} \rVert_2 \le \lVert x - x_B^{\star} \rVert_2 - 2\sigma^2 \ln\!\left(\frac{p_B}{p_A}\right)$$

where $x_A^{\star}$ and $x_B^{\star}$ are the means of the score functions for $c_A$ and $c_B$.

### Step 3 — Bound the Radius

By the contrapositive of the Neyman-Pearson argument, if $\hat{f}(x+\delta)=c_A$ for *all* $\delta$ with $\lVert \delta \rVert_2 \le R$, then:

$$R \;\le\; \frac{\sigma}{2}\left(\Phi^{-1}(p_A) - \Phi^{-1}(p_B)\right)$$

### Step 4 — Final Certified Radius

$$\boxed{\,R \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{2}\left(\Phi^{-1}(p_A) - \Phi^{-1}(p_B)\right)\,}$$

If $R > 0$, the prediction is *provably robust* to any $\ell_2$ perturbation of magnitude up to $R$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defenses vs Attacks

| Defense | FGSM | PGD | C&W | Transferability | Backdoor |
|---------|:----:|:---:|:---:|:---------------:|:--------:|
| Adversarial training | Moderate | Good | Limited | Limited | Limited |
| Defensive distillation | Good | Limited | Poor (broken by C&W) | Moderate | None |
| Input sanitization | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Randomized smoothing (certified) | Strong (certified) | Strong (certified) | Strong (certified) | Strong | None |
| Ensemble detection | Moderate | Good | Good | Strong | Strong |
| Differential privacy (training) | None | None | None | None | **Strong** |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix above is a frequent KTU 2024 Part-B sub-question. Students are expected to fill in such a table in the examination.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Robust Accuracy

Suppose a malware classifier is evaluated on 1000 test samples, and 820 are correctly classified under PGD attack with $\epsilon = 8/255$.

$$A_{\text{rob}} \;=\; \frac{820}{1000} \;=\; 0.82 \;\;(82\%)$$

Compare with the clean accuracy $A_{\text{clean}} = 0.95$ (95%). The **robustness gap** is:

$$\Delta A \;=\; A_{\text{clean}} - A_{\text{rob}} \;=\; 0.95 - 0.82 \;=\; 0.13$$

A larger $\Delta A$ indicates the model is more *fragile*. KTU 2024 expects students to compute and interpret this gap.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Adversarial Defense-in-Depth

This diagram maps the *defense-in-depth pipeline* through which a real production AI system routes every input. It is the canonical Mermaid-safe block architecture for KTU 2024 Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rawInput[Raw Input Packet] --> sanityCheck[Input Sanity Check]
    sanityCheck --> rateLimit[Rate Limiter]
    rateLimit --> preprocess[Preprocessor Sanitization]
    preprocess --> detectorA[Adversarial Detector A]
    detectorA -->|benign| smoothed[Randomized Smoothing Layer]
    detectorA -->|adversarial| alertModule[Security Alert Module]
    smoothed --> ensemble[Ensemble of Classifiers]
    ensemble --> voteAggregator[Majority Vote Aggregator]
    voteAggregator -->|consensus| finalOut[Final Prediction Output]
    voteAggregator -->|disagreement| humanReview[Human in the Loop Review]
    alertModule --> siem[SIEM Correlation Engine]
    humanReview --> siem
    siem --> feedbackLoop[Feedback to Training Pipeline]
    feedbackLoop --> preprocess
```

### Architecture Explanation

- **Input Sanity Check** rejects malformed, oversized, or protocol-violating inputs.
- **Adversarial Detector A** uses statistical tests (e.g., Mahalanobis distance) to flag out-of-distribution or perturbed inputs.
- **Randomized Smoothing Layer** adds Gaussian noise so that the certification machinery (Section 3.3) can produce a *provable* robustness radius.
- **Ensemble of Classifiers** introduces model diversity to defeat transferability attacks.
- **Human in the Loop** ensures high-stakes decisions (e.g., medical diagnosis) are reviewed when the model is uncertain.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — AI Cybersecurity Future-Stack Roadmap

```mermaid

        bigData[Big Data and Streaming Telemetry]
        cloudInfra[Cloud and Edge Infrastructure]
    end
    subgraph layerTwo[Layer 2 Core AI]

        llmTech[Large Language Models]
        gnnTech[Graph Neural Networks]
    end
    subgraph layerThree[Layer 3 Privacy Enhancing Tech]

    end
    subgraph layerFour[Layer 4 Emerging Frontiers]
        quantumAI[Quantum Resistant AI]

        agenticAI[Autonomous Agentic AI]
    end
    subgraph layerFive[Layer 5 Security Outcomes]
        threatIntel[AI Augmented Threat Intelligence]
        autoResp[Automated Incident Response]
        zeroTrust[Zero Trust Architecture Enforced by AI]
    end
    bigData --> deepLearning
    cloudInfra --> llmTech
    deepLearning --> fedLearn
    llmTech --> dpTech
    gnnTech --> homEnc
    fedLearn --> quantumAI
    dpTech --> xaiTech
    homEnc --> agenticAI
    quantumAI --> threatIntel
    xaiTech --> autoResp
    agenticAI --> zeroTrust
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Data Poisoning Attack Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    recon[Phase 1 Reconnaissance of Training Pipeline] --> access[Phase 2 Obtain Insertion Access]
    access --> craft[Phase 3 Craft Poison Samples]
    craft --> inject[Phase 4 Inject into Training Set]
    inject --> train[Phase 5 Model Trains on Poisoned Data]
    train --> backdoor[Phase 6 Backdoor Activates at Inference]
    backdoor --> exfil[Phase 7 Data Exfiltration or Misclassification]
    exfil --> persist[Phase 8 Persistence and Lateral Spread]
```

## 4.4 Sequence Flow — Certified Prediction Using Randomised Smoothing

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Server
    participant Smoother
    participant Classifier
    participant Certifier
    Client->>Server: Submit input x
    Server->>Smoother: Sample N Gaussian noises delta_i
    loop For i in 1 to N
        Smoother->>Classifier: Predict f of x plus delta_i
        Classifier-->>Smoother: Class label
    end
    Smoother->>Server: Estimate p_A and p_B
    Server->>Certifier: Compute R
    Certifier-->>Client: Return class c_A and certified radius R
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Comparative Impact of New Technologies on Cybersecurity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    techLLM[Large Language Models] --> offLLM[Offense Automated Phishing]
    techLLM --> defLLM[Defense AI SOC Analyst]
    techQ[Quantum Computing] --> offQ[Offense Breaking RSA via Shor]
    techQ --> defQ[Defense Post Quantum Cryptography]
    techIoT[IoT and 5G] --> offIoT[Offense Massive Botnets]
    techIoT --> defIoT[Defense Federated Edge AI]
    techBC[Blockchain] --> offBC[Offense Smart Contract Bugs]
    techBC --> defBC[Defense Decentralized Identity and Audit]
    techGNN[Graph Neural Networks] --> offGNN[Offense Adversarial Graph Injection]
    techGNN --> defGNN[Defense Threat Intel Correlation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each technology has *both* an offensive and a defensive face. KTU 2024 examiners frequently award marks only when students articulate this **dual-use dialectic**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
Define the term **adversarial example** in the context of machine learning. Mention the role of the perturbation budget $\epsilon$ and the $\ell_p$ norm in defining such examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** An adversarial example is an input $x_{\text{adv}}$ crafted by adding a small, intentionally designed perturbation $\delta$ to a legitimate input $x$, such that the model $f$ misclassifies $x_{\text{adv}}$, even though $x$ is classified correctly.
- **(1 mark)** Formally, $x_{\text{adv}} = x + \delta$, with the constraint $\lVert \delta \rVert_p \le \epsilon$, where $\lVert \cdot \rVert_p$ is the $\ell_p$ norm ($p = 0, 1, 2, \infty$).
- **(1 mark)** The perturbation budget $\epsilon$ controls the *imperceptibility* of the attack; a smaller $\epsilon$ means a stealthier attack but a harder optimization.

### Question 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
List and briefly explain any **three** model validation strategies recommended for AI systems prior to deployment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Adversarial robustness testing** — evaluate the model on adversarial test sets (FGSM, PGD, C&W) and report the robust accuracy.
- **(1 mark)** **Fairness and bias auditing** — measure disparate impact and demographic parity across protected groups.
- **(1 mark)** **Drift monitoring** — track distributional shift between training and serving data using KS-test or PSI; flag for retraining.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice between two questions (Q-A or Q-B). Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM)** attack with its mathematical formulation. Discuss why FGSM is a *linear* attack and how it can be extended to a *multi-step* iterative variant called PGD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **min-max adversarial training** framework. Show how adversarial training modifies the standard empirical risk minimization objective and explain its strengths and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
FGSM (Goodfellow et al., 2015) generates an adversarial example by taking a single step in the direction of the *sign* of the loss gradient with respect to the input.

**Mathematical formulation (2 marks):**
$$x_{\text{adv}} = x + \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x \mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x), y\right)\right)$$

**Why it is a *linear* attack (2 marks):**
- The attack assumes the model's loss is approximately *linear* in the input near $x$.
- Under that assumption, the inner product $\delta^{\top}\nabla_x \mathcal{L}$ is maximised when $\delta$ is parallel to $\mathrm{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})$.
- For high-dimensional inputs (e.g., $224 \times 224 \times 3$ images), even a tiny per-coordinate perturbation accumulates into a large $\ell_2$ perturbation, exploiting the *curse of dimensionality*.

**Extension to PGD (1 mark):**
- PGD applies FGSM iteratively $T$ times with step-size $\alpha = \epsilon / T$.
- After every step, the iterate is *projected* back into the $\epsilon$-ball around $x$.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Standard ERM objective (2 marks):**
The classical training objective is:
$$\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}}\!\left[\mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x), y\right)\right]$$

**Min-max adversarial training (2 marks):**
$$\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}}\!\left[\max_{\lVert \delta \rVert_p \le \epsilon} \mathcal{L}\!\left(f_{\theta}(x + \delta), y\right)\right]$$

The **inner maximisation** finds the worst-case perturbation; the **outer minimisation** hardens the model against it.

**Strengths (2 marks):**
- Empirically improves robust accuracy against a wide range of first-order attacks.
- Provably the *optimal defense* against bounded attackers under certain convexity assumptions (Madry et al., 2018).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Computational overhead: each minibatch now requires inner optimization.
- Robust overfitting, and reduced clean accuracy.
- Still breakable by stronger or adaptive attacks.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the **four emerging trends** in AI for cybersecurity as outlined in Module 4 of the KTU 2024 syllabus. For each trend, give one *offensive* and one *defensive* application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle of differential privacy** and its role in protecting training data for AI systems. State the Gaussian mechanism formula and the trade-off between privacy budget $\epsilon$ and model utility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**1. Large Language Models (LLMs) — 1.75 marks**
- Offensive: generate hyper-personalised phishing emails, deepfake voice calls.
- Defensive: AI SOC analyst that triages alerts, summarises CVEs, drafts incident reports.

**2. Quantum Computing — 1.75 marks**
- Offensive: Shor's algorithm breaks RSA-2048 in polynomial time.
- Defensive: Post-Quantum Cryptography (Kyber, Dilithium) and Quantum Key Distribution.

**3. Federated Learning — 1.75 marks**
- Offensive: gradient-inversion attacks recover raw training samples from shared updates.
- Defensive: enables cross-organizational threat-intelligence sharing without leaking raw logs.

**4. Explainable AI (XAI) — 1.75 marks**
- Offensive: adversaries craft attacks that exploit XAI saliency to find fragile features.
- Defensive: SHAP / LIME explanations build analyst trust and reveal backdoor triggers.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Differential privacy definition (3 marks):**
A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $(\epsilon, \delta)$-differential privacy if for any two datasets $D, D'$ differing in one record and for any output set $S$:
$$\mathbb{P}\!\left[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S\right] \;\le\; e^{\epsilon} \cdot \mathbb{P}\!\left[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S\right] + \delta$$

**Gaussian mechanism (2 marks):**
For a query $f$ with $\ell_2$ sensitivity $\Delta_2 f$, the Gaussian mechanism is:
$$\mathcal{M}(D) \;=\; f(D) + \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sigma^2 I\right), \quad \sigma \;\ge\; \frac{\Delta_2 f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25 / \delta)}}{\epsilon}$$

**Trade-off (2 marks):**
- Smaller $\epsilon$ ⇒ stronger privacy ⇒ larger noise ⇒ lower model accuracy.
- Larger $\epsilon$ ⇒ weaker privacy ⇒ smaller noise ⇒ higher utility.
- In deep learning, DP-SGD clips per-sample gradients and adds Gaussian noise, typically reducing accuracy by 1–5% for $\epsilon \approx 1$ on CIFAR-10.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in FGSM** — Untargeted FGSM uses *+ sign*; targeted FGSM uses *− sign*. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 2. **Mis-defining $\epsilon$** — Students often write $\epsilon$ as a *probability*; it is a *perturbation magnitude*. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the projection step in PGD** — PGD is *not* just FGSM repeated; the projection $\Pi_{B_{\epsilon}(x)}$ is essential. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 4. **Conflating differential privacy with encryption** — DP adds statistical noise, it does not encrypt. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 5. **One-sided future-trend answers** — Always mention BOTH the offensive and the defensive impact. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 6. **No math in adversarial training** — Examiners expect the explicit min-max form; verbal-only answers lose 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

- **Adversarial example:** $x_{\text{adv}} = x + \delta$ with $\lVert \delta \rVert_p \le \epsilon$.
- **FGSM (one-step):** $x_{\text{adv}} = x + \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})$.
- **PGD (iterative):** same update with projection onto $B_{\epsilon}(x)$.
- **C&W (optimization-based):** minimizes $\lVert \delta \rVert_2^2 + c \cdot f(x+\delta)$.
- **Adversarial training:** min-max objective; outer min over $\theta$, inner max over $\delta$.
- **Defensive distillation:** smooth decision surface using temperature-$T$ soft labels.
- **Randomized smoothing:** certified $\ell_2$ radius $R = \frac{\sigma}{2}(\Phi^{-1}(p_A) - \Phi^{-1}(p_B))$.
- **Differential privacy (Gaussian):** $\sigma \ge \frac{\Delta_2 f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25 / \delta)}}{\epsilon}$.
- **Federated learning:** data stays local; only model updates are shared.
- **Model validation pipeline:** static, dynamic, behavioural, continuous.
- **Emerging trends:** LLMs, Quantum, Federated, XAI, GNNs, Blockchain, IoT/Edge AI.
- **Dual-use dialectic:** every new tech has BOTH offensive and defensive impact — always state both.
- **Data poisoning rate:** even $\tau \le 1\%$ can plant a backdoor.
- **Robust accuracy vs clean accuracy:** the gap is the *robustness gap*; minimise it.
- **CIA triad for AI:** Confidentiality (model extraction), Integrity (evasion), Availability (poisoning).
